  Root cause confirmed
     
  The npm-publish regression was an environment issue, not a code issue. Stale node_modules on the mini (last pnpm 
  install was May 13, lockfile updated May 17) was missing @types/node. That broke typeof import('fs')/import('path') in

  graph, tsf's parallel builder deadlocked waiting on it, which presented as 9 packages "hanging." The pnpm install
  added @types/node 20.19.27 and the deadlock is gone.

⏺ While the build runs, here's my take on Browserbase for Sharpee.
 
  Where it actually fits

  Browserbase is cloud-hosted, Playwright/Puppeteer-drivable Chromium (you connect over CDP, plus session recording and
  a live-view debugger). The key question is which Sharpee testing surface you'd point it at, because they have very
  different needs:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Surface              │     Needs a browser?     │                 Browserbase fit                  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Engine / game logic (transcript   │ No — runs on node        │ No. Keep these. They're ~170ms and               │
  │ tests)                            │ dist/cli/sharpee.js      │ deterministic. Don't move game-logic coverage    │
  │                                   │                          │ into a browser.                                  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Single-player web client          │                          │ Marginal. Local Playwright already does this;    │
  │ (dist/web/{story},                │ Yes                      │ Browserbase mainly buys you cross-browser + no   │
  │ platform-browser)                 │                          │ local binary management in CI.                   │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zifmia multi-user (already has    │                          │                                                  │
  │ Playwright E2E, Phase 8           │ Yes, and concurrently    │ This is the real fit.                            │
  │ AC-03–AC-13)                      │                          │                                                  │
  └───────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The genuine win is multi-user

  Zifmia's whole point is N users in a shared room over websockets. The hard thing to test is concurrent sessions — two
  browsers in the same room, each with its own per-user Renderer over the shared packet stream (the ADR-163 channel
  model). Browserbase gives you N independent cloud browser contexts trivially, so "user A types take lamp, does user
  B's pane update?" becomes a clean multi-session test instead of juggling local browser contexts. That's the scenario
  where it earns its keep.

  Adoption cost is low, but it's a real dependency

  Your existing zifmia Playwright scripts mostly port by swapping the launch for a CDP connect to Browserbase — you
  don't rewrite tests. The costs to weigh:
  - Paid per browser-minute — fine for a nightly/PR multi-user suite, wasteful if pointed at fast single-player checks.
  - Network latency + cloud dependency in the test path — flakier than local, and an API-key/secret to manage in CI.
  - Their AI-driver (Stagehand) is tempting for "play the game in English," but it's nondeterministic — wrong tool for
  IF, where transcript tests already give you exact expected output.

  My recommendation

  Keep the testing pyramid as-is: transcript tests carry game logic, local Playwright carries single-player UI smoke.
  Add Browserbase only for the zifmia concurrent-multi-user layer, where local orchestration is the actual pain. That's
  a targeted addition, not a testing-strategy change.

  One caveat per my own rule: I'd verify Browserbase's current pricing and that it's actively maintained before we
  commit to it — I can do that lookup when you want to move on it.

  What surface did you have in mind — were you thinking multi-user zifmia, or driving the single-player web client? I'll
  check the build status now.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

  earlier tsf build --npm only wrote to ~/.tsf-publish, never to local dist/, so local dist/ is stale/empty.

⏺ Diagnosis confirmed exactly:

  - packages/core/dist/ does not exist — no index.d.ts.
  - But packages/core/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo + tsconfig.esm.tsbuildinfo exist, dated May 17 22:34 (stale).
  - 43 .tsbuildinfo files across packages, all from that era.

  So tsc reads the stale tsbuildinfo, concludes core is up-to-date, emits nothing, and dist/ stays absent → if-domain
  can't resolve @sharpee/core. This is the precise failure mode last session documented as a pre-existing bug in the
  pnpm clean path — unrelated to channel-io or the tsf work. tsf build --npm sidesteps it (it stages fresh to
  ~/.tsf-publish), which is why that build passed and this one didn't.

  I'm stopping here per the "never auto-retry/fix-rebuild" directive, and because clearing these means deleting files.
  Two ways forward:

  1. Targeted: delete the 43 stale .tsbuildinfo files, then re-run ./build.sh -s dungeo. Clean, gets us to baseline, no
  source touched. (.tsbuildinfo is a regenerable build cache, not source — safe to delete, but I won't without your
  say-so.)
  2. Same, then fix the root cause: also extend the clean scripts (or set tsBuildInfoFile into dist/) so pnpm clean
  removes them and this stops recurring — that's a packages/ change, so it needs discussion first regardless.

  The walkthrough regression is blocked until the build succeeds, so I can't proceed without clearing this.

  Want me to do (1) — delete the stale .tsbuildinfo files and rebuild — and we treat the root-cause fix in (2) as a
  separate discussion?

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee

  ⎿  === packages/extensions/testing ===
     build: tsc
     build:esm: undefined

⏺ Now the real picture emerges, and it's subtler than I framed it. These packages' build is tsc only (CJS → dist/), yet
  their exports point import at dist-esm/. The dist-esm/ directories are produced by build.sh/tsf, not by pnpm build —
  that's the post-tsf convention (tsf recompiles ESM, so per-package esm compile was dropped as redundant). But the
  clean scripts still only remove dist, so those tsf-produced dist-esm/ dirs go stale — which is exactly what bit us
  today.

  Let me audit by the correct signal — packages whose exports reference dist-esm but whose clean doesn't remove it:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      printf "%-30s %-12s %s\n" "PACKAGE" "EXPORTS-ESM" "CLEAN"…)
  ⎿  PACKAGE                        EXPORTS-ESM  CLEAN
     @sharpee/bridge                YES          rimraf dist tsconfig.tsbuildinfo
     @sharpee/interpreter           YES          rm -rf dist tsconfig.tsbuildinfo
     … +34 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The audit reveals the scope is bigger than the two extensions I flagged. Every package whose clean only removes dist
  (not dist-esm) but for which the build produces a dist-esm/ is affected — because tsf emits dist-esm/ for essentially
  all packages. The 18 dual-output packages already clean it; these don't:

  bridge, character, helpers, queries, runtime, platform-browser, ext-testing, ext-basic-combat (8 active) + interpreter
  (parked). extension-conversation is correctly CJS-only (no dist-esm), so it's excluded.

  So "item 2" is really: make clean remove dist-esm wherever the build emits one. I'll derive the set programmatically
  (dist-esm dir exists on disk AND clean doesn't already remove it), insert dist-esm into those clean scripts, then
  validate. Same exact-replacement approach as before.

       / `typeof import('path')` references in `packages/extensions/testing/src/checkpoints/store.ts` with TS2307. Be
      cause `ext-testing` sits upstream in the dependency graph, tsf's parallel builder deadlocked waiting on it — pr
      esenting as 9 packages hanging (ext-testing, parser-en-us, stdlib, engine, ext-basic-combat, plugin-npc, platfo
      rm-browser, transcript-tester, sharpee).
   20
   21 Fix: `pnpm install` (the documented prerequisite that had simply never been run). This added `@davidcornelson/t
      sf 1.0.0`, `@types/node 20.19.27`, and refreshed stryker/vitest. `pnpm-lock.yaml` and `package.json` unchanged
      — pure node_modules sync, **no repo diff**.
   22
   23 Result: `tsf build --npm` completed 25/25 packages, all 9 previously-hanging packages built, empty stderr, stag
      ed to `~/.tsf-publish`.
   24
   25 ### Diagnosed and fixed stale tsbuildinfo no-op build failures (durable, committed)
   26
   27 The prior session documented the tsbuildinfo silent-no-op bug. This session applied the durable fix.
   28
   29 Confirmed the failure mode: `pnpm clean` removes `dist/` and `dist-esm/` but left 43 `.tsbuildinfo` files. On r
      ebuild, `tsc` sees stale tsbuildinfo, decides inputs are current, emits nothing with exit 0. `build.sh` prints
      `[core] ✓` falsely. Then downstream packages fail TS2307 "Cannot find module '@sharpee/core'" because `dist/` w
      as never recreated.
   30
   31 First delete pass used `find packages -maxdepth 2` which missed `packages/extensions/*` (depth 3). Caught when
      rebuild then failed on `@sharpee/ext-testing` dist-esm resolution. Corrected to delete all tsbuildinfo at any d
      epth; rebuild was then clean.
   32
   33 **Commit 812ba541** — extended `clean` script in all 29 packages that had one to also remove `tsconfig.tsbuildi
      nfo` (and `tsconfig.esm.tsbuildinfo` where an ESM build exists). Verified: `pnpm clean` now clears 43 → 0 tsbui
      ldinfo; full clean+build cycle green.
   34
   35 **Commit 6aa78974** — follow-up: 9 packages (bridge, character, helpers, queries, runtime, platform-browser, in
      terpreter, ext-basic-combat, ext-testing) emit a `dist-esm/` tree via tsf but their clean scripts only removed
      `dist/`. Added `dist-esm` to those 9 clean scripts. `extension-conversation` correctly left dist-only (no ESM t
      ree). `interpreter` is workspace-excluded via turbo (`!packages/interpreter`) so turbo skips it; its script is
      corrected regardless. Verified: `pnpm clean` now removes 27/28 dist-esm trees.
   36
   37 This closes the prior session's "Long Term" open item: "Move .tsbuildinfo files into dist/ ... or extend clean
      scripts" — chose extend-clean-scripts.

⏺ Here's what's actually there — and an important catch.

  npm-test/ — the npm regression test (git-tracked, 57 files). It's a standalone mini-story named sharpee-npm-regression
  whose src/ and 10 transcripts (01-navigation … 10-npc: scenery, portables, containers, light-dark, readable,
  switchable, wearable, supporter, npc) mirror the familyzoo tutorial features — that's the family-zoo association.
  run.sh copies it to a temp dir, npm installs @sharpee/*, compiles, and runs the transcripts. It proves the packages
  work for an external consumer.

  But it tests the registry, not your local build. Its deps are ^0.9.95, and run.sh does a plain npm install — so it
  pulls the last published packages off npm, not what tsf build --npm just staged in ~/.tsf-publish. So today it would
  validate published 0.9.x, not the local build we just fixed.

  The actual local-build packager, pack-release.sh, is stale/broken. It still lists packages/text-service in its
  PACKAGES array — deleted in ADR-174 Phase 3 — and hardcodes VERSION="0.9.60-beta". It would fail if run today.

  So the honest picture: you have a familyzoo-flavored npm consumer test, but it points at the registry, and the

  But it tests the registry, not your local build. Its deps are ^0.9.95, and run.sh does a plain npm install — so it
  pulls the last published packages off npm, not what tsf build --npm just staged in ~/.tsf-publish. So today it would
  validate published 0.9.x, not the local build we just fixed.

  The actual local-build packager, pack-release.sh, is stale/broken. It still lists packages/text-service in its
  PACKAGES array — deleted in ADR-174 Phase 3 — and hardcodes VERSION="0.9.60-beta". It would fail if run today.

  So the honest picture: you have a familyzoo-flavored npm consumer test, but it points at the registry, and the
  local-tarball path that would feed it your local build is broken post-text-service-removal.

  To make this a true "npm local build test" against what we just fixed, the options are:

  ┌────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │             Option             │                                  What it does                                  │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Point npm-test at              │ Install the locally-staged packages (file: refs/tarballs) instead of the       │
  │ ~/.tsf-publish                 │ registry — tests this build                                                    │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fix pack-release.sh            │ Remove text-service, un-hardcode the version, regenerate the tarball it        │
  │                                │ consumes                                                                       │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Just bump npm-test deps + run  │ Validates the registry packages (after you publish), not the local build       │
  │ as-is                          │                                                                                │
  └────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Which direction do you want? If it's "validate the local build before publishing," I'd wire npm-test to install from
  ~/.tsf-publish — want me to look at exactly how to do that?

⏺ Now I have the established pattern (copy scaffold → npm install → tsc → transcript-test). The new piece is sourcing
  packages from the local build (~/.tsf-publish) instead of the registry. But one thing I must verify before designing:
  familyzoo has 16 versioned transcripts (v01–v16) and 16 versioned source files — I need to know how a transcript
  that build+test, sourcing @sharpee/* from the local build.

  The design, mirroring npm-test-dungeo/ but local instead of registry:
  - Pack each staged package (~/.tsf-publish/sharpee/<pkg>) for familyzoo's transitive @sharpee closure into tarballs
  (npm pack — the exact bytes publish would upload = highest npm fidelity).
  - Generate a consumer package.json with file: tarball deps (closure + transcript-tester); third-party deps like fflate
  still come from the registry, as a real consumer's would.
  - Copy familyzoo src/transcripts, npm install, tsc, run all 16 transcripts.

  Let me write the harness (git-tracked, alongside the other npm-test* dirs):

⏺ Done and validated. The harness lives in npm-test-familyzoo/ (run.sh, gen-consumer.mjs, tsconfig.json, README.md,
  .gitignore).

  Integration Reality Statement — local-build npm consumer test
  - OWNED: the @sharpee/* packages produced by tsf build --npm (staged in ~/.tsf-publish)
  - EXTERNAL: third-party deps (fflate, etc.) and the tsc/npm toolchain — left to resolve from the registry, as a real
  consumer's would
  - REAL-PATH TEST: ./npm-test-familyzoo/run.sh — packs the staged packages into real tarballs, npm installs them via
  file: refs, compiles familyzoo with tsc, runs all 16 transcripts. No stub of the npm packages — the actual publish
  bytes are exercised.
  - RESULT: 15/16 transcripts pass; the one failure (v16-scoring) reproduces identically in the workspace build — a
  pre-existing story-content assertion (walkthrough scores 75/100, transcript asserts "perfect score"), not a packaging
  defect.

  What this buys you: a pre-publish gate. npm-test-dungeo tests the registry (after publish); this tests what you just
  built (before publish). A transcript that passes in the workspace but fails here would flag a real packaging defect —
  missing files, bad exports, wrong main.

  Two notes:
  - It currently uses the staging from this session's earlier tsf build --npm. Run with --build to rebuild staging first
  for a fresh check.
  - Pre-existing bug surfaced (not mine to fix unasked): familyzoo's v16-scoring walkthrough only reaches 75/100 but
  asserts "perfect score" — either the walkthrough is incomplete or the assertion is aspirational. Worth a familyzoo
  follow-up.

  I'm on main and haven't committed. Want me to branch and commit the harness (and open a PR), or leave it uncommitted
  for now?

❯ Research family zoo v16 issue                                                                                         

⏺ Story-level research (autonomous per CLAUDE.md). Let me dig into the v16 scoring logic and the walkthrough.

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== full v16-scoring transcript ==="; cat tutorials/familyzoo/tests/transcripts/v16-scoring.transcript…)
  ⎿  === full v16-scoring transcript ===
     title: V16 — Scoring and Endgame
     story: familyzoo
     … +117 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The transcript's own comments tally to 75 ("this should trigger victory (75 pts total)"), but the output says "75 out
  of 100 ... rank of Expert" and the assertion wants "perfect score". So the question is whether max should be 75,
  whether 25 points are uncollected, or whether "perfect score" is the wrong assertion. Let me examine v16's scoring
  logic:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== scoring-related lines in v16.ts ==="; grep -nE "awardScore|maxScore|max_score|MAX|perfect
      score|Expert|rank|victory|Vict…)
  ⎿  === scoring-related lines in v16.ts ===
     5: *   - world.awardScore() — award points for achievements (idempotent)
     6: *   - world.setMaxScore() — set the maximum possible score
     … +39 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Found the crux. v16 sets MAX_SCORE = 75 and calls world.setMaxScore(75); the victory daemon fires at getScore() >= 75,
  and the walkthrough correctly reaches 75. But the score output says "75 out of 100 … rank of Expert" — so the score
  reporting is using max=100, not the story's 75. That means setMaxScore(75) isn't reaching the score formatter. This
  looks like a platform issue, not story content. Let me trace where that output and the "perfect score" message come
  from:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== where do 'perfect score' / 'rank of' / 'out of' / 'Expert' strings live? ==="; grep -rnE "perfect
      score|rank of|out of|E…)
  ⎿  === where do 'perfect score' / 'rank of' / 'out of' / 'Expert' strings live? ===
     packages/engine/src/vocabulary-manager.ts:52:   * Marks all entities as out of scope first, then marks
     packages/engine/src/vocabulary-manager.ts:64:    // Mark all entities as out of scope first
     … +60 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Two parallel scoring stores exist: world.setMaxScore() → ScoreLedger (world-model), and a separate SCORING capability
  (stdlib) with its own maxScore. The score action likely reads the capability (default max 100), while v16 sets the
  ledger (75). Let me confirm by reading the score action and the capability:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== scoring action dir ==="; ls packages/stdlib/src/actions/standard/scoring/ 2>&1…)
  ⎿  === scoring action dir ===
     index.ts
     scoring-events.ts
     … +43 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The score action uses world.getScore() (=75 ✓) and world.getMaxScore(), and fires perfect_score when score === 
  maxScore. So the whole bug is: getMaxScore() returns 100, not the 75 that v16 set. Let me find getMaxScore and the
  capability default:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== WorldModel.getMaxScore + setMaxScore bodies ==="; sed -n '885,915p'
      packages/world-model/src/world/WorldModel.ts…)
  ⎿  === WorldModel.getMaxScore + setMaxScore bodies ===

       hasScore(id: string): boolean {

⏺ Root cause confirmed and airtight. Here's the research result.

  What's actually happening

  v16-scoring is not a packaging bug, not a scoring-platform bug, and not a broken walkthrough. It's a version-targeting
  bug: the transcript runs against the wrong story version.

  The chain of evidence:
  
  1. v16.ts sets MAX_SCORE = 75; its victory daemon fires at getScore() >= 75; the walkthrough collects exactly 75.
  Internally consistent — for v16.
  2. But familyzoo/src/index.ts re-exports ./v17, and v17/ is the newer multi-file version where v17/scoring.ts: 
  MAX_SCORE = 100 (the "After Hours" expansion added 25 points). v17/index.ts calls setMaxScore(100).
  3. The transcript's entry: v16 header is dead. transcript-tester's loadStory() hardcodes loading <path>/dist/index.js
  and takes story || default — it never receives or consults the entry: header. A repo-wide search found no consumer of 
  entry: anywhere (not the tester, not the bundle CLI).
  4. So every familyzoo transcript runs against v17. The v16-era walkthrough scores 75 of v17's 100 → score_with_rank →
  "75 out of 100, rank of Expert"; the 100-point victory never triggers; [OK: contains "perfect score"] fails.
  Correctly.

  The bigger finding

  This isn't isolated to v16. All 16 versioned familyzoo transcripts (v01–v16) run against v17, not their named 
  versions. v01–v15 pass only because v17 is a cumulative superset — their assertions still hold. v16-scoring is the one
  that exposes the gap, because v17 changed a value (the perfect-score threshold) rather than just adding content. The
  per-version test fidelity is currently a fiction.

  Fix options (no changes made — your call)

  ┌─────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │                     Fix                     │          Scope           │             Effect              │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Implement entry: in transcript-tester —     │ Platform                 │ Restores real per-version       │
  │ 1   │ when present, load dist/<entry>.js (or      │ (transcript-tester) —    │ testing; v16-scoring → v16 →    │
  │     │ dist/<entry>/index.js) instead of           │ needs discussion         │ passes. Fixes the whole suite's │
  │     │ dist/index.js                               │                          │  fidelity. The honest fix.      │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Retarget v16-scoring to v17 — rewrite the   │                          │ Quick green, but conflates the  │
  │ 2   │ walkthrough to collect all 100 and assert   │ Story/test only          │ v16 tutorial stage with v17,    │
  │     │ perfect score                               │                          │ and leaves v01–v15 still        │
  │     │                                             │                          │ silently running against v17    │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3   │ Drop the dead entry: fields, relabel        │ Test only                │ Honest about what runs, but     │
  │     │ transcripts as "current story"              │                          │ abandons per-version coverage   │
  └─────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
  
  My recommendation: #1 — the entry: header is clearly intended design, and its absence is the actual defect;
  implementing it is what makes the versioned tutorial transcripts mean what they claim. It's a transcript-tester
  (platform) change, so it needs your go-ahead before I touch it.

  Want me to write up #1 as a proper plan/ADR for the transcript-tester change, or just patch v16-scoring (#2) for now?

⏺ Plan written to docs/work/transcript-entry-support/plan-20260617-entry-header-support.md. Summary:

  The change — make both transcript runners honor entry:. A shared resolveStoryModulePath(storyPath, entry?) resolves
  dist/<entry>.js (file form, e.g. v16) → dist/<entry>/index.js (dir form, e.g. v17) → src fallbacks, with traversal
  rejection; default (no entry) stays dist/index.js. Thread transcript.header.entry into the per-transcript reload in
  both cli.ts and fast-cli.ts.

  Why two loaders — cli.ts/loadStory (slow path) and fast-cli.ts/loadStoryAndCreateGame (the bundle's fast path) both
  hardcode dist/index.js; both need it, via one shared resolver so they can't drift.

  Scope — 5 files in @sharpee/transcript-tester + tests. No familyzoo changes — once entry: is honored, v16-scoring
  loads the version it was written for and passes; v01–v15 keep passing (no entry → v17 superset). Additive and
  default-preserving, so dungeo etc. are untouched.

  Acceptance — unit tests for the resolver (both forms + traversal rejection), integration: v16-scoring passes via both
  runners, v01–v15 + dungeo unchanged, and npm-test-familyzoo reports 16/16 after rebuilding staging.

  Three decisions I flagged (with my proposed defaults):
  1. Chain + differing entry: → warn, hard-error only under --stop-on-failure.
  2. --entry flag for play/restore mode → out of scope, add later if wanted.
  3. ADR-180 → file a short ADR (it sets a test-authoring contract) alongside the code, vs. code-first.

  This is a platform change, so I'm not implementing yet. Do you want to proceed with implementation (and if so, your
  calls on 1–3), adjust the plan, or have me run it past adr-review first?

  its own inline loadStoryAndCreateGame (line 204, hardcodes dist/index.js). That is the `node dist/cli/sharpee.js
  --test` path — the one CLAUDE.md tells everyone to use. Without it, the bundle silently ignores `entry:`.
    Update contracts specified:    FAIL — it's THREE duplicated loaders, not two (cli.ts/loadStory, fast-cli.ts,

  consumed by all three atomically — bundle-entry.js already `require`s the package, so it can call the export instead
  of hand-copying a 3rd time.

❯ This is all kind of smelly and I’m wondering if we need to step back and determine a productization build and test    
  implementation with hard app like we did with DevArch.                                                                

⏺ You're right, and I think the smell is structural, not cosmetic. Everything I've done this session has been
  whack-a-mole on the same underlying thing.

  The actual mess (evidence from this session)

  - Three hand-copied story loaders: cli.ts/loadStory, fast-cli.ts/loadStoryAndCreateGame, and scripts/bundle-entry.js's
  inline copy. The entry: bug isn't a missing feature — it's a symptom of "the runner is three divergent copies." Any
  fix has to be applied three times and will drift again.
  - build.sh is a ~1,100-line untested bash monolith doing versioning, package build, genai-api gen, bundling, story
  build, browser/runner/zifmia/shite clients — and it's where the silent .tsbuildinfo no-op lived. Nobody tests the
  thing that builds everything.
  - Dead/stale scripts with no owner: publish:beta (just fixed), npm-latest.sh (just deleted), pack-release.sh (still
  references the deleted text-service, hardcodes 0.9.60-beta).
  - Three near-duplicate npm-test harnesses: npm-test/, npm-test-dungeo/, npm-test-familyzoo/ — each its own run.sh +
  package.json, same shape copied.
  - Authored conventions with no enforcement: entry: headers written into transcripts that nothing reads.

  The diagnosis

  The build half got productized — tsf (@davidcornelson/tsf) is a real, published, owned tool. The bundle + story-build 
  + transcript-run + npm-verify + publish-verify half never did. It's accreted bash and copy-paste with no single owner,
  no config, and no tests of its own. That's exactly the gap.

  What "hard app like DevArch" would mean here

  DevArch works because it's a product: one entry point, declarative config, its own validation harness (run.sh,
  rule-firing harness), versioned and installable, lifecycle-managed. The Sharpee build/test layer deserves the same
  shape:

  - One story loader/runner — entry-aware — that the CLI, the fast path, and the bundle all call (kills the 3-copy class
  of bug outright).
  - One transcript-test surface; the npm consumer-test generalized over any story (collapses the three npm-test-* dirs
  into one parameterized command).
  - Bundle/build orchestration that delegates compile to tsf instead of re-implementing it in bash.
  - Config-driven, self-tested, versioned — a real package, not scripts.

  The neat part: phase 1 of that product is the same work as fixing entry: correctly — extract the single shared loader
  and point all front-ends at it. The fix and the foundation converge.

  This is squarely your call on scope and timing — you've built tsf and DevArch, so you know what productizing a tool
  costs and buys. Two forks frame it:

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · How do you want to proceed on the build/test tooling? → Spec it first
     · What's the intended scope of this tool? → Sharpee-internal devtool

⏺ Good — that's the right call. Spec first, internal devtool, don't build yet. I'll write the design spec (the pre-ADR
  convergence doc), make concrete proposals with rationale, and surface the real decisions for you rather than deciding
  them unilaterally.

       3 **Date**: 2026-06-17
       4 **Status**: DRAFT SPEC — convergence doc, pre-ADR. Decisions in §7 must be
       5 resolved before this becomes an ADR and any code is written.
       6 **Scope decision (set)**: a **Sharpee-internal, tested devtool package** — not a
       7 published product. Owns build/test/verify orchestration.
       8 **Origin**: session 90c6c2. A string of build/test fixes (tsbuildinfo no-op,
       9 clean drift, dead `publish:beta`/`npm-latest.sh`, the unimplemented `entry:`
      10 header) revealed the tooling is accreted bash + copy-paste, not a product.
     … +124 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Spec written to docs/work/sharpee-devkit/spec-20260617-build-test-devkit.md. The shape:

  - devkit owns orchestration + test (one entry-aware story loader, transcript run, parameterized npm consumer-test,
  bundle, build orchestration, publish-verify); tsf stays the compiler/publisher and devkit delegates to it. Clean
  split: tsf compiles and publishes; devkit orchestrates, bundles, and tests.
  - Phased, each shippable: Phase 1 = extract the single shared loader (this is the correct entry: fix and kills the
  3-copy bug) → Phase 2 = collapse the three npm-test-* harnesses → Phase 3 = absorb build.sh, retire dead scripts →
  Phase 4 = client builds (optional).
  - Self-tested with a validation harness, DevArch-style — the tool that tests Sharpee is itself tested.

  I deliberately did not decide §7. My recommendations, for you to confirm or redirect:

  ┌─────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │     Decision     │                                   My recommendation                                    │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 1   │ Location/name    │ tools/devkit, @sharpee/devkit — it's tooling, mirrors tools/zifmia; keeps it out of    │
  │     │                  │ the runtime packages/ graph                                                            │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 2   │ build.sh         │ Wrap during migration, retire at end of Phase 3 — no big-bang                          │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3   │ Phase-1 loader   │ Land it in transcript-tester now, move into devkit at Phase 3 — decouples the urgent   │
  │     │ home             │ entry: fix from standing up a new package                                              │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 4   │ Config           │ Start minimal; reuse ts-forge.config.json, add a devkit section only when justified —  │
  │     │                  │ no new config file yet                                                                 │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 5   │ Client builds    │ Defer to Phase 4; core build+test first                                                │
  ├─────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 6   │ The bundle       │ Keep dist/cli/sharpee.js as a devkit-produced artifact — it's the ~170ms fast path,    │
  │     │                  │ worth preserving                                                                       │
  └─────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The one with real tension is #3: landing the shared loader in transcript-tester now would ship the entry: fix
  immediately as a pragmatic interim — but you chose "spec first," so I'm holding it inside the phased plan rather than
  sneaking it in early. Say the word if you'd rather pull Phase 1 forward.

  Once you've weighed in on §7, I'll promote this to ADR-180 (the decision record), and we sequence the phases with your
  go-ahead per phase. Want to settle §7 now, or sit with the spec first?

❯ I only see 6 items                                                                                                    

⏺ Two of my lists happen to have 6 items, so let me make sure I'm answering the right one:
  
  - The CLI surface (§4) — 6 commands: build, bundle, test, test:npm, verify, clean. That's a deliberate starter set; a
  productized tool would likely also need watch (dev loop), version/stamp (or explicitly defer to tsf), and play (the
  REPL). I trimmed to the core.
  - The open decisions (§7) — 6 items. That set is only the decisions I flagged, not the full design surface. For
  something this structural it's genuinely light — I compressed out several real ones:
    - Story discovery — how devkit knows what stories exist and how each builds (convention vs per-story config).
    - Version ownership — build.sh stamps versions first today; does devkit own that or delegate to tsf version?
    - Fixture story — the self-validation harness needs a fixture; new minimal one vs reuse dungeo/familyzoo.
    - Artifact assertion — should devkit prove outputs exist post-build (kill the silent-✓ class we hit with
  tsbuildinfo)? I'd argue that's a hard requirement, not a decision.
    - Migration conformance — during cutover, must devkit and build.sh produce byte-identical artifacts as a gate?
    - Docs — build.sh is heavily documented in CLAUDE.md; migration means rewriting that.

  Which 6 did you mean — the CLI commands or the decisions? Either way I think you're right that it reads thin; tell me
  which, and I'll expand that section of the spec properly (and I'll keep CI out of it, per your call).

✻ Baked for 15s

❯ Let’s go through the open questions one at a time.                                                                    

⏺ Good — let's take them in order.
  
  Decision 1 of 6: Package location and name

  The choice: where devkit lives in the repo and what it's called.

  Location — tools/devkit vs packages/devkit:
  - packages/ is the publishable runtime graph — every @sharpee/* library, and it's what tsf/ts-forge.config.json
  enumerate for npm publish. Putting a non-published devtool there risks it getting swept into the publish set, and
  mixes tooling into the runtime dependency graph.
  - tools/ already holds zifmia and shite — apps/tooling, workspace members but not published libraries. devkit is
  exactly that category.

  Name / bin:
  - @sharpee/devkit reads well and signals "dev tooling" (it's broader than "build" — it also tests and verifies).
  - Bin name needs to avoid the existing sharpee bin and node dist/cli/sharpee.js. devkit as the bin is clean.

  My recommendation: tools/devkit, package @sharpee/devkit, CLI bin devkit.
  Why: it's tooling not a runtime library; tools/ keeps it out of the publish graph (no accidental npm publish, no
  contamination of the package dependency tree); mirrors the tools/zifmia convention; still a normal pnpm workspace
  member so it's buildable/runnable like the others.

  The main thing to get right is not landing it in packages/ — that's the one with downstream consequences (publish +
  dep-graph). Name and bin are easy to change later.

  Do you agree with tools/devkit / @sharpee/devkit / bin devkit, or do you want it elsewhere or named differently?

⏺ Decision 2 of 6: What happens to build.sh

  The choice: does devkit replace build.sh in one shot, or hollow it out gradually?

  Full replacement (big-bang): rewrite all of build.sh's ~1,100 lines (versioning, package build, genai-api gen,
  bundling, story build, 4+ client builds) into devkit, then delete it. High risk, nothing ships until it's all done,
  and it's easy to silently drop a behavior — exactly the opposite of the phased plan we agreed on.

  Wrap-then-retire (incremental): build.sh stays untouched through Phases 1–2 (devkit owns the loader + transcript run +
  npm-verify, which build.sh doesn't really do anyway). In Phase 3, devkit absorbs build/bundle; at that point build.sh
  becomes a thin shim (./build.sh → devkit build) for muscle memory, then gets deleted once docs move. A conformance 
  gate — devkit's output must match build.sh's byte-for-byte — guards the Phase-3 cutover.

  My recommendation: wrap-then-retire. It matches the phased plan, never leaves the repo unbuildable, and the
  conformance gate means we can prove devkit reproduces build.sh before we trust it. The front door flips from
  ./build.sh to devkit only in Phase 3, and build.sh dies shortly after — no permanent dual-entry (consistent with your
  no-back-compat stance).

  The real commitment here is the conformance gate in Phase 3 — that's what keeps the migration honest.

  Agree with wrap-then-retire (build.sh untouched until Phase 3, then shimmed and deleted, gated by an
  output-conformance check)?

✻ Brewed for 34s
  
❯ Full replacement                                                                                                      

⏺ Understood — full replacement. devkit fully owns the build; build.sh gets deleted at cutover, no shim, no delegation.
  Your call, and it's the cleaner end state.

  One safeguard I'd keep regardless (it's verification, not wrapping): a one-time parity check — diff devkit's outputs
  against build.sh's before deleting build.sh — so full replacement can't silently drop a behavior. That's exactly the
  failure class this whole productization is reacting to.

  And one implication worth flagging now, because it pre-constrains a later question: full replacement means devkit must
  reach parity on everything build.sh does — package build (via tsf), genai-api generation, bundling, version stamping,
  and the client builds (browser/runner/zifmia/shite). So Decision 5 (client builds) is effectively forced toward
  "devkit owns them" unless we deliberately carve some out. I'll raise it properly when we get there.

  Recording it.

⏺ Decision 3 of 6: Where the shared story-loader lives

  Originally I framed this as "transcript-tester now (interim) vs devkit (proper home)." But working through the
  dependency direction changes the answer — there's an architectural constraint, not just a timing preference.

  The constraint: devkit orchestrates transcript-tester (devkit test runs transcripts via the tester). So devkit depends
  on transcript-tester. If the shared loader lived in devkit and transcript-tester imported it, you'd get
  transcript-tester → devkit → transcript-tester — a dependency cycle.

  So the loader must sit at or below transcript-tester. Its actual job — load a story module, build a testable game — is
  a transcript-tester concern anyway. bundle-entry.js already requires transcript-tester/dist/index.js, so it can
  consume the loader from there too.

  My recommendation: the single entry-aware loader lives in transcript-tester, exported from its index, consumed by
  cli.ts, fast-cli.ts, and bundle-entry.js. devkit calls transcript-tester's runner from above. This isn't a "pragmatic
  interim" — it's the correct home given the dependency direction, and it stays put.

  A side effect worth naming: this means the entry: fix lands here naturally, as the act of consolidating the three
  loaders into one. It's the first concrete build step, not a separate patch — consistent with "spec first," just that
  the spec's Phase 1 happens to also fix entry:.

  Agree the loader belongs in transcript-tester (with devkit orchestrating it from above)?

✻ Baked for 30s

❯ Shouldn’t loader be its own thing?                                                                                    

⏺ Yes — you're right, and it's the better call. I was settling for "in transcript-tester" because it dodged the cycle,
  but that conflates two concerns: loading/assembling a story into a runnable game is more fundamental than the 
  transcript test framework. Interactive --play needs it too, and it shouldn't have to pull in the test framework just
  to boot a story.

  Decision 3 (revised): the loader is its own package

  What it is: the one module that resolves a story module (entry-aware: dist/<entry>.js → dist/<entry>/index.js →
  dist/index.js) and assembles GameEngine + world + player + parser + language + perception → a ready-to-run game.
  That's exactly the logic triplicated across cli.ts, fast-cli.ts, and bundle-entry.js today.

  Layering (no cycle):
  engine + world-model + parser + lang  (runtime libs)
          ↑
  @sharpee/story-host   ← the loader, depends only downward
          ↑
     ┌────┼─────────────┐
  transcript-tester   bundle (--play/--test)   devkit
  Everyone depends down into the loader; the loader depends on nobody above it. Clean.

  One real consequence to flag: transcript-tester is a published package, so anything it depends on must also be
  published. That makes the loader a new published @sharpee/* package — which means the 6-point new-package registration
  (ts-forge.config.json, sharpee package.json/index.ts/tsconfig, build.sh, root package.json) and it joins the publish
  set. That's the cost of doing it right; given no-back-compat, growing the publish set is fine.

  So this lives in packages/story-host (published runtime lib), not tools/ — different from devkit, because the
  runtime/bundle and the published tester depend on it.

  Questions:
  1. Agree it's its own published package in packages/?
  2. Name — I proposed @sharpee/story-host; alternatives: @sharpee/game-loader, @sharpee/story-loader,
  @sharpee/bootstrap. Preference?

⏺ Four down (1, 2, 3, 5). Two left.

  Decision 4 of 6: Configuration

  The choice: how devkit is configured — a new config file, extending ts-forge.config.json, or convention.

  Don't extend ts-forge.config.json: that's tsf's file, tsf owns its schema, and tsf may reject unknown keys. devkit
  invokes tsf; mixing devkit's orchestration config into tsf's compile config couples them and muddies ownership. Keep
  tsf's config tsf's.

  My recommendation: convention-first, devkit-owned config only when justified.
  - Story discovery by convention — stories/* and tutorials/* with a package.json, exactly as build.sh resolves them
  today. No config needed to list them.
  - Per-story hints (entry points, which targets) read from that story's own package.json (a sharpee/devkit key) —
  config lives next to the thing it configures, not in a central registry.
  - A central devkit.config.json only appears when there's genuine project-wide config that can't be conventional (e.g.
  if the bundle's platform package-set ever needs to be author-tunable — today it's effectively fixed and can stay
  internal).

  Why: build.sh works off convention + flags today and that part isn't the problem; the problem was duplication and dead
  code, not lack of a config file. Starting convention-first avoids inventing a config schema before we know its real
  shape, and keeps devkit and tsf cleanly separated. We add a config file the moment a real need appears, not before.

  Agree — convention-first, devkit owns any config it needs, nothing bolted onto ts-forge.config.json?

✻ Cooked for 42s

❯ We should get away from conventions for story location                                                                

⏺ Agreed — and that actually flips my recommendation, which is the right correction. Convention-scanning (stories/*,
  tutorials/*) is exactly the kind of implicit magic a productized tool shouldn't rely on: it forces stories into
  blessed directories, bakes in the arbitrary stories/ vs tutorials/ split, and can't reference a story anywhere else.

  Decision 4 (revised): explicit story manifest, no directory convention

  devkit gets an explicit config that declares each story — so yes, there is a devkit config file (reversing what I
  said). Shape, roughly:

  // devkit.config.json (devkit-owned)
  {
    "stories": [
      { "name": "dungeo",    "path": "stories/dungeo",     "targets": ["bundle","browser"] },
      { "name": "familyzoo", "path": "tutorials/familyzoo", "targets": ["bundle"] }
    ]
  }

  Each entry: name, path (anywhere — not tied to stories/ or tutorials/), targets, optionally entry points. devkit
  builds/tests only what's declared — nothing is discovered by scanning. Explicit, greppable, testable.

  Two things I'd hold to:
  - Still devkit-owned, not bolted onto ts-forge.config.json — tsf's compile config stays tsf's.
  - Flag, don't decide now: ts-forge.config.json also lists story project paths (e.g. tutorials/familyzoo/tsconfig.json)
  for compilation. So we'd have two places naming story locations. Worth reconciling later — likely devkit's manifest
  becomes the source of truth and tsf's project list is derived/fed from it, so they can't drift. I'll note it as an
  open sub-question rather than settle it here.

  Agree with an explicit story manifest as devkit's config (paths unconstrained), tsf-overlap flagged for later?

※ recap: We're spec'ing a Sharpee-internal build/test devtool (@sharpee/devkit) to replace the accreted build scripts,
  going through six design decisions one at a time. Five are settled; you're on Decision 4 (config) and just need to
  confirm an explicit story manifest as devkit's config.

❯ The story is a location that debit targets and has no config or debit has an init for a story by location and may     
  have ~/.sharpee/devkit memory                                                                                         

⏺ Reading "devkit" for the voice-to-text "debit" — let me play back what I think you mean, because it changes Decision 4
  meaningfully:

  Decision 4 (revised again): a story is just a location; no committed config

  - A story = a location. A path to a buildable story directory. The story carries no devkit config, and there's no
  committed manifest in the repo. devkit just targets a location: devkit build <path>, devkit test <path> …. This is the
  canonical input — always works, fully reproducible, no machine state required (matters for fresh checkouts).
  - devkit init <location> optionally registers that location (name → path) into a user-level memory at
  ~/.sharpee/devkit — so afterward you can say devkit build dungeo instead of repeating the path, and devkit list shows
  what's registered. This memory is machine-level, not committed, and can point at stories anywhere on the box
  (including other repos) — which is the real payoff of dropping the directory convention.
  - The memory is pure convenience over the location — never a source of truth. Anything devkit can do by name, it can
  do by raw path. So nothing breaks on a machine that's never run init.

  This is the same shape as your other tooling: ~/.devarch, my ~/.claude/.../memory. devkit gets ~/.sharpee/devkit as
  its registry.

  Nice consequences:
  - No committed devkit.config.json → the tsf-overlap worry I raised mostly evaporates (no second repo-level registry to
  drift against tsf's project list).
  - The earlier "explicit manifest" idea is replaced by "explicit location, optionally remembered."

  Did I read that right — story is a location, no config, devkit init populates a user-level ~/.sharpee/devkit registry
  as a name→path convenience? If so I'll record Decision 4 that way.

✻ Brewed for 44s

❯ Correct

      135 +   reproducible input requiring no machine state. `devkit init <location>`                            
      136 +   optionally registers a name→path mapping into a **user-level memory at                             
      137 +   `~/.sharpee/devkit`** (machine-level, not committed; can point at stories                          
      138 +   anywhere, incl. other repos) so a story can later be referenced by name;                           
      139 +   `devkit list` shows registered stories. The memory is pure convenience over                        
      140 +   the location — never a source of truth, nothing requires `init`. Consequences:                     
      141 +   no committed `devkit.config.json` (so no second repo-level registry to drift                       
      142 +   against tsf's project list); mirrors `~/.devarch` tooling pattern. Nothing is                      
      143 +   bolted onto `ts-forge.config.json`.                                                                
      144  5. **Client builds / targets** — RESOLVED (2026-06-17): devkit covers only the
      145     **three live targets** — CLI platform bundle, `browser` (single-player static
      146     client), `zifmia` (ADR-177 multi-user server). **Dropped:** `shite` (abandoned

⏺ Five down. Last one.

  Decision 6 of 6: The bundle (dist/cli/sharpee.js)

  Context: the bundle is an esbuild artifact that packs the whole platform into one file so the CLI starts in ~170ms
  instead of ~5s loading individual packages. It's what --test/--play run against today, and bundle-entry.js (with its
  hand-copied loader) is its entry point.

  The question is really two:
  
  1. Keep the bundle at all? — Yes. ~170ms vs ~5s on every transcript run is a real dev-loop win; it earns its place.
  devkit's bundle command produces it, and bundle-entry.js's inline loader gets replaced by @sharpee/bootstrap.
  2. Is it user-facing, or devkit's internal engine? — This is the actual decision. Today people type node 
  dist/cli/sharpee.js --test …. In the productized world, the front door should be devkit: devkit test <location>,
  devkit play <location>. The bundle becomes devkit's internal fast engine — devkit produces it and runs against it for
  speed — not the advertised UX. The raw node dist/cli/sharpee.js can survive as a low-level entry devkit invokes (or
  for power users), but it's no longer the blessed path.

  Recommendation: keep the bundle as a devkit-produced, internally-used perf artifact; devkit is the single front door;
  its loader is @sharpee/bootstrap. So the story is a location (Decision 4), devkit loads it via bootstrap (Decision 3),
  and runs it against the fast bundled platform (Decision 6).

  Agree — keep the bundle, but as devkit's internal fast engine with devkit as the user-facing CLI?         

✻ Sautéed for 44s

※ recap: We're spec'ing a productized Sharpee build/test devtool (`@sharpee/devkit`) to replace the accreted scripts. 
  Five of six design decisions are settled; the last is whether to keep the fast bundle as devkit's internal engine. 
  Next: you confirm that, then I write ADR-180.
  
❯ Yes

⏺ All six decisions resolved. Here's the converged design:

  ├─────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 5   │ Targets       │ CLI bundle + browser + zifmia; drop shite + runner; defer .sharpee                        │
  ├─────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 6   │ Bundle        │ Keep as devkit's internal fast engine; devkit is the front door; loader = bootstrap       │
  └─────┴───────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The shape in one line: a story is a location → @sharpee/bootstrap loads it (entry-aware) → devkit runs it against the
  fast bundled platform, builds the three live targets, and fully replaces build.sh.

  A few residual integration questions surfaced during the walkthrough (not blockers — for the ADR/implementation):
  - tsf ↔ arbitrary locations: tsf's project list is fixed in ts-forge.config.json, but a story can now be "any
  location." So devkit either invokes tsf against the story's own tsconfig ad hoc, or runs the story's own build script
  (familyzoo's is just tsc). Needs nailing down.
  - bootstrap registration: the 6-point new-package setup, and it joins the publish set.
  - parity gate: how we capture build.sh's current outputs to diff against devkit's before deletion.

  Next step is to promote this to the decision record. Want me to:
  1. Write ADR-180 from the resolved spec (Context/Decision/Consequences), and
  2. rework the phase plan to match (Phase 1 = build @sharpee/bootstrap + route all three loaders through it, which also
  ships the entry: fix) —

  or sit with the converged spec first?

  (Context → options-context → Decision → Layering → Migration → Invariants → Acceptance Criteria → Tests → Constrains

⏺ Review complete. ADR-180 is well-built but a few implementation-readiness gaps remain — including one that's the same
  class as the bug this gate exists to catch.

  ADR Review: ADR-180 — Build/Test devkit and @sharpee/bootstrap
  Status: PROPOSED
  ════════════════════════════════════

  Architecture Compliance
    Language layer separation:     PASS — devtooling only; bootstrap wires the game but defines no game prose. CLI
  errors are dev-facing.
    Logic location:                PASS — bootstrap correctly sits ABOVE engine: it wires locale-specific
  parser-en-us/lang-en-us, which engine must not depend on. A separate package is justified, not arbitrary.
    Capability dispatch:           N/A — no verbs or entity behaviors.

  Contract Completeness
    All affected packages named:   NEEDS WORK — names devkit/bootstrap/transcript-tester/bundle-entry/tsf, but doesn't
  enumerate the transcript-tester edits (types.ts `entry`, parser.ts, index export) or the 6 bootstrap-registration
  files. Cross-link the entry: plan.
    Update contracts specified:    NEEDS WORK — the load-bearing one: "all THREE loaders routed through bootstrap,
  atomically, or Phase 1 is incomplete" is implied by an invariant but not stated as a contract. Updating 2 of 3 leaves
  the bundle path broken — the exact failure we already hit once.
    Interface contracts specified: FAIL — bootstrap's public API isn't specified. Three consumers depend on it; the ADR
  needs the exported signatures (e.g. `loadStory(location, { entry? }): Promise<LoadedGame>`,
  `resolveStoryModulePath(...)`) and the returned game type.
    Boundary contracts specified:  PASS (Phase 1) — bootstrap's boundaries are clear. NOTE: the devkit→tsf boundary is
  deferred to Open Q1; fine for Phase 1, must close before Phase 3.

  Test Requirements
    End-to-end test specified:     PASS — AC-2 (entry: v16 → dist/v16.js, v16-scoring passes via both runners) and AC-7
  (real build+transcript+npm harness) are concrete.
    Boundary tests specified:      PASS — entry resolution file/dir/default forms.
    Negative tests specified:      PASS — traversal rejection, nonexistent entry throws, AC-6 no-op build fails loudly.

  Acceptance Criteria
    Explicit acceptance criteria:  PASS — AC-1..AC-7, verifiable.
    Save/restore implications:     NEEDS WORK — `~/.sharpee/devkit` is new persistent (machine) state; its format and
  stale-path behavior (registered path no longer exists) are unspecified.
    Backward compatibility:        NEEDS WORK — deleting build.sh breaks the workflow CLAUDE.md documents heavily. No
  AC/step for updating CLAUDE.md + docs at cutover — that's the "docs lie" smell.

  Open Questions
    No unresolved blockers:        NEEDS WORK — Q3 (bootstrap registration) is a Phase-1 REQUIREMENT, not optional;
  reclassify as a Phase-1 task. Q1/Q2 are correctly Phase-3, non-blocking.

  ────────────────────────────────────
  Score: 7 PASS / 1 N/A / 6 NEEDS WORK or FAIL
  Verdict: NEEDS WORK

  The one that matters most: the interface contract (bootstrap's API is undefined) plus the atomic-update contract (all
  three loaders must route through bootstrap, or the bundle path silently stays broken). That pair is exactly how the
  third loader slipped through last time — high-leverage to nail before code.

  To reach READY, six tightenings — all clarifications, no design changes:
  1. Specify bootstrap's public API (signatures + returned game type).
  2. State the atomic contract: Phase 1 isn't done until cli.ts, fast-cli.ts, and bundle-entry.js all route through
  bootstrap.
  3. Enumerate the transcript-tester edits + the 6 bootstrap-registration files (or cross-link the entry: plan).
  4. Add the ~/.sharpee/devkit memory format + stale-path handling.
  5. Add an AC/step: update CLAUDE.md and build.sh-referencing docs at cutover.
  6. Move Q3 from "open question" to Phase-1 task.

  Want me to fold these six into ADR-180 so it lands at READY? None change the decisions — they sharpen contracts and
  ACs.

       87 +### `@sharpee/bootstrap` public API (contract)                                                        
       88 +                                                                                                      
       89 +Three consumers depend on this surface, so it is fixed here:                                          
       90 +                                                                                                      
       91 +```ts                                                                                                 
       92 +// Resolve the story module file to require, entry-aware. Throws on traversal                         
       93 +// (entry containing '/', '\\', '..', or absolute) and on no candidate found.                         
       94 +function resolveStoryModulePath(location: string, entry?: string): string;                            
       95 +                                                                                                      
       96 +// Load + assemble a runnable game. Resolves the module, requires it, takes                           
       97 +// `story || default`, builds GameEngine + world + player + parser + language +                       
       98 +// perception, applies story.extendParser/extendLanguage, setStory, start.                            
       99 +function loadStory(location: string, opts?: { entry?: string }): Promise<LoadedGame>;                 
      100 +                                                                                                      
      101 +interface LoadedGame {                                                                                
      102 +  engine: GameEngine;                                                                                 
      103 +  world: WorldModel;                                                                                  
      104 +  player: IFEntity;                                                                                   
      105 +  parser: Parser;                                                                                     
      106 +  language: LanguageProvider;                                                                         
      107 +  execute(input: string): Promise<TurnResult>;   // run one command                                   
      108 +  getOutput(): string;                            // captured text since last read                    
      109 +  // (superset of today's transcript-tester TestableGame; that type is replaced by this)              
      110 +}                                                                                                     
      111 +```                                                                                                   
      112 +                                                                                                      
      113 +`resolveStoryModulePath` is the single home for entry resolution; no front-end                        
      114 +reimplements it.                                                                                      
      115 +                                                                                                      
      116 +### `~/.sharpee/devkit` memory format                                                                 
      117 +                                                                                                      
      118 +A JSON file: `{ "stories": { "<name>": { "path": "<absolute path>" } } }`.                            
      119 +`devkit init <location> [--name X]` upserts an entry (default name = basename).                       
      120 +On lookup by name, devkit resolves the path and **errors if it no longer                              
      121 +exists** (stale registration is reported, never silently skipped); `devkit list`                      
      122 +flags stale entries. The file is machine-level, git-ignored, and rebuildable by                       
      123 +re-running `init` — losing it costs only the name shortcuts, never a build.                           
      124 +

※ recap: Goal: productize Sharpee's build/test tooling into a self-tested devtool. ADR-180 (devkit + @sharpee/bootstrap)
  is written, reviewed, and pushed as PR #115, still PROPOSED. Next: you read it, then tell me to accept and start

❯ Wait. I think we crossed wires. devkit should be a part of the npm install, no?                                       

⏺ Good catch — and you're right, that's a real wire-cross, and it's my fault for how I framed the scope question. I
  conflated two different things:

  - "Sharpee-specific" (it's Sharpee's build/test tool, not a generic standalone product) — true.
  - "unpublished / monorepo-only" — which I wrongly bundled into "internal," and that drove Decision 1 to tools/devkit,
  not-published.

  Those aren't the same. The "a story is a location, anywhere" decision (Decision 4) forces the issue: a story author
  writing their game in their own repo, outside this monorepo, needs devkit to build and test it — which means they get
  it via npm install, exactly like they get @sharpee/sharpee and (today) @sharpee/transcript-tester. An unpublished
  tools/devkit literally can't reach them. So yes: devkit must be published.

  That reverses Decision 1's publish/location call (the name @sharpee/devkit and bin devkit still stand). It stays
  Sharpee-specific — not the generic "standalone product" I offered as the other extreme — just published so Sharpee
  authors can install it. It joins the publish set and the 6-point registration, same as bootstrap.

  One thing to pin down — what "part of the npm install" means precisely:

  - (A) Bundled into @sharpee/sharpee — authors already install the umbrella to get the platform; devkit's CLI comes
  with it. One install, devkit is just there.
  - (B) Separate dev-dependency — authors add npm i -D @sharpee/devkit alongside the platform, like adding any build
  tool.

  A is the most "part of the npm install" reading — install Sharpee, get the tool. B keeps the build tool out of the
  runtime dependency (you don't ship devkit to production). Most ecosystems lean B for build tooling, but A matches
  "it's part of the install."

  Which did you mean — does devkit ride along inside @sharpee/sharpee (A), or is it a separate devDep authors install
  (B)? Once you call it, I'll fix Decision 1 in the ADR.

✻ Baked for 1m 1s

❯ A                                                                                                                     

⏺ A it is — install the Sharpee SDK, get the devkit CLI. That cleanly reverses Decision 1, and it actually resolves
  nicely: since @sharpee/sharpee depends on @sharpee/devkit, npm drops devkit's bin into node_modules/.bin automatically
  — no re-declaration needed. devkit moves into the published graph (packages/devkit), and because packages/sharpee
  will depend on it, it has to be packages/, not tools/ (a published package can't depend on a tools/ workspace member
  that isn't published). 

  One consequence to name, not relitigate: A means devkit's build-only transitive deps (tsf, the bundler) ride along in
  anything that installs @sharpee/sharpee. That's clean if @sharpee/sharpee is the authoring SDK (authors install it to
  build/test; the runtime ships built bundles / the Story Runtime Baseline, not the umbrella). If stories ever
  runtime-depend on @sharpee/sharpee, devkit would bloat them. I'll note that assumption in the ADR rather than block on
  it.

  Let me fix the ADR (and the spec) — Decision 1, the layering diagram, and the now-wrong "Out of Scope: publishing
  devkit" line.

※ recap: We're productizing Sharpee's build/test tooling as a devtool (ADR-180), now PROPOSED and pushed for your review
  in PR #115. Next: read it on the app and tell me whether to accept it and start Phase 1 (the @sharpee/bootstrap 
  loader).
  
❯ So devkit can still build with a bundled system                                                                       

⏺ Right — and that actually defuses the bloat caveat I raised. Yes: devkit can ship as a bundled artifact. The repo
  already knows how to do this (esbuild → dist/cli/sharpee.js); devkit's CLI + its pure-JS orchestration deps (including
  tsf's logic) get bundled into a single distributed file. So @sharpee/sharpee depending on devkit pulls in one bundled
  tool, not a sprawling node_modules of build packages. The "build-only deps bloat the install" concern largely goes
  away.

  Two honest limits on how far the bundling goes — neither fatal:
  - Native-binary tools can't be inlined. esbuild ships per-platform binaries; if devkit uses it for bundling, esbuild
  stays a real dependency (or devkit shells out to one). Same for anything that isn't pure JS.
  - The author's TypeScript compiler stays real. Compiling a story's .ts needs tsc/typescript present — but the author
  needs that in their project anyway, so it's not devkit-imposed bloat.

  Net: shipping devkit bundled shrinks the install surface a lot, just not to literally zero. And it's orthogonal to
  Decision 6 — that's about devkit using the platform bundle as its fast run engine; this is about devkit itself being
  distributed bundled.

  So the Decision-1 assumption gets stronger: it's no longer "hope @sharpee/sharpee is authoring-only so the deps don't
  hurt" — it's "devkit ships bundled, so even ridden along in the SDK it stays lean."

  Want me to record "devkit is distributed as a bundled artifact" as an explicit decision in ADR-180 (with those two
  native-binary/tsc caveats)? It's a real distribution choice worth pinning, and it tightens the assumption I flagged.

✻ Worked for 52s

❯ Not what I meant. Can the author build a platform browser with the bundled platform + story so it is fast for users?  

⏺ Ah — got it now, totally different question. You're asking about the player-facing artifact, not devkit's
  distribution. Yes — that's exactly what the browser target is for.

  devkit build <story> --browser (the kept live target) compiles and bundles the platform + the story into one optimized
  static web app at dist/web/{story}/. The player loads a single fast payload — same speed rationale as the CLI bundle
  (dist/cli/sharpee.js, ~170ms), but for the browser: no module-by-module loading, just the bundled platform-browser
  runtime (ADR-170, framework-free) plus the compiled story, ready to serve.

  So in the devkit world there are really three distinct bundles, and I conflated them last turn:

  ADR-180 currently lists browser as a live target but only as "single-player static client" — it doesn't spell out the
  optimized bundled platform+story for end-user load intent. Want me to make that explicit in Decision 5 (and an AC: the
  browser build emits a self-contained bundle that boots without fetching the platform piecemeal)? That nails the
  player-performance contract so it can't get lost in implementation.

  ADR-180 now states the browser target as an explicit player-facing contract:

  ▎ An author can build a complete, fast, self-contained browser version of their story — the framework-free
  ▎ platform-browser runtime + compiled story bundled into one optimized payload that boots without fetching the
  ▎ platform piecemeal, portable to any static host.

  And AC-9 pins the performance guarantee so it can't get lost in implementation: cold cache, no app server, served as
  static files → boots and reaches the first turn in a single fast load.

  - CLI bundle → fast dev/test engine (devkit-internal).

  ⎿  Added 2 lines, removed 2 lines
      1  # ADR-180: Build/Test devkit and `@sharpee/bootstrap`
      2  
      3 -## Status: PROPOSED                                                                                     
      3 +## Status: ACCEPTED                                                                                     
      4  
      5 -## Date: 2026-06-18                                                                                     
      5 +## Date: 2026-06-18 (accepted 2026-06-18, session 90c6c2)                                               
      6  
      7  ## Context
      8  

  Read 3 files (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Important discovery before I write a line: the three loaders have behaviorally drifted, not just structurally
  duplicated —

  - story-loader.ts (cli.ts path): basic — no testing extension, engine.start() with no capabilities.
  - fast-cli.ts: adds the TestingExtension (for $ commands), still no explicit capabilities.
  - bundle-entry.js: TestingExtension + ADR-163/165 channel capabilities (engine.start({ capabilities })) +
  channel-packet handling — the most current/correct.

  So consolidation isn't behavior-neutral: it forces choosing one canonical behavior. The right choice is
  bundle-entry.js's — it's ADR-163-current and it's the path the dungeo walkthrough baseline already runs through, so
  adopting it keeps the main regression baseline unchanged while the other two paths upgrade to match. Let me capture
  the rest of that canonical loader before scaffolding.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The full canonical loader reveals the drift is deeper than capabilities — it's the output-capture pipeline itself:

  - story-loader.ts (cli.ts) and fast-cli.ts capture output via text:output + renderToString.
  - bundle-entry.js captures via ADR-163 channel:packet + flattenContent (the main channel), with channel:manifest and
  engine.start({ capabilities }).

  Those are two different output pipelines. Both currently produce passing results, but they're genuinely separate code
  — the bundle path is the ADR-163/174-current one; text:output is the older path. So "route the three loaders through
  one bootstrap" means picking the canonical pipeline and switching the other two onto it — not a behavior-neutral
  extraction.

  Canonical choice is clear: the channel:packet path. It's ADR-current and it's exactly what the dungeo walkthrough 
  baseline already runs through (the bundle), so adopting it leaves the main regression baseline untouched. The risk is
  concentrated in the cli.ts + fast-cli.ts paths flipping pipelines (their consumers: the npm-test harnesses, any
  fast-cli usage).
  conservative path if you'd rather land entry: + assembly first and de-risk the pipeline flip separately. Which way?

  daemon/visit interaction. Let me identify exactly which award is missing by replaying the bare command sequence and

⏺ Decisive finding: all 12 awards fire — 75/75 — when the commands run directly. So the walkthrough is complete; nothing

⏺ Decisive: isolated cli.ts = 70, direct bootstrap replay of the identical command sequence = 75. Same loader, same

⏺ All movement/action assertions pass in the runner (rooms are entered) — yet 5 points don't register that do in direct
  play. This is a transcript-runner execution discrepancy, and it's pre-existing (it produced the original failure too).
  One cheap check before I report: run isolated with --verbose and compare the scoring commands' output to direct
  replay, to spot which action doesn't "take":

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== runner (verbose) outputs for scoring commands ==="; node packages/transcript-tester/dist/cli.js
      tutorials/familyzoo tuto…)
  ⎿  === runner (verbose) outputs for scoring commands ===
         ✓ Contains "scored 0"
         You have scored 0 out of 75, earning you the rank of Novice.
     … +232 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ I've found it — and it's neither content nor loader. The smoking gun is in the verbose trace: v16 has turn-gated
  scheduler daemons (*DING DONG* "It's FEEDING TIME at the Petting Zoo!", closing-time announcements). And the event
  turn-numbers reveal the runner counts turns offset by one from direct play — the leading score meta-command doesn't
  advance a turn in the runner the way it does in direct executeCommand.

  So the chain is:
  1. The walkthrough collects a full 75/75 when run directly through the game (proven three ways).
  2. The runner yields 70 for the identical sequence, isolated.
  3. v16 gates some scoring on scheduler timing; the runner's turn-counting is off-by-one vs direct play, shifting the
  player relative to a turn-gated scoring window → one 5-point award is lost.

  This is a transcript-runner turn-counting bug, not a familyzoo content bug — and it's pre-existing (it produced the
  original "failure" too; my entry: fix just changed the denominator from 100 to 75). It's a transcript-tester
  (platform) issue, distinct from the ADR-180 entry: work, which is validated and correct.

  So the "missing 5 points" you asked me to chase resolved to: nothing is missing — the walkthrough is complete; the
  runner mis-times it. No content change would fix it correctly; the fix belongs in the runner's turn handling (make
  meta-commands like score count consistently with real play) or in not gating story scoring on exact turn numbers.

  I made no changes this turn — pure investigation. Where to take it:

  1. Fix the runner's turn-counting to match direct play (the correct fix; would also help any other turn-gated story) —
  a transcript-tester change, and it cleanly closes v16-scoring.
  2. De-gate v16's scoring from scheduler turns (story-side) — narrower, but masks the runner bug.
  3. Defer — log it; it doesn't block the ADR-180 platform work (entry: + the consumer rewiring still to finish).

  Given it's a genuine runner bug that could silently mis-score other turn-sensitive transcripts, I'd lean 1. But it's a
  fresh thread — want me to dig into the runner's turn handling, or bank this finding and get back to finishing the
  Phase 1 rewiring (bundle-entry.js + fast-cli.ts)?

  dungeo). AC-2's "v16-scoring via the bundle path" is blocked by this, independent of the loader work.

  WorldModel), but untested and a separate concern.

  Your call:
  1. Fix the bundle/helpers gap now — add @sharpee/helpers to the bundle, rebuild, validate familyzoo-via-bundle → fully
  closes AC-2.
  2. File it as a separate pre-existing issue and mark PR #117 ready — the loader-unification (the actual Phase 1
  deliverable) is done; the bundle-composition gap is orthogonal.
  3. Wrap here.

  I lean 2 — the helpers/bundle issue is genuinely separate from the loader productization, and bundling a new package
  warrants its own validation rather than a tail-end one-liner. Which way?
