⏺ I've now got the full picture of Phase 2. Here's what I found and a proposed plan.
     
  What Phase 2 is

  ADR-180 Phase 2: build one parameterized devkit test:npm <location> that reproduces all three npm consumer harnesses,
  then retire npm-test/, npm-test-dungeo/, npm-test-familyzoo/.

  The three harnesses are the same skeleton (temp dir → copy story src + transcripts → consumer package.json → npm 
  install → tsc → run transcripts → report) differing only on a few variants:

  ┌─────────────────┬─────────────────────┬────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Variant     │      npm-test       │    npm-test-dungeo     │          npm-test-familyzoo           │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Package source  │ registry (^0.9.95)  │ registry               │ local build (~/.tsf-publish tarballs) │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Dep closure     │ hand-listed         │ hand-listed            │ auto transitive (gen-consumer.mjs)    │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Transcript mode │ per-file            │ --chain                │ per-file                              │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Transcript glob │ tests/transcripts/* │ walkthroughs/wt-*      │ tests/transcripts/*                   │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Extras          │ —                   │ --quick, --walkthrough │ --build                               │
  └─────────────────┴─────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┘

  gen-consumer.mjs's auto transitive-closure approach is the drift-free one and should become the single mechanism for
  both registry and local modes (registry mode emits version refs instead of file: tarballs).

  Proposed plan

  1. Create packages/devkit (published @sharpee/devkit, bin devkit), 6-point registration per the new-package checklist.
  2. Implement devkit test:npm <location> with flags --local/--registry, --chain, --transcripts <glob>, --quick.
  Consumer-package generation reuses the closure logic from gen-consumer.mjs.
  3. devkit's own test suite + a real-path validation harness against a fixture story (AC-7).
  4. Validate parity against all three current harnesses, then delete them.

  This is a packages/ change, so per CLAUDE.md I'm discussing before writing any code. Three real decisions:

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · When should @sharpee/sharpee depend on @sharpee/devkit (so the `devkit` bin ships on npm install, per ADR 
     Decision 1)? → Defer to Phase 3 (Recommended)
     · What should `devkit test:npm` default to when no source flag is given? → Local build (Recommended)
     · What fixture should devkit's own validation harness (AC-7) use? → Reuse npm-test/ fixture (Recommended)
  
⏺ All three recommendations confirmed. Let me verify the registration mechanics so the plan is accurate.

  light. Writing the plan:

⏺ Plan written to docs/work/sharpee-devkit/plan-20260618-phase2-test-npm.md. Summary:

  Goal: Create @sharpee/devkit with one parameterized devkit test:npm <location> that collapses all three npm consumer
  harnesses, then retire them.

  Shape:
  - New packages/devkit (published, bin devkit). For test:npm it orchestrates npm/tsc/transcript-test as subprocesses —
  no @sharpee/* runtime deps, keeping Phase 2 light. 
  - The drift-free transitive-closure logic from gen-consumer.mjs becomes the single consumer-package generator,
  parameterized by --local (default, pack ~/.tsf-publish tarballs) vs --registry (version refs). Flags: --chain,
  --transcripts <glob>, --quick.
  - AC-7 fixture = the existing npm-test/ story moved to packages/devkit/fixtures/basic-story/.
  - Tests: unit (closure logic) + a real-path integration run against the fixture and ~/.tsf-publish (no stubs).

  Two gates I'll stop at:
  1. Parity — reproduce each harness's result before deleting anything (familyzoo --local ≈ 15/16 with v16 #116 known;
  dungeo --registry --chain; fixture --registry).
  2. Deletion — I will not remove npm-test*/ until parity passes and you confirm (per the
  never-delete-without-confirmation rule).

  One note: npm-test-dungeo --walkthrough (run the local bundle directly, no npm) isn't actually an npm consumer test —
  it's already covered by node dist/cli/sharpee.js --test --chain. I'm folding it into the Phase 3 devkit test surface
  rather than test:npm. Flagging in case you want it in scope here.

  This is a packages/ change — shall I proceed with implementation on a new branch feat/adr-180-phase2-test-npm?

⏺ Now tests. First the Behavior Statements (rule 12):

  computeClosure — DOES: returns all packages reachable from the seed via depsOf,
  including the seed. WHEN: given seed + depsOf. BECAUSE: a consumer must install the
  full transitive @sharpee closure. REJECTS: n/a (pure/total; cycles guarded by
  visited set).

  generateConsumer — DOES: writes a consumer package.json whose dependencies = sorted
  closure (local→file: tarballs, registry→version refs). WHEN: valid storyPkgPath +
  staging containing the seed. BECAUSE: single drift-free dep source (AC-4). REJECTS
  WHEN: a seed dep is absent from staging → throws; staging dir missing → throws.

  runTestNpm — DOES: stands up a temp consumer, installs/compiles/runs transcripts,
  returns pass/fail counts. WHEN: valid story location. BECAUSE: one command replaces
  three harnesses (AC-4). REJECTS WHEN: no package.json/src/ → throws; no transcripts
  matched (non-quick) → throws.

⏺ Important finding: @sharpee/bootstrap is unpublished on the registry, and staged
  package graphs diverge from published ones during the publish-lag window. This
  exposes a design flaw in my registry mode: computing the full closure from staging
  metadata and pinning every member to one version breaks when staging's graph ≠ the
  registry's.

  The correct behavior: registry mode should declare only the story's seed deps and 
  let npm resolve transitive deps from the registry (exactly how a real consumer
  installs). Full-closure packing stays for local mode (required there, since file:
  tarballs don't resolve their own @sharpee deps). Refactoring:

⏺ Phase 2 implementation is complete and validated. Here's where it stands:

  What's built

  @sharpee/devkit (packages/devkit) with devkit test:npm <location> — one
  parameterized command replacing all three harnesses. For test:npm it orchestrates
  npm/tsc/transcript-test as subprocesses (zero @sharpee runtime deps). The drift-free
  closure generator is the single mechanism; --local packs the full closure as
  tarballs, --registry declares seed deps and lets npm resolve transitively. Flags:
  --local/--registry, --chain, --transcripts, --quick, --version, --staging, --keep.
  Registered in ts-forge.config.json + build.sh. Unit tests: 9 passing, 1 gated
  (GREEN).

  Parity results

  Harness: npm-test-familyzoo (local)
  devkit equivalent: test:npm familyzoo --local             
  Result: 15/16 — exact match (v16 = known #116)                                  
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Harness: npm-test-dungeo (registry)       
  devkit equivalent: test:npm dungeo --registry --chain                           
  Result: pipeline ✅; tsc fails on publish-lag (registry 0.9.113 predates HEAD's 
    ADR-174 channel API) — old harness fails identically today
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Harness: npm-test (fixture, registry)     
  devkit equivalent: test:npm fixtures/basic-story --registry 
  Result: not run — same publish-lag wall
  
  The local path is fully proven (familyzoo). Registry e2e is publish-gated — it can't
  pass until the current batch (incl. unpublished @sharpee/bootstrap) is published.
  That's a real-world state, not a devkit defect.

  One finding to flag
  
  My extra dungeo --local diagnostic compiled cleanly but the walkthrough chain failed
  ~75% across two runs (not the RNG signature). Since familyzoo passes on the same
  staging, the local pipeline is sound — this is a dungeo-specific
  npm-staged-vs-bundle divergence (combat/save-restore), likely kin to issue #118.
  Orthogonal to Phase 2; no prior baseline contradicted (dungeo was never a local-npm
  subject).

  Two things I need from you

⏺ PR #121 merged to main. Phase 3 plan written to
  docs/work/sharpee-devkit/plan-20260618-phase3-build-replacement.md.

  Phase 3 is large — it replaces a 1334-line build.sh. I've mapped the full parity
  surface and resolved the ADR's two open questions:
  - tsf ↔ locations: monorepo stories build via pnpm --filter (as today); external
  paths run their own npm run build (as test:npm already does).
  - AC-5 "byte-identical": impossible while BUILD_DATE is a live timestamp. Fix: make
  build-date injectable so the parity harness freezes version + date and gets a true
  byte-for-byte diff (esbuild is deterministic given identical inputs).

  I've decomposed it into 4 independently-shippable sub-increments, with build.sh
  coexisting until the final cutover so nothing breaks mid-flight:
  - 3a — devkit build + devkit bundle (CLI bundle) — version stamping, ordered
  platform build via tsf, genai-api, story build, esbuild bundle. The core.
  - 3b — browser target (--browser → dist/web/{story}/).
  - 3c — zifmia target.
  - 3d — cutover: parity gate green → delete build.sh + dead scripts; add devkit
  clean/verify; umbrella wiring; AC-8 doc sweep.

  This is a platform change, so confirming direction before I write code:

⏺ Found it: tsf is node_modules/.bin/tsf (a real workspace bin), but it's also a shell
  alias in the interactive profile — and execFileSync (devkit) / bare tsf (build.sh
  non-interactive) can't resolve an alias. The ESM-pass output (dist-esm) doesn't even
  feed the browser bundle (which uses CJS via --conditions=require), so this only
  blocks completion.

  Right fix: make devkit resolve tsf to the workspace bin deterministically (more
  robust than build.sh's bare tsf). Updating build.ts:

⏺ Update(packages/devkit/src/commands/build.ts)
  ⎿  Added 10 lines
      54    return new Date().toISOString().replace(/\.\d{3}Z$/, 'Z');
      55  }
      56  
      57 +/**                                                                  
      58 + * Resolve the `tsf` executable. Prefers the workspace-local `node_mo
         +dules/.bin/tsf`                                                      
      59 + * (build.sh relies on a bare `tsf`, which fails when tsf is only a s
         +hell alias);                                                         
      60 + * falls back to `tsf` on PATH. Produces identical compiler output ei
         +ther way.                                                            
      61 + */                                                                  
      62 +function tsfBin(root: string): string {                              
      63 +  const local = join(root, 'node_modules', '.bin', 'tsf');           
      64 +  return existsSync(local) ? local : 'tsf';                          
      65 +}                                                                    
      66 +                                                                     
      67  /** Rewrite a package.json's version, preserving key order + 2-space
          indent + trailing newline. */
      68  function writePkgVersion(pkgPath: string, version: string): void {
      69    const pkg = JSON.parse(readFileSync(pkgPath, 'utf8'));

  ADR-180 Phase 3b — complete

  PR #123: https://github.com/ChicagoDave/sharpee/pull/123 (commit a288961e)

  What landed: devkit build <story> --browser → the self-contained dist/web/<story>/
  client (IIFE game.js + CSS + themes + assets + website mirror), a faithful port of
  build.sh's build_browser_client.

  Byte-parity gate: PASS — dist/web/dungeo/ and the website mirror are byte-identical
  to build.sh through two real full browser builds. Two real issues surfaced and fixed
  en route:
  - devkit copied a .DS_Store that build.sh's shell glob skips → devkit now skips
  dotfiles (matches build.sh + keeps Finder cruft out of the deliverable).
  - tsf robustness: devkit resolves it via node_modules/.bin/tsf rather than a bare
  tsf that fails when tsf is only a shell alias — devkit's ESM pass now works where
  build.sh's wouldn't.

  AC-9 met. 17 unit tests green.

  One process note: I committed 3b to main by mistake (forgot to branch after the 3a
  merge), caught it on push, moved the commit to feat/adr-180-phase3b-browser, and
  rewound main to origin — no harm done, nothing pushed to main.

  I held off merging #123. Want me to merge it and start 3c (zifmia), or pause here?
  Heads-up: 3d is the irreversible cutover (deletes build.sh) — I'll stop for explicit
  confirmation before that one regardless.

⏺ Plan written. 3d is the cutover — I'll do the non-destructive work first
      101  /**
      102 + * Resolve the `tsf` executable. Prefers the workspace-local `node_m
          +odules/.bin/tsf`                                                    
      103 + * (a bare `tsf` fails when tsf is only a shell alias / not on a non
          +-interactive PATH);                                                 
      104 + * falls back to `tsf` on PATH. Produces identical compiler output e
          +ither way.                                                          
      105 + */                                                                 
      106 +export function tsfBin(root: string): string {                      
      107 +  const local = join(root, 'node_modules', '.bin', 'tsf');          
      108 +  return existsSync(local) ? local : 'tsf';                         
      109 +}

      113   * `stories/<name>` then `tutorials/<name>`. Returns absolute path o
           r null.
      114   */

       43  ## Build Order
       44  
       74 -The build system ensures correct dependency order:                  
       45 +1. **Stamp versions** — writes `version.ts` + `package.json` version
          +s (unless `--no-version`)                                           
       46 +2. **Build platform** — compiles all platform packages in dependency
          + order (via `pnpm --filter`)                                        
       47 +3. **Generate genai-api** — refreshes `packages/sharpee/docs/genai-a
          +pi/`                                                                
       48 +4. **Build story** — compiles the story (if given)                  
       49 +5. **Bundle** — assembles `dist/cli/sharpee.js`                     
       50 +6. **Client targets** — `--browser` and/or `--zifmia` (if requested)
       51  
       76 -1. **Update versions** - Generates version files (unless `--no-versi
          -on`)                                                                
       77 -2. **Build platform** - Compiles all platform packages in order     
       78 -3. **Bundle** - Creates `dist/cli/sharpee.js`                       
       79 -4. **Build story** - Compiles the specified story (if `-s`)         
       80 -5. **Build client** - Creates client bundle (if `-c`)               
       81 -                                                                    
       82 -### Platform Package Order                                          
       83 -                                                                    
       84 -1. core                                                             
       85 -2. if-domain                                                        
       86 -3. world-model                                                      
       87 -4. event-processor                                                  
       88 -5. lang-en-us                                                       
       89 -6. parser-en-us                                                     
       90 -7. if-services                                                      
       91 -8. text-blocks                                                      
       92 -9. text-service                                                     
       93 -10. stdlib                                                          
       94 -11. plugins, plugin-npc, plugin-scheduler, plugin-state-machine     
       95 -12. engine                                                          
       96 -13. sharpee                                                         
       97 -14. transcript-tester                                               
       98 -

       53  
      101 -### Format                                                          
       54 +Versions use a simple prerelease format `X.Y.Z-beta` (e.g. `0.9.113-
          +beta`). The build                                                   
       55 +stamps story/client `version.ts` files:                             
       56  
      103 -Versions use a simple prerelease format:                            
      104 -                                                                    
      105 -```                                                                 
      106 -X.Y.Z-beta                                                          
      107 -```                                                                 
      108 -                                                                    
      109 -Example: `0.9.85-beta`                                              
      110 -                                                                    
      111 -- `X.Y.Z` - Base semantic version (from package.json)               
      112 -- `beta` - Prerelease tag                                           
      113 -                                                                    
      114 -### Generated Files                                                 
      115 -

      117 -

      106  ## Performance Tips
      107  
      188 -1. **Use `--skip`** - Always skip unchanged packages                
      189 -2. **Use `--no-version`** - Skip version bumps during rapid iteratio
          -n                                                                   
      190 -3. **Use the bundle** - `node dist/cli/sharpee.js` loads in ~170ms v
          -s 5+ seconds for packages                                           
      108 +1. **Use `--skip <pkg>`** — resume from the changed package; skip un
          +changed ones.                                                       
      109 +2. **Use `--no-version`** — skip version stamping during rapid itera
          +tion.                                                               
      110 +3. **Use the bundle** — `node dist/cli/sharpee.js` loads in ~170ms v
          +s 5+ seconds for packages.

          -erbose output.                                                      
      116 +`devkit build` asserts each package emits `dist/index.js` (the `.tsb
          +uildinfo` silent-no-op                                              
      117 +class is precluded). If artifacts look stale, run `devkit clean` the
          +n rebuild.                                                          
      118  
      198 -### Circular dependency detected                                    
      119 +### Circular dependency                                             
      120  
      200 -Use `madge` to find cycles:                                         
      201 -                                                                    
      121  ```bash
      122  npx madge --circular stories/dungeo/dist/index.js
      123  ```
     ...
      126  
      127  ### TypeScript errors

          -se `--skip` to build from a specific package.

      212 -### Stale bundle

      214 -Force a full rebuild without `--skip`:                              
      215 -

      217 -./build.sh -s dungeo                                                
      218 -```                                                                 
      129 +Build the failing package's dependencies first, or use `--skip <pkg>
          +` to build from it.

⏺ This is material new information that changes the picture. Deleting build.sh broke
  more than the doc sweep — there are active invokers beyond AC-8's named scope:

  - build-macos.sh / build-ubuntu.sh (lines 176/214): real release wrappers that parse
  --zifmia then bash build.sh "$@" — broken.
  - pack-release.sh (line 54): ./build.sh --no-version — but this is the dead script
  the ADR flagged for deletion (also references the removed text-service).
  - tools/vscode-ext/src/build-provider.ts: invokes the buildScript config + -s <id> —
  broken (devkit takes a positional story, not -s).
  - tools/ide/SharpeeIDE/*.swift: the IDE references build.sh — but that's your
  parallel IDE session, which my memory says to treat as out-of-scope for this
  session's commits.
  - tools/zifmia/tests/e2e/global-setup.ts: a fix: hint string.

  The "delete build.sh now" call was made when the visible live references looked like
  the doc sweep plus a couple of comments. The actual blast radius is wider and
  entangles out-of-scope IDE code. build.sh is deleted in the working tree but not
  committed — easily restored.

❯ we should have talked about the devkit usage patterns. I would have thought a build

⏺ You're right, and I should have surfaced the invocation pattern as a decision
  instead of quietly standardizing on node packages/devkit/dist/cli.js … during the
  doc sweep. That was a unilateral call on developer-facing ergonomics — exactly the
  kind of thing worth a quick conversation. Two honest gaps fell out of it:

  1. No convenient entry point. I leaned on node packages/devkit/dist/cli.js. The
  devkit bin does exist (the umbrella wiring puts it in node_modules/.bin), but
  in-repo that's only reachable via pnpm exec devkit — there's no ./devkit at the repo
  root the way there was ./build.sh. Your ./devkit … is the natural expectation and I
  didn't provide it.
  2. build takes a name, not a path — which actually contradicts ADR-180. The ADR's
  Decision 4 is explicit: "a story is a location… devkit build <path>." But I
  implemented build's story arg as a bare name resolved through the
  stories//tutorials/ convention (resolveStoryDir), so devkit build stories/dungeo or
  an out-of-repo path fails today. Ironically test:npm already takes a real path, so
  the two commands are inconsistent — and your path-to-story instinct matches the ADR
  better than what I shipped.

  (On --build vs build: the approved ADR CLI surface is subcommand-style — devkit 
  build|bundle|test|… — so I'd lean to keeping build as a subcommand rather than a
  --build flag, but that's part of what's worth confirming.)

  This is a clean follow-up PR: add a ./devkit entry, make build accept a path (per
  the ADR), and re-sweep the docs I just wrote. Before I touch anything, let me get
  the shape right:

     #!/bin/bash
     … +39 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
  ⎿  Shell cwd was reset to /Users/david/repos/sharpee

⏺ That's the model to follow. Here's the DevArch pattern:
  
  - A single committed executable at a known path (docs/workflow/devarch, #!/bin/bash,
  chmod +x), invoked as ./docs/workflow/devarch <subcommand> — e.g.
  ./docs/workflow/devarch install, … init, … activate <capability>.
  - Subcommand style (install/update/init/activate), with --flags for options — not
  flag-style verbs.
  - It resolves through symlinks, so a global npm install also lets you type bare
  devarch <cmd>. In-repo path form and installed-bin form coexist.

  So your instinct is right, and DevArch settles the open questions for me — I don't
  need to ask:

  1. Add a committed ./devkit wrapper (bash, execs node packages/devkit/dist/cli.js 
  "$@", resolves its own dir). Invoked ./devkit build <path> — exactly what you
  expected. The one difference from DevArch: its logic is self-contained bash;
  devkit's logic is in TS, so the wrapper is a thin shim over the node CLI. The
  published devkit bin (via @sharpee/sharpee) already covers installed consumers —
  same dual in-repo/installed shape as DevArch's symlink.
  2. Keep subcommands (build, bundle, test:npm, clean, verify) — DevArch uses
  subcommands, so build, not --build. Your --build was the one thing I'd push back on,
  and DevArch backs that up.
  3. Make build take a path (./devkit build stories/dungeo or any location), with
  bare-name fallback to stories//tutorials/ — this fixes the ADR-Decision-4 deviation
  and matches test:npm, which already takes a path.

  Then re-sweep the docs I wrote from node packages/devkit/dist/cli.js build dungeo →
  ./devkit build <path>.

  This is a focused follow-up PR (new ./devkit wrapper + resolveStoryDir path support
  + doc re-sweep + a unit test for path resolution). Want me to build it on a
  feat/adr-180-devkit-entry branch?

  Modeled on DevArch (./docs/workflow/devarch <subcommand> — committed executable,
  subcommand style, in-repo-path + installed-bin coexist):

  1. ./devkit wrapper at repo root — thin bash shim that execs node 
  packages/devkit/dist/cli.js "$@" (DevArch's logic is self-contained bash; ours is
  TS, so it's a shim, not the logic). The published bin still serves installed
  consumers.

  subcommand style, so build not --build.

  bare-name fallback — fixes the ADR Decision-4 deviation and matches test:npm.

  tutorials/familyzoo all work.

  5. Tests for resolveStory + a wrapper smoke.

  Three decisions I want your call on before building:

  - Wrapper when devkit isn't built yet: error with pnpm --filter @sharpee/devkit 
  build instructions (my rec — predictable), vs auto-build on first run (more like
  ./build.sh "just worked", but a surprise pnpm call).
  - Wrapper location: repo root ./devkit (my rec — matches the old ./build.sh), vs a
  path like packages/devkit/devkit (closer to DevArch's docs/workflow/ placement).
  - Out-of-repo paths: defer (my rec — a path outside the workspace can't use pnpm 
  --filter; it needs the story's own build script + pairs with the reserved
  ~/.sharpee/devkit registry), vs implement now.

  How do you want those three?
  
✻ Cooked for 1m 29s

❯ you need to explain those open questions better                                     

⏺ Fair — let me explain what's actually behind each, because each one has a real
  mechanic that isn't obvious from the one-liners.

  1. What happens when devkit itself isn't built yet

  The mechanic: the old ./build.sh was pure bash — it ran instantly on a fresh clone,
  nothing to compile. devkit is the opposite: it's a TypeScript tool, so
  packages/devkit/dist/cli.js only exists after you compile it, and dist/ is
  gitignored. So on a fresh clone, after devkit clean, or after pulling devkit source
  changes, ./devkit would point at a file that isn't there yet. The tool that builds
  everything has to be built first — a chicken-and-egg the bash script never had.

  So the wrapper has to decide what to do when its own dist/cli.js is missing:

  - Error with instructions (my rec): print "devkit not built — run pnpm --filter 
  @sharpee/devkit build" and exit. Predictable, nothing happens behind your back — but
  a dev hits a wall on first use and runs a second command.
  - Auto-build on first run: the wrapper silently runs the devkit build itself, then
  proceeds. Feels like ./build.sh "just worked." Costs: a hidden multi-second pnpm
  build on first invoke, assumes pnpm + pnpm install are already done, and it only
  triggers when dist is absent — it won't catch a stale dist (devkit source changed
  but dist exists), so it's a bootstrap convenience, not a freshness guarantee.

  (Staleness — rebuilding devkit when its own source changed — is a separate question;
  the realistic answer there is "devs rebuild devkit when they edit it," same as any
  package. The wrapper shouldn't rebuild devkit on every call.)

  2. Where the ./devkit file lives

  - Repo root ./devkit (my rec): sits exactly where ./build.sh was, so ./devkit build 
  dungeo matches muscle memory and is short. Cost: one more file at the repo root.
  - packages/devkit/devkit: lives with the package it fronts (cleaner ownership), but
  you'd type ./packages/devkit/devkit build dungeo — long, which defeats the ergonomic
  point. DevArch only tolerates a deep path (docs/workflow/devarch) because it's
  vendored into a consumer repo; we own the repo, so we don't have that constraint.
  - Middle option — no new file at all: a root package.json script, so you run pnpm 
  devkit build dungeo. Idiomatic for a pnpm monorepo, nothing committed at root — but
  it's pnpm devkit (extra typing + pnpm's startup overhead) and doesn't match the
  ./devkit you described.

  The tension is just ergonomics-at-root vs co-location-with-package. Since the whole
  reason for this work is ergonomics, root wins on its own terms.

  3. Out-of-repo story paths — why it's a second build mechanism, not just "accept any
  path"

  The mechanic: when devkit builds an in-repo story, it runs pnpm --filter 
  @sharpee/story-dungeo build. That works only because dungeo is a workspace package —
  it's in pnpm-workspace.yaml, so pnpm knows it by name and --filter finds it.

  ADR Decision 4's vision is "a story is a location anywhere, including other repos" —
  e.g. ./devkit build ~/my-game. But an external story is not a workspace package:
  there's no @sharpee/story-my-game for --filter to find. To build it, devkit would
  have to run that story's own build (npm run build/tsc inside its directory), the way
  there's no @sharpee/story-my-game for --filter to find. To build it, devkit would
  have to run that story's own build (npm run build/tsc inside its directory), the way
  an external author building their own game does. That's a genuinely different code
  path from pnpm --filter — so "accept any path" actually means "add a second build
  mechanism."

  This is exactly ADR-180's Open Question #1 (tsf ↔ arbitrary locations), which the
  ADR left for implementation time. And it pairs with the reserved ~/.sharpee/devkit
  registry: the point of "story anywhere" is devkit init ~/my-game → devkit build
  my-game, and init/list/the registry aren't built yet. So even if I added
  external-path building now, its companion (registration) would be missing, making it
  only half-usable.

  - Defer (my rec): build handles in-repo paths and bare names now — that covers
  ./devkit build stories/dungeo, tutorials/familyzoo, dungeo, i.e. everything in this
  monorepo. A path that resolves outside the workspace errors clearly ("out-of-repo
  builds come with devkit init/the location registry"). Focused, nothing half-built.
  - Implement now: add the story's-own-build path for external locations. Resolves ADR
  Open Q1, but expands this PR and lands a capability whose registration half is
  still reserved.

  So none of the three blocks the core win (a ./devkit build <in-repo-path> that
  matches ./build.sh). They're about: the bootstrap wrinkle, the file's home, and how
  far to chase the "story anywhere" vision now. Which way do you want each?

✻ Cogitated for 1m 3s

⏺ This is the key thing I missed. The website's installed-author doc builds with npx 
  @sharpee/sharpee build (and init, init-browser) — the @sharpee/sharpee bin's own 
  CLI, which is separate from devkit. Let me see what that bin actually does, since it
  determines how the two entry points relate:

⏺ Now it's clear, and it exposes something bigger than my plan. There are two separate
  CLIs for the two audiences:

  - @sharpee/sharpee bin (packages/sharpee/src/cli/): init, init-browser, build,
  build-browser, ifid. This is the installed-author tool — documented in the website
  as npx @sharpee/sharpee init my-adventure → npx @sharpee/sharpee build. It compiles
  their single story against the published packages.
  - devkit (ADR-180): builds the monorepo — platform packages in dependency order, the
  CLI bundle, in-repo story builds. The repo-clone author / maintainer tool that
  replaced ./build.sh.

  Let me confirm what the sharpee CLI's build actually does, so I describe the overlap
  accurately:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee; echo "=== sharpee CLI build.ts (what it builds)
      ==="; sed -n '1,40p' packages/sharpee/src/cli/build.ts)
  ⎿  === sharpee CLI build.ts (what it builds) ===
     /**
      * CLI: sharpee build
     … +38 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ That confirms it — and it's bigger than a wrapper. @sharpee/sharpee build already 
  does what an installed author needs: it builds a .sharpee bundle + browser client
  from a standalone story project, and with --test compiles and runs transcripts. So
  the installed author's toolkit (init, build, build-browser, .sharpee, --test) is
  complete and separate from devkit.

  That surfaces three things my plan and even the 3d cutover glossed over:

  1. Out-of-repo isn't devkit's job at all. I framed my Q3 as "defer out-of-repo path
  support in devkit." But the installed/standalone author is already served by the
  @sharpee/sharpee CLI. So devkit legitimately only ever needs in-repo paths — Q3
  isn't "defer," it's "out of scope by design." That simplifies the plan.
  2. The .sharpee gap I called 'accepted' in 3d is narrower than I said. The monorepo
  dist/stories/X.sharpee path (build.sh build_story_bundle) did go away — but .sharpee
  building itself still exists via npx @sharpee/sharpee build. I overstated the loss
  in the 3d PR/docs; that's worth correcting.
  3. ADR-180 Decision 1 ("ship devkit via @sharpee/sharpee so installed authors get 
  the bin") looks redundant. Installed authors use the @sharpee/sharpee CLI, not
  devkit. And the two CLIs now overlap — both build browser clients (devkit --browser
  for a monorepo story; @sharpee/sharpee build-browser for a standalone project).

  So the real fork — which should drive the entry-point design — is what's the 
  boundary between the two CLIs?

  - A. Two tools, clean split (my lean): devkit = repo/maintainer monorepo build
  (platform + bundle + in-repo stories). @sharpee/sharpee CLI = installed single-story
  author. devkit is not shipped via the SDK (revisit Decision 1); devkit handles
  in-repo paths only; docs stay split (./devkit for the repo entry point,
  @sharpee/sharpee for the install entry point).
  - B. Unify on devkit: the @sharpee/sharpee build/build-browser commands delegate to
  (or are replaced by) devkit; devkit absorbs single-story/standalone building. One
  build tool, bigger change, kills the overlap.
  - C. Status quo + just add ./devkit: leave both as-is, accept the overlap, only add
  the ./devkit wrapper + in-repo path support for the repo author; defer the boundary
  question.

  My plan as written assumed something like A but still tried to chase out-of-repo in
  devkit, which double-covers the sharpee CLI. Before I revise it: which model do you
  want — A, B, or C? That decides whether devkit gets out-of-repo support at all,
  whether Decision 1 stands, and how the two doc entry points should describe
  building.

✻ Churned for 2m 33s
  
❯ the problem is that we're unifying build processes, so the npm build process should 
  be the same as the repo build process, though the repo process will have more       
  detailed experienced developer steps                                                
  
⏺ That's the key reframe — it's Model B, but the framing is "one process, two depths,"
  not "two tools." Let me state it back to make sure I've got it:

  One build tool (devkit), one process. Both audiences run the same devkit commands;
  the repo/monorepo author just has additional experienced-dev steps available.

  - Installed author (npm install @sharpee/sharpee, story = their project): devkit 
  build compiles their story against the published packages and produces the
  browser/.sharpee outputs. No platform-package building — they consume published
  @sharpee/*.
  - Repo author (monorepo): the same devkit build, plus the deeper steps — building
  the platform packages in dependency order, the CLI bundle, --skip, parity,
  verify/publish.

  devkit decides which depth by context: a workspace (pnpm-workspace.yaml, story is a
  workspace package) → full monorepo build; a standalone project → just the author's
  story. Same command, location-aware.

  This actually flips three of my earlier conclusions:

  1. Decision 1 was right after all. devkit should ship via @sharpee/sharpee — because
  it's the installed author's build tool too, not just the maintainer's.
  2. "Out-of-repo" is core, not deferred. The standalone author IS that case, and they
  use devkit — so devkit must gain the standalone build path (compile the author's
  story via its own toolchain + browser + .sharpee).
  3. The @sharpee/sharpee CLI's build commands unify into devkit. @sharpee/sharpee 
  build/build-browser (and its .sharpee bundling) either delegate to devkit or move
  into it. That also restores the .sharpee capability I thought 3d lost — it just
  becomes a devkit command.

  Two concrete things this surfaces that the revised plan has to settle:

  - An init naming collision. @sharpee/sharpee init my-adventure scaffolds a new story
  project; ADR-180's devkit init <location> registers a name→path in 
  ~/.sharpee/devkit. Unified, devkit init can only mean one — scaffolding is the
  author-valuable one; the registry needs a different verb (devkit register?).
  - Delegate vs absorb: does the @sharpee/sharpee bin keep its commands but shell out
  to devkit, or do those commands move into devkit and the sharpee bin just re-expose
  them?

  This is bigger than the "add a ./devkit wrapper" follow-up I scoped — it's
  completing ADR-180's unification intent and resolving its Open Q1 (tsf ↔ arbitrary
  locations). I think the right move is to rewrite the plan around this unification
  (and likely record it as an ADR-180 amendment, since it changes devkit's scope,
  confirms Decision 1, and folds in the sharpee CLI), rather than ship the narrow
  entry-point PR.

  Want me to rewrite plan-20260618-devkit-entry.md into a "unify the build process on
  devkit" plan along these lines — and should that carry an ADR-180 amendment? I'll
  hold off on any code until we've got that plan right.

✻ Brewed for 57s

❯ we dump the npx stuff and replace it with devkit (or at this point do we name it    
  'sharpee'?                                                                          
  
⏺ Yes — I'd name it sharpee, and now is the cheapest possible moment to decide it
  (devkit is five PRs old and not yet in anyone's muscle memory; once authors learn
  devkit, renaming gets expensive).

  The reasoning lines up cleanly:
  - It's the brand and the front door. DevArch's command is devarch — the product
  name, one word, not "devarch-kit." The Sharpee analog is sharpee, not a second
  "devkit" brand an author has to learn after installing @sharpee/sharpee.
  - The sharpee bin already exists (@sharpee/sharpee → dist/cli/index.js). An
  installed author typing sharpee build is the most intuitive thing there is; npx 
  @sharpee/sharpee build collapses to sharpee build.
  - ADR-180 itself said devkit is "Sharpee-specific, not a generic standalone product"
  — which is an argument for naming it after the product.

  What I'd keep vs change (so we don't throw away the merged work):
  - @sharpee/devkit survives as the internal build engine — all the logic I just built
  (build.ts, bundle.ts, browser.ts, zifmia.ts, consumer-gen.ts, clean/verify) stays.
  That also keeps the build-only deps (tsf, transcript-tester) out of the runtime
  surface, which Decision 1 explicitly worried about.
  - The sharpee command becomes the unified front door, delegating into devkit.
  @sharpee/sharpee already depends on @sharpee/devkit (wired in 3d), so its bin just
  re-exposes devkit's commands as sharpee build/test/play/bundle/clean/verify, plus
  the standalone-author commands (init, build-browser/.sharpee) that consolidate from
  the old sharpee CLI.
  - Drop the devkit bin (or keep it as a hidden alias). Repo entry becomes ./sharpee
  (root wrapper); installed entry is the sharpee bin.

  Cost of deciding sharpee now: re-point the bin/command surface, and re-sweep the
  docs I touched in 3d (./devkit build → ./sharpee build, node packages/devkit/... →
  sharpee ...). The implementation modules don't move. It's churn, but small churn,
  and far smaller than doing it later.

  So the consolidated picture: one command, sharpee; one engine, @sharpee/devkit; one 
  process, two depths (standalone author vs monorepo maintainer). The init collision
  still needs settling (scaffold-a-project wins the sharpee init verb; the
  location-registry gets a different verb), and we choose delegate-vs-absorb for the
  old sharpee CLI commands.

  If that's the direction, I'll rewrite the plan as "unify the build process under the
  sharpee command (devkit engine)" with an ADR-180 amendment capturing: command
  renamed to sharpee, devkit = internal engine, Decision 1 affirmed, Open Q1
  (standalone/arbitrary-location build) resolved, and the sharpee-CLI consolidation.
  Shall I write that up — still no code until the plan's right?

  author run the same sharpee build; the repo author has the extra experienced-dev
  steps. Engine stays @sharpee/devkit; the sharpee bin is the front door that

  sharpee register/list.

  .sharpee), U3 (scaffolding + registry), plus an ADR-180 amendment alongside U1.

  build/test/bundle/browser logic clearly lives in the devkit engine — but scaffolding
  (init, ifid): does that move into devkit too, or stay in @sharpee/sharpee's CLI and
  just get dispatched? It's package-shaped (it scaffolds a project that depends on
  @sharpee/sharpee), so I lean toward leaving it in the sharpee package — but your
  call.

✻ Baked for 1m 23s

❯ /devarch:plan-review                                                                

⏺ The plan lives at docs/work/sharpee-devkit/plan-20260618-unify-sharpee-cli.md (per
  our convention — plans go to docs/work, not docs/context/plan.md), and it doesn't
  carry a formal "References consulted" section. Rather than punt on the literal
  precondition, I'll run the adversarial check against the reference this plan
  actually governs and amends — ADR-180 — since that's where the conflict surface
  lives.

  Plan Review: unify the build process under the `sharpee` command (devkit engine)
  Checked 3 phases (U1/U2/U3 + amendment) against ADR-180
  ════════════════════════════════════

  Findings (by severity):
  
    [CONTRADICTION] Command renamed devkit → sharpee
      Where:     Phase U1 — command/bin surface
      Reference: ADR-180 §Decision 1 ("CLI bin `devkit`") + §CLI surface
                 ("devkit build|bundle|test|test:npm|verify|clean|init|list")
      Conflict:  Plan makes the command `sharpee`; ADR-180 fixes the bin as `devkit`.
      Fix:       Amend the ADR — rewrite §Decision 1 (bin = `sharpee`;
  `@sharpee/devkit`
                 is the internal engine) and the §CLI surface line. The plan already
                 commits to this amendment; it must land WITH U1, not after.

    [CONTRADICTION] `init` redefined (scaffold vs register-location)
      Where:     Phase U3 — `sharpee init` / `sharpee register`
      Reference: ADR-180 §Decision 4, §"~/.sharpee/devkit memory format"
                 ("`devkit init <location>` upserts an entry"), §AC-3
                 ("`devkit test <name>` (after init)"), §Invariants
      Conflict:  Plan makes `init` = scaffold a project and moves
  location-registration
                 to `register`; ADR-180 defines `init` = register a name→path.
      Fix:       Amend the ADR — in §Decision 4, the memory-format section, AC-3, and
                 the invariants, rename the registry verb to `register`/`list` and
  free
                 `init` for scaffolding. (Plan's U3 + amendment cover this; make the
                 section list explicit so nothing is missed.)

    [TENSION] Absorbing sharpee-CLI logic risks a dependency cycle   (advisory)
      Where:     Phase U2 — "absorb build/browser/.sharpee into the engine"
      Reference: ADR-180 §Invariants ("devkit depends on tsf and transcript-tester;
                 never the reverse — no cycle")
      Note:      Moving `build-browser`/`.sharpee` logic out of packages/sharpee into
                 @sharpee/devkit must NOT make devkit import @sharpee/sharpee — that
                 would create sharpee → devkit → sharpee. Keep the dependency one-way
                 (sharpee bin → devkit engine only).

    [TENSION] `./sharpee` root wrapper vs "no new bash scripts"   (advisory)
      Where:     Phase U1 — committed `./sharpee` wrapper
      Reference: ADR-180 §"Constrains Future Sessions" ("New build/test/run
                 capabilities go in devkit, not new bash scripts")
      Note:      Fine as long as the wrapper stays a thin exec shim (no build logic in
                 bash); the constraint targets logic-in-bash, which this respects.

    [TENSION] Standalone build/test must still load via bootstrap   (advisory)
      Where:     Phase U2 — standalone build/test mode
      Reference: ADR-180 §Invariants ("exactly one story-loading implementation,
                 @sharpee/bootstrap")
      Note:      Standalone *compilation* is new, but runtime *loading* (test/play)
                 must still go through @sharpee/bootstrap — don't introduce a second
                 loader for the standalone path.

  ────────────────────────────────────
  Verdict: CONFLICTS FOUND — resolve before implementing

  Bottom line: the two CONTRACTIONS are both with ADR-180 as currently written, and
  both are things the plan already intends to fix by amending ADR-180 (rename
  devkit→sharpee, init→register). They're blocking only in the sense that the
  amendment must land with Phase U1 — if we rename the command and redefine init
  without updating ADR-180 §Decision 1 / §Decision 4 / §CLI surface / §AC-3 /
  §Invariants, the code and the record diverge. The three TENSIONS are advisory
  guardrails for U2 (cycle direction, bootstrap-only loading) and U1 (keep the wrapper
  a thin shim).

  So the plan is sound provided the ADR-180 amendment is treated as a U1 deliverable,
  not a footnote. Want me to expand the plan's "ADR-180 amendment" bullet into an
  explicit checklist of the exact sections to rewrite (Decision 1, Decision 4, CLI
  surface, memory-format, AC-3, Invariants), so the amendment can't drift from the
  rename? Still no code.

※ recap: ADR-180 is delivered and merged; now we're planning the follow-up to unify 
  build under one `sharpee` command (devkit as engine). The plan is written and

❯ move to devkit - I think the npm package and execution roles are confusing and      
  having a distinct execution tool just more clear                                    
  
⏺ Got it — scaffolding logic moves into the devkit engine. But your reasoning ("the
  npm package and execution roles are confusing; a distinct execution tool is
  clearer") actually pushes on something bigger than scaffolding, and it cuts against
  the command name we locked in earlier — so let me flag it rather than quietly run
  with sharpee.

  The tension: if the goal is to keep the execution tool distinct from the 
  @sharpee/sharpee library package, then naming the command sharpee re-creates exactly
  the overload you're calling confusing — import { … } from '@sharpee/sharpee' (the
  library) vs sharpee build (the tool), same word for both roles. The cleanest
  expression of "distinct execution tool" is to let the tool keep its own distinct
  name: devkit.

  Two coherent end-states:

  (1) Distinct tool, name stays devkit (matches your reasoning — my new rec):
  - Command = devkit (+ ./devkit repo wrapper). @sharpee/devkit owns all execution
  logic — build/test/bundle/browser/.sharpee/scaffold.
  - @sharpee/sharpee becomes a pure library (the importable SDK) — its CLI/bin is
  dropped.
  - Installed authors still get the devkit bin because @sharpee/sharpee depends on
  @sharpee/devkit (ADR-180 Decision 1) — so npm install @sharpee/sharpee puts devkit
  on PATH.
  - Bonus: this cancels most of the U1 amendment churn — the just-merged devkit name
  stays, no rename, no doc re-sweep from devkit→sharpee. The amendment shrinks to:
  Decision 4 (init=scaffold, register verb), Decision 5 (.sharpee restored), Open Q1
  (resolved), and the engine/library role split.

  (2) Command = sharpee (what we'd planned):
  - The tool borrows the brand; @sharpee/sharpee keeps a thin dispatcher bin into
  devkit.
  - More "branded," but it re-overloads sharpee across library + command — the
  confusion you just named.

  I'd flip back to devkit — it's the literal reading of "distinct execution tool,"
  gives the cleanest library-vs-tool split, and is less work (no rename). That
  reverses the sharpee-command call from a few turns ago, which is why I'm surfacing
  it instead of assuming.

  Which do you want — devkit (distinct) or sharpee (branded)? Once you pick, I'll fold
  it into the plan + the amendment checklist (the checklist gets much shorter if it's
  devkit), and then we're ready to implement.

✻ Baked for 1m 22s

❯ that's not what I ws talking about. the idea of npm install, npx do-stuff (baked    
  into packaging) OR npm install and sharpee do-stuff, to me is a big difference      
  
⏺ Ah — I misread that. You're not talking about internal naming; you're talking about
  the author's invocation model, and you're right that it's a big difference:

  - npm install → npx @sharpee/sharpee build — execution is "baked into the package,"
  invoked through the package-runner every time. No distinct tool; you're npx-ing the
  library. (This is what the website getting-started currently shows.)
  - npm install → sharpee build — a distinct sharpee command you just run, like any
  real CLI.

  You want the second. That's the "distinct execution tool" — the thing you type is
  sharpee, not "npx the package." Agreed it's a meaningfully better author experience,
  and it also un-confuses the two roles: @sharpee/sharpee is the thing you
  install/import, sharpee is the thing you run.

  The one real consequence to settle is how a bare sharpee command gets onto an 
  author's PATH, because that's the difference between the two workflows mechanically:

  - A normal project-local npm install @sharpee/sharpee only puts sharpee in
  node_modules/.bin — which works inside npm run/pnpm scripts, but a bare sharpee 
  build in the shell still needs .bin on PATH. That quietly slides back toward the npx
  feel.
  - To genuinely type sharpee build anywhere, it's a global install (npm i -g 
  @sharpee/sharpee → sharpee on PATH) or a DevArch-style installer (sharpee install
  that puts the command on PATH). DevArch does exactly this — devarch is a real global
  command, not npx.

  So the direction I'd confirm: author flow = npm i -g @sharpee/sharpee (or an 
  installer), then sharpee init my-game / sharpee build / sharpee test as a
  first-class command — and the website docs drop the npx @sharpee/sharpee … form. The
  command is sharpee (your earlier call stands; my detour to devkit was off-base).
  Internally it's still the devkit engine — implementation detail.

  Which delivery do you want for the bare command — global install (npm i -g,
  simplest, DevArch-like), an installer (sharpee install, most control over PATH), or
  are you fine with project-local + sharpee via package scripts (no bare shell
  command, but docs say sharpee build because it runs through the project's scripts)?
  That's the piece that actually decides Workflow-2-for-real, and then I'll bake it
  into the plan.

✻ Crunched for 1m 32s
  
❯ we could follow the Claude CLI model                                                

⏺ That's a clean answer, and it ties the whole thread together. The Claude CLI model
  is:

  - A distinct, globally-installed CLI — npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code (or
  a native installer script), then you run bare claude anywhere. Install once, run by
  name — exactly your "npm install → sharpee do-stuff," never npx.
  - Separate from the SDK library. The CLI (@anthropic-ai/claude-code) is a different 
  package from the importable SDK (@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk). That's precisely
  the "npm package vs execution role" split you wanted — the thing you install to run
  is not the thing you import.
  - Self-contained + self-updating (claude can update itself), so it's a first-class
  tool, not a per-project bin.

  Mapped onto Sharpee:
  
  - Library / SDK: @sharpee/sharpee — what a story project depends on and imports. It
  drops the CLI bin entirely; pure library.
  - Execution tool: a distinct, globally-installable package providing the sharpee
  command (wrapping the devkit engine + commands). npm i -g … (or an installer) →
  sharpee init my-game, sharpee build, sharpee test anywhere. No npx, no reliance on a
  project-local .bin.
  - Repo authors use the same global sharpee (location-aware: monorepo vs standalone),
  or a repo-linked dev build.

  The notable consequence for the record: this rewrites ADR-180 Decision 1. Decision 1
  said devkit ships via the @sharpee/sharpee dependency so the bin appears on a local
  install. The Claude model says the opposite — the CLI is its own globally-installed
  package, not a transitive bin of the library. That's cleaner (no umbrella coupling,
  no build-tooling deps dragged into the SDK) but it's a real amendment, bigger than
  the rename.

  One sub-choice it raises (the Claude model allows package-name ≠ command, like
  claude-code→claude): the global-install target can either be the existing 
  @sharpee/devkit (npm i -g @sharpee/devkit → sharpee command — reuses the merged
  package, one package) or a dedicated @sharpee/cli (cleaner "install this to get
  sharpee," but a new package). I lean toward reusing @sharpee/devkit as the install
  target with bin sharpee — one package, mirrors how claude-code provides claude.

  So, to confirm the direction before I fold it into the plan + the Decision-1
  amendment:
  - sharpee = a distinct, globally-installed CLI (npm -g and/or installer), not
  shipped via @sharpee/sharpee; @sharpee/sharpee becomes a pure library; docs drop
  npx.
  - Global-install target: reuse @sharpee/devkit (bin sharpee) vs new @sharpee/cli —
  which do you want?

  That, plus a "should sharpee self-update like claude?" question for later, and I'll
  rewrite the plan around the global-CLI model. Still no code.

⏺ Plan updated and now internally consistent. The architecture is fully settled:

  Delivery: distinct, globally-installed CLI (Claude Code model) — npm i -g 
  @sharpee/devkit (or an installer) → bare sharpee command, run anywhere. No npx.

  Packages — clean role split:
  - @sharpee/sharpee = pure library (import it; drop its CLI bin).
  - @sharpee/devkit = the CLI tool (reused), bin sharpee — package-name≠command, like
  claude-code→claude. Holds the engine + all commands
  (build/test/bundle/browser/.sharpee/scaffold/clean/verify/register/list/ifid).
  - Reverse the 3d umbrella wiring (@sharpee/sharpee→@sharpee/devkit dep removed); CLI
  installs separately, not as a transitive bin. One-way dep, no cycle.

  Command: sharpee (subcommands), ./sharpee wrapper for the repo, location-aware
  (monorepo vs standalone). init=scaffold, register/list=location registry.
  Self-update deferred.

  Phasing: U1 (distinct CLI + bin rename + drop sharpee CLI bin + remove umbrella dep
  + ./sharpee wrapper + in-repo path support + doc re-sweep dropping npx) → U2
  (standalone build mode + move packages/sharpee/src/cli/* into devkit, restoring
  .sharpee) → U3 (scaffolding + register/list). The ADR-180 amendment (now a
  Decision-1 reversal, not just a rename) lands with U1.

      128 +export interface ResolvedStory {                                    
      129 +  /** Story slug (directory basename). */                           
      130 +  name: string;                                                     
      131 +  /** Absolute story directory. */                                  
      132 +  dir: string;                                                      
      133 +  /** Workspace package name if the story is an in-repo workspace st
          +ory; else null. */                                                  
      134 +  pkg: string | null;                                               
      135 +  /** True iff dir is a direct child of <root>/stories or <root>/tut
          +orials. */                                                          
      136 +  inRepo: boolean;                                                  
      137 +  /** True iff dir is under <root>/stories (build.sh stamps version.
          +ts only for these). */                                              
      138 +  underStories: boolean;                                            
      139 +}                                                                   
      140 +                                                                    
      141 +function classifyStory(root: string, dir: string): ResolvedStory {  
      150 +      ? `@sharpee/tutorial-${name}`                                 
      151 +      : null;                                                       
      152 +  return { name, dir, pkg, inRepo, underStories };                  
      153 +}                                                                   
      154 +                                                                    
      155 +/**                                                                 
      156 + * Resolve a story given either a **path** (a directory with a packa
          +ge.json, tried                                                      
      157 + * relative to cwd then to root) or a bare **name** (stories/<name> 
          +then tutorials/<name>).                                             
      158 + * Returns null if neither resolves. This is the single resolver `bu
          +ild` + `stampVersions`                                              
      159 + * share, so path and name forms behave identically (ADR-180 Decisio
          +n 4: a story is a location).                                        
      160 + */                                                                 
      161 +export function resolveStory(root: string, nameOrPath: string): Reso
          +lvedStory | null {                                                  
      162 +  for (const dir of [resolve(nameOrPath), resolve(root, nameOrPath)]
          +) {                                                                 
      163 +    if (existsSync(dir) && existsSync(join(dir, 'package.json'))) re
          +turn classifyStory(root, dir);                                      
      164 +  }                                                                 
      165 +  const byName = resolveStoryDir(root, nameOrPath);                 
      166 +  return byName ? classifyStory(root, byName) : null;               
      167 +}                                                                   
      168 +                                                                    
      169  /** Read a package.json's `version` field. */
      170  export function readVersion(pkgJsonPath: string): string {
      171    return JSON.parse(readFileSync(pkgJsonPath, 'utf8')).version;

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee; gh pr merge 126 --merge --delete-branch 2>&1 |
      tail -2; git checkout main -q && git pull -q 2>&1 | tail -1; git checkout -b

⏺ Merged; on the U2 branch. The CLI to absorb is ~1055 lines across 6 files, and its
  only @sharpee/* import is @sharpee/core (one spot) — so moving it into devkit adds
  just a @sharpee/core dep (a leaf, no cycle), and nothing imports @sharpee/sharpee.
  Good — the move is clean dependency-wise.

  But U2 has one genuine design fork I want to lock before I move ~1000 lines of
  cross-package code (this is a published-package change, so confirming the shape
  first):

  The build verb serves two different jobs. devkit's current build is monorepo
  (platform packages + bundle + story via pnpm --filter). The sharpee CLI's build is
  standalone (compile the author's story + emit .sharpee + browser). U2 unifies them
  into one location-aware build:
  - In the monorepo → today's devkit build [story] (platform + bundle + story).
  - In a standalone author project → compile the author's story via its own toolchain
  + emit browser/.sharpee (≈ today's sharpee build).

  My proposed command shape after the move:
  - sharpee build [story|path] — location-aware as above.
  - sharpee build --browser — the browser client in either mode (fold the standalone
  build-browser into --browser; keep build-browser as a deprecated alias).
  - sharpee bundle:story — the .sharpee bundle (both modes; restores it).
  - sharpee init / init-browser / ifid — scaffolding/utility, moved as-is.

  Sequence once that's confirmed: add @sharpee/core to devkit deps → move
  packages/sharpee/src/cli/* into @sharpee/devkit and wire into its dispatcher
  (location-aware build) → rename devkit bin devkit→sharpee + atomically drop
  @sharpee/sharpee's bin → global-install + website-docs sweep (drop npx) → ADR-180
  Amendment 1 (the checklist) → tests (standalone build real-path, bin presence,
  command dispatch).

  Is that the build split you want (monorepo vs standalone, one location-aware verb;
  --browser unified; bundle:story for .sharpee)? Confirm and I'll execute the full U2.

✻ Cooked for 1m 38s

         +the clauses                                                          
      10 +tagged `(amended — see Amendment 1)` below. New state:               
      11 +                                                                     
      12 +- **One command, two depths.** The installed author and the repo main
         +tainer run the                                                       
      13 +  **same** CLI; the maintainer just has extra steps. The user-facing 
         +command is                                                           
      14 +  **`sharpee`** (subcommands), location-aware: inside the monorepo it
         + builds platform +                                                   
      15 +  bundle + in-repo stories; in a standalone author project it builds 
         +*that* project's                                                     
      16 +  story via its own toolchain (+ `.sharpee` + browser).              
      17 +- **Delivery = a distinct, globally-installed CLI (the Claude Code mo
         +del).** `@sharpee/devkit`                                            
      18 +  (the engine) exposes bin **`sharpee`**; authors `npm i -g @sharpee/
         +devkit` (and/or an                                                   
      19 +  installer) and run bare `sharpee` — **not** `npx @sharpee/sharpee …
         +`. This **reverses**                                                 
      20 +  Decision 1's "ships via the `@sharpee/sharpee` dependency / bin on 
         +local install": the                                                  
      21 +  CLI is installed separately, NOT a transitive bin of the library. `
         +@sharpee/sharpee` is a                                               
      22 +  **pure library** (its CLI bin is dropped; `src/cli/*` moved into `@
         +sharpee/devkit`).                                                    
      23 +  Package-name ≠ command (like `claude-code`→`claude`). devkit must n
         +ot depend on                                                         
      24 +  `@sharpee/sharpee` (one-way; no cycle). In-repo, a committed `./sha
         +rpee` wrapper is the entry.                                          
      25 +- **`init` = scaffold a project** (from the absorbed sharpee CLI). Th
         +e name→path location                                                 
      26 +  registry verb is **`register`** (+ `list`) — *not* `init`.

         +bundle:story | bundle |                                              
      28 +  test | test:npm | play | clean | verify | init | init-browser | ifi
         +d | register | list`.

         +-filter`; mode detected

         +e `build` (and a

         +.

⏺ U2 shipped — PR #127: https://github.com/ChicagoDave/sharpee/pull/127

  Phase U2 done — the unification

  The big one. sharpee is now one command with two depths, delivered as a distinct
  global CLI (Claude model).

  - Bin handoff: @sharpee/devkit bin devkit→sharpee; @sharpee/sharpee's CLI bin
  dropped → it's a pure library now (builds clean, verified no bin collision). This
  reverses ADR-180 Decision 1 — the CLI is a distinct npm i -g @sharpee/devkit, not a
  transitive bin.
  - Absorbed the sharpee CLI (~1000 lines, git-mv preserving history):
  init/init-browser/build/build-browser/ifid → packages/devkit/src/standalone/. devkit
  gained @sharpee/core/transcript-tester/fflate (one-way, no cycle).
  - Location-aware build: detectMode() → monorepo (platform+bundle+story) vs
  standalone (author's own toolchain → .sharpee+browser). Resolves ADR-180 Open Q1 and
  restores .sharpee.
  - ADR-180 Amendment 1 recorded (+ inline clause tags) so code and record don't
  diverge.
  - Website docs swept off npx @sharpee/sharpee to the global sharpee command.
  - 25 tests pass. Smoked sharpee help/ifid/standalone-routing; sharpee builds as a
  pure library.

  One honest gap I flagged in the PR: I verified dispatch + module-load + that the
  moved code is the previously-shipped sharpee CLI, but I have not run a full
  standalone sharpee build end-to-end (out-of-repo project → .sharpee). That real-path
  test is the right gate before calling the standalone path proven — I'd recommend a
  standalone-build harness as a follow-up (it pairs naturally with U3).

  U3 remaining: sharpee register/list + the ~/.sharpee/devkit registry (ADR AC-3), a
  dedicated bundle:story verb, and optionally sharpee update.

  Want me to merge #127 and build that standalone-build verification harness (+ start
  U3), or pause here? Given the flagged e2e gap, I'd lean toward landing the
  standalone harness before U3 so the unified build is actually proven on the
  standalone path.

  The complete arc landed this session:

  ┌──────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────┐
  │  Track   │                           Phases                           │ Status │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤

  │          │ global CLI), U3 (registry + standalone harness)            │        │
  └──────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────┘

  End state: sharpee is the single, location-aware build/test/scaffold CLI
  (@sharpee/devkit engine), delivered as a distinct global install (Claude model);
  @sharpee/sharpee is a pure library; build.sh is gone; the standalone path is proven
  e2e; ADR-180 Amendment 1 records it all.

  Verification results

  Real publish set = 27 packages, all at 1.0.0 — the intersection of "in
  ts-forge.config.json projects" AND "has publishConfig". This is the key thing: tsf
  gates publishing on both being in the build context and having publishConfig.
  They're now aligned.

  - ✅ @sharpee/devkit → in context, publishConfig:{access:public}, 1.0.0 — first-time
  publish will work.
  - ✅ @sharpee/sharpee → 1.0.0 (pure library now).
  - ✅ Stories/tutorials correctly excluded (story-dungeo, tutorial-familyzoo, etc.
  have no publishConfig).
  - ✅ interpreter is built but not published (no publishConfig) — internal, correct.
  - ✅ No manual --access edits needed — tsf hardcodes npm publish --access public
  (publish.ts:199). Good that you stopped me before I touched publishConfig.access.
  - ⚠️  Two orphans have publishConfig but are not in the build context, so tsf can't
  publish them — but they're version-stale: @sharpee/core-extensions (1.0.0-alpha.1)
  and @sharpee/extension-conversation (0.9.112). Not a blocker for this release; just
  noting they're out of sync if you ever add them to the config.

  tsf also self-protects: it aborts publish if any staged manifest still contains a
  workspace: protocol (publish.ts:152-173).

  ---
  1.0.0 publish runbook

  0. Commit the version bump first (currently staged, not committed)
  git commit -m "chore(release): 1.0.0"

  1. Authenticate (you noted this)
  npm login
  npm whoami        # tsf aborts publish if this fails (publish.ts:132)
  First-time @sharpee/devkit publish + 2FA: keep your OTP handy — tsf runs npm publish
  with inherited stdio, so the OTP prompt passes through interactively.

  2. Build to the npm staging dir (~/.tsf-publish/)
  tsf build --npm

  3. Dry-run and read the list before committing to the registry
  tsf publish --dry-run
  Confirm: 27 packages, every line @1.0.0, and @sharpee/devkit is present. (Dry-run
  skips the npm whoami check.)

  4. Publish for real — publishes in dependency order, --access public automatic
  tsf publish              # defaults to --tag latest
  If it fails partway (e.g., an OTP timeout), re-run with --changed to resume only
  what isn't yet on the registry at 1.0.0:
  tsf publish --changed

  5. Verify the registry
  npm view @sharpee/devkit version     # → 1.0.0 (was 404)
  npm view @sharpee/sharpee version    # → 1.0.0

  6. Tag the release
  git tag v1.0.0
  git push && git push --tags

  One decision for you: do you want to delete or version-align the two orphans
  (core-extensions 1.0.0-alpha.1, extension-conversation 0.9.112) before tagging, or
  leave them as-is? They won't publish either way — purely a tidiness call.

❯ do I need to worry about this: npm warn Unknown project config                      
  "public-hoist-pattern". This will stop working in the next major version of npm.    
  See `npm help npmrc` for supported config options.                                  
  
⏺ Short answer: no, it won't affect your publish — but it's worth a 2-minute fix. Let
  me find where it's set.

  Searched for 1 pattern, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ No, you don't need to worry about it for the 1.0.0 publish — it's cosmetic and won't
  affect anything.

  Here's what's happening:

  - public-hoist-pattern[] is a pnpm-specific setting. It lives in the repo's .npmrc
  (lines 7-8) so pnpm can flat-hoist @sharpee/* and lz-string for the dev workspace /
  bundle-cache resolution (ADR-178).
  - Both pnpm and npm read .npmrc. pnpm understands public-hoist-pattern; npm doesn't,
  so every time tsf shells out to npm pack / npm publish, npm prints that warning and
  ignores the key. It does not change what gets packed or published.
  - The "will stop working in the next major version of npm" part is about npm
  rejecting unknown keys — but this key was never for npm. pnpm reads .npmrc itself,
  so npm tightening up doesn't break your pnpm behavior. Worst case, future npm errors
  instead of warns on the unknown key, which is why it's worth tidying eventually.

  Optional cleanup (not now): You're on pnpm@10.13.1, and pnpm 10 supports these
  settings in pnpm-workspace.yaml instead of .npmrc. Moving them there gets pnpm to
  keep hoisting while npm never sees the key — warning gone:

  # pnpm-workspace.yaml
  publicHoistPattern:
    - '@sharpee/*'
    - 'lz-string'
  ...and delete lines 7-8 from .npmrc.
  
  That touches workspace config (platform-level), so I'd want your go-ahead before
  doing it — and I'd keep it out of the 1.0.0 release commit either way. For the
  publish itself: ignore the warning and proceed.

     … +40 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The processes are dead and storage is stable (48 GB free on boot, 887 GB external).
  And the log just told us exactly what's wrong.

  This is a real, reproducible bug — and it's nasty. Look at the tail: every other
  suite passed, then:

  Test Suite 'WorkspaceRootTests' started at 2026-06-18 19:05:56.522.
  Test Case '-[...testIgnoresDirectoryNamedBuildSh]' started.
  ** BUILD INTERRUPTED **   ← my kill

  testIgnoresDirectoryNamedBuildSh started and hung — same case that stalled the May
  run. "Pushing storage + non-responsive" is the signature of an infinite loop in 
  WorkspaceRoot.find() that balloons memory until macOS swaps to disk. My strong
  suspicion: the walk-to-root never terminates because deletingLastPathComponent() at
  the filesystem root yields a growing /../../.. path instead of a fixpoint, so the
  parent.path == current.path guard never fires. The tests that pass are exactly the
  ones where build.sh is found early (return before reaching root); the two that hang
  (testIgnoresDirectoryNamedBuildSh, testReturnsNilWhenNoAncestor) are exactly the
  ones that walk all the way up.

  Let me prove it safely with a bounded probe (hard iteration cap — no hang risk)
  before touching any code:

⏺ Bash(cat > /tmp/probe.swift <<'EOF'
      import Foundation…)
  ⎿  DID NOT CONVERGE in 40 iters — INFINITE LOOP CONFIRMED
     current=/../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../.
     ./../../..  parent=/../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../..
     … +20 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Confirmed — infinite loop, root cause nailed. The probe never converges:
  deletingLastPathComponent() on a directory-style file URL (trailing slash, which is
  how both the test's tempDir and a real NSOpenPanel folder selection are built) keeps
  prepending /../ forever once it walks past root. .path grows without bound, so the
  parent.path == current.path fixpoint guard never fires. The ever-growing strings
  balloon memory → macOS swaps to disk → "pushing storage" and non-responsive. Exactly
  your symptom.

  This is the original May bug, and it's worse than a test problem:

  - It's a real production hang. AppDelegate calls WorkspaceRoot.find(from: 
  projectURL) on every project load. If you open any folder not inside a Sharpee 
  workspace — a documented, expected case (the "not inside a Sharpee workspace" alert)
  — the real IDE hangs and eats your disk, exactly like the test did.
  - testTerminatesAtFilesystemRoot passed because it uses URL(fileURLWithPath: "/")
  (no trailing slash, converges); the two hanging tests build trailing-slash dir URLs
  that walk to root.

  The fix (in WorkspaceRoot.find): stop relying on the unreliable parent-fixpoint and
  instead bail explicitly when we reach root — check current.path == "/" after testing
  for build.sh there, before descending again. The walk naturally passes through a
  URL with path exactly "/" (e.g. file:///private/ → file:///) before it can ever
  produce /.., so this terminates cleanly and still checks /build.sh:

  while true {
      let candidate = current.appendingPathComponent(marker)
      var isDir: ObjCBool = false
      if fm.fileExists(atPath: candidate.path, isDirectory: &isDir), !isDir.boolValue
  {
          return current
      }
      if current.path == "/" { return nil }   // reached root, no marker — stop
      current = current.deletingLastPathComponent()
  }

  That's a one-function change in tools/ide/SharpeeIDE/Workspace/WorkspaceRoot.swift
  (IDE-only, not platform).

  Given how storage-sensitive the builds have been, I'd rather not kick off another
  full xcodebuild test without your say-so. Two options:

  1. I apply the fix, then you run the tests in the Xcode GUI (DerivedData already on
  the external drive) — no CLI build pressure from me.
  2. I apply the fix and run the CLI test once more (now safe — the loop is gone, so
  it won't balloon).

  Want me to apply the fix? And if so, which way do you want to verify?
