⏺ Neon is the riskiest integration (untagged since 0.6.0, plus a potential
  SwiftTreeSitter-version conflict with our pinned 0.10.0). Per the no-retry rule,
  I'll research the exact API and version constraints before touching project.yml.

⏺ Fetch(https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ChimeHQ/Neon/main/Package.swift)
  ⎿  Received 1.3KB (200 OK)

⏺ Fetch(https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ChimeHQ/Neon/main/README.md)
  ⎿  Received 11.2KB (200 OK)

⏺ Before touching project.yml, the research surfaced a real conflict that changes the
  cost of this increment — worth flagging rather than thrashing on builds.

  The snag: a SwiftTreeSitter version conflict

  Neon (main) declares its dependency as SwiftTreeSitter from ChimeHQ/SwiftTreeSitter,
  branch main. We currently pin tree-sitter/swift-tree-sitter at exact 0.10.0. Two
  problems:

  1. Pin conflict. A package can resolve to only one version in the graph. Neon wants
  SwiftTreeSitter branch: main; we want exactVersion: 0.10.0. SPM can't satisfy both —
  to add Neon I'd have to move our pin to branch: main, then re-verify the working 
  SyntaxHighlighter (Query / LanguageConfiguration / resolve-highlights) against main,
  which may differ from 0.10.0.
  2. Reproducibility. Neon is untagged since 0.6.0 and pulls SwiftTreeSitter from main
  — so adopting it means two branch-pinned deps, i.e. non-reproducible builds, until
  ChimeHQ tags again.

  Plus Neon's TextViewHighlighter API isn't pinned down in the docs I can reach — so
  I'd be integrating partly blind, which is exactly what the no-retry rule penalizes.

  Is Neon even worth it here?

  Neon's payoff is incremental re-parse + viewport-limited styling — valuable for huge
  files. But tree-sitter is built to parse on every keystroke; for typical IF story
  files our current full re-parse is effectively instant. So the heavy, conflict-prone
  integration buys little for this codebase right now.

  Options

  - Defer Neon (recommended). Commit the clean A+B+C batch. Revisit Neon only if
  per-edit re-parse is a measured problem — by then ChimeHQ may have tagged a release
  that pins a stable SwiftTreeSitter.
  - Lightweight incremental, no Neon. Get most of the benefit cheaply:
  debounce/coalesce re-highlight (and/or run it off the main thread) so large files
  never stutter — no dependency conflict, low risk. ~1 small increment.
  - Full Neon anyway. Move SwiftTreeSitter to branch: main, re-verify the highlighter,
  integrate TextViewHighlighter. Highest risk/cost; accepts non-reproducible builds.

  I stopped short of editing anything — nothing changed on the tree since the last
  green run. Given the conflict, my honest read is the Neon payoff doesn't justify the
  cost on this codebase yet. How do you want to proceed?

⏺ User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · How should I handle the incremental-re-highlight work given the Neon version 
     conflict? → Defer Neon, commit batch

⏺ Deferring Neon and committing the batch. First, two doc updates so the version
  conflict is recorded (a future session shouldn't rediscover it): the plan's 2.3 note
  and an ADR consequence.

      55  | 2.4 ✅ | Bracket matching — partner highlight at the caret | **DONE
          ** — `BracketMatcher` (balanced char scan); tree-aware string/comment
           skipping is a follow-up |
      56  | 2.5 ✅ | Auto-indent on newline — carry leading whitespace + one le
          vel after `{`/`(`/`[` | **DONE** — `AutoIndenter` via `doCommandBy` |
      57  | 2.6 ✅ | TS-family gating; non-TS files render plain | **DONE** — `
          canHighlight` (ts/tsx/mts/cts) |

  - browser-entry.ts author.join(', ') — legitimate (formatting an author list).
  Leave.
  - .becomes(...) in npcs/index.ts (10 calls) — that's the known ResponseChainBuilder
  API drift, already tracked in issue #130. Needs the broader @sharpee/character
  audit, not a spot fix.

  intentional multi-line readable content (a letter, a theatre program) where the
  newlines are real structure — salutation, blank lines, distinct program lines.
  .join('\n') is a defensible way to write that, and converting it would change
  rendered text, so I left them.

  that renders as a hard line break to the player:

  I write to inform you of my decision regarding the estate. The property

     # ADR-079: Dungeon Text Alignment

⏺ You're right — it's a real gap. Here's the lay of the land and the design tension,
  then how I'd proceed (no code — this is platform territory that needs an ADR first).

  What exists today

  Sharpee has a decoration model (ADR-174): authors write [name:content] in any prose,
  the prose-pipeline parses it, and it emits a sharpee-{name} CSS class on the wire.
  The closed platform vocabulary is inline character styling only:
  - switches: em strong u st code super sub
  - IF-semantic: item npc room direction command quote
  - discrete classes: color-*, bgcolor-*, size-small/large, font-mono

  Two deliberate constraints matter here (ADR-174, verbatim): "everything is a class"
  — no parameterized decorations — and no inline styles on the wire (mirrors
  HTML+CSS). Parameterized concerns are expressed as discrete classes (color-red, not
  color:#f00).

  So: layout/block macros — newline, indent, center — are not defined anywhere. You're
  correct. (\n literally passes through, which is why the letter renders its line
  breaks; that's the only "macro" today, and it's accidental.)

  Your three macros vs the current model

  ┌─────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Macro    │                               Fit                               │
  ├─────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │             │ Doesn't fit [name:content] (that syntax needs content + a       │
  │ newline     │ colon). A void macro like [br] is a new syntax need. \n works   │
  │             │ but is implicit.                                                │
  ├─────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ indent      │ Fits cleanly as discrete classes — [indent:…] or levels         │
  │             │ indent-1/2/3. Block-ish, but addable.                           │
  ├─────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ center (no  │ Fits as a discrete class [center:…].                            │
  │ param)      │                                                                 │
  ├─────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ center @ %  │ Conflicts with "everything is a class" — 50% is an arbitrary    │
  │ of width    │ parameter a CSS class can't carry. This is the real decision.   │
  └─────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The crux

  center @ 50% forces a choice ADR-174 explicitly closed off:

  - Option A — stay class-only. Offer preset widths as discrete classes: center,
  center-narrow, center-wide. No arbitrary %. Cheapest; fully consistent with ADR-174;
  loses fine control.
  - Option B — introduce parameterized decorations. Extend the wire so a decoration
  can carry a value ([center:50:…] → wire carries {name:center, value:50}, client maps
  to layout). More expressive; reopens ADR-174's no-params decision; but arguably
  more consistent with the channels philosophy in your own notes — "wire is data-only,
  the client decides rendering" ([[project_decoration_model]],
  [[project_channels_universal_ui_surface]]). A numeric value is data; the class-only
  rule was the stricter stance.

  There's also a block-vs-inline wrinkle: today the pipeline produces inline-decorated
  ITextBlocks (ADR-133). center/indent are block-layout, so they may need block-level
  structure, not just a span class — that's the deeper part of the design.

  My read

  This warrants a small ADR ("Author layout macros / parameterized decorations")
  rather than an ad-hoc add, because it (a) reopens ADR-174's no-parameters decision
  and (b) introduces the first block-layout decorations. I'd lean Option B — it's the
  more honest fit with the data-only channel model and it future-proofs (tables,
  columns, rules will hit the same wall) — but it's the bigger commitment.

  How do you want to handle it? Options: I draft the ADR (lay out A vs B fully,
  syntax, wire shape, block-vs-inline, client contract) for your decision; or just
  capture it as a GitHub issue for the backlog; or talk it through more first. I
  haven't touched anything.

  it's for your acceptance). I'll lay out A vs B fully with a recommendation, concrete
  syntax/wire/contract, and the bits the /adr-review checklist looks for (affected
  packages, tests, acceptance criteria).

⏺ Drafted docs/architecture/adrs/adr-183-author-layout-macros.md (Status: PROPOSED).

  It's structured for your decision and to survive /adr-review:

  - The crux up front — two of your three macros (newline, indent) fit ADR-174; center
  @ % doesn't, because an arbitrary width can't be a fixed class. That's what forces
  an ADR.
  - Recommended decision (Option B) — parameterized, block-aware decorations, three
  additive pieces:
    a. Vocabulary entries gain metadata (display: inline|block, void, param).
    b. Syntax: optional [name=value:content] (param parsed only before the first :, so
  content keeps its colons) + void [br/] (and \n stays equivalent).
    c. Wire: IDecoration.value emitted as data-sharpee-value, never an inline style —
  so it honors ADR-174's no-inline-styles rule while carrying the % as data, which is
  what the channel model says the wire should do.
  - New names: br (inline/void), indent, center (optional % param), right.
  - Alternatives: A (class-only presets — cheaper, no arbitrary %, hits the wall again
  on tables/columns) and C (inline styles — rejected). With the rationale for B over
  A.
  - Contracts/tests/acceptance — affected packages (engine prose-pipeline,
  channel-service, text-blocks, platform CSS), end-to-end + negative + backward-compat
  tests, and acceptance criteria; the Alderman letter/program flagged as natural
  fixtures.

  It's uncommitted. Worth noting the honest tension it surfaces: B reopens ADR-174's 
  "everything is a class" decision — deliberately and narrowly (optional params,
  value-as-data). If you'd rather not reopen that, Option A keeps ADR-174 intact at
  the cost of arbitrary widths.

  Want me to run /adr-review 183 on it, commit it, or adjust the A-vs-B framing first?

❯ well let's go through my thinking: the author has a poem to display centered in     
  talics and it has short lined verses                                                
  
⏺ Good example — a poem stresses the model in exactly the right place. Let me work it
  through.

  The poem in the ADR as currently drafted

  centered + italic + line breaks means nesting a block, an inline, and explicit
  breaks:

  [center=60:[em:Roses are red,[br/]Violets are blue,[br/]Sugar is sweet,[br/]And so
  are you.]]

  center=60 → a 60%-wide column, horizontally centered, text-align:center so each
  short verse line centers within it; em → italic across the whole thing; [br/] → the
  line breaks. That works… but it's ugly, and the poem fights you in two ways:

  What the example exposes

  1. Newlines are the real problem, not centering. An author writing a poem will type
  it with actual line breaks. But what does a literal \n inside the text mean? Today
  it's accidental: a terminal client prints it; an HTML client collapses it to a
  space. So the author either gets inconsistent results or has to litter [br/] on
  every line. The poem makes the unavoidable question concrete: we have to define 
  newline semantics, not just add a center macro.

  2. Stanzas. "Short lined verses" implies a blank line between stanzas — another
  structural break the author wants to express naturally, not as [br/][br/].

  The refinement I'd make

  Define that block decorations preserve their internal line structure (source newline
  → line break; blank line → stanza gap) — i.e. a block behaves like white-space: 
  pre-line rather than HTML's collapse. Then the poem reads like a poem:

  [center=60:[em:
  Roses are red,
  Violets are blue,

  Sugar is sweet,
  And so are you.
  ]]

  No [br/] spam, blank line = stanza, italic + centered 60% column. [br/] still exists
  for a one-off break inside a normal (collapsing) paragraph, but you don't reach for
  it inside a verse block.

  That pushes one decision up to the surface, and it's yours to make: what are the 
  default newline semantics of prose?
  - Collapse by default (HTML-like), and block decorations like center/verse opt into
  preserve. Safe for normal descriptions; poems use a block.
  - Preserve by default (terminal/IF-like), and we never worry about \n — but then web
  descriptions need authors to be deliberate about wrapping (the very thing that bit
  The Alderman rooms).

  There's also a naming question the poem raises: is the right block here center, or a
  dedicated verse/poem block (preserves lines, centered or not) that's about the
  structure, with center=%/em as styling on top? A verse block separates "this is
  line-structured content" from "center it / italicize it."
         +plicit-only line breaks**.

         +espace rule is the one                                               
      35 +behavior change (see Consequences).

      37 +### 0. Newlines are explicit; source whitespace is not significant   
      38 +                                                                     
      39 +The **only** way to produce a line break is the `[br]` macro. Source 
         +whitespace — spaces, tabs,                                           
      40 +and literal `\n` — is collapsed HTML-style: any run of whitespace bec
         +omes a single space, and                                             
      41 +leading/trailing whitespace is trimmed per block. A literal `\n` ther
         +efore renders as a space,                                            
      42 +never a break.                                                       
      43 +                                                                     
      44 +This means authors may wrap source however they like (across array fr
         +agments, a long string, or                                           
      45 +a multi-line template) with identical output — the class of bug that 
         +produced The Alderman's

  are you.]]

  by [br][br].

  Two things this settles, worth noting:
  - The Alderman letter/program are now clearly "wrong" — their \n breaks will
  collapse to spaces, so they must be rewritten with [br]. That's the real answer to
  the earlier "fix the letter body" question: under this model it's not a judgment
  call, it's required. (The rooms cleanup already conforms — single-paragraph prose.)
  - Stanza ergonomics: a long poem means a lot of [br]. You chose explicit-only, so
  [br][br] for stanza gaps is the cost — no verse/preserve-newlines block. Fine by me;
  just flagging it's the trade you're accepting.

⏺ Added. The vocabulary now has a clean void pair:

  - [br] → single line break (<br class="sharpee-br">)
  - [p] → paragraph gap (client maps .sharpee-p to the blank-line spacing)

  Poem with stanzas reads better now:

  [center=60:[em:Roses are red,[br]Violets are blue,[p]Sugar is sweet,[br]And so are
  you.]]

  One small semantic to keep in mind (no action needed unless you disagree): I've
  defined [p] as a void separator (like [br], just bigger), not a wrapping paragraph
  element (<p>…</p>). That matches your "explicit token" framing and keeps it simple —
  the only consequence is a leading/trailing [p] is a no-op (nothing to separate),
  same as trimming. If you'd ever want true semantic <p> wrapping (for
  accessibility/CSS), that'd be a [p:content] block instead — but the void separator
  is the lighter, consistent choice and I've gone with it.

  The model now: [br]/[p] (void breaks), em/strong/… (inline), center=%/indent=n/right
  (block, parameterized), explicit-only whitespace. Keep refining, or is it settled
  enough to commit ADR-183 and run /adr-review?

⏺ Reviewed ADR-183, and I read the existing decoration code it names (text-blocks
  types, resolver.ts, platform-vocabulary.ts) to check the contracts against reality.
  That surfaced two real gaps.

  ADR Review: ADR-183 — Author layout macros (parameterized & block decorations)

  Address before implementation:
  1. Put `display` (inline|block) + a void flag on the wire decoration, OR specify how
     channel-service learns it. Today the wire carries only className+content; adding `value`
     alone is insufficient — the renderer can't choose div/span/br. Define the
     pipeline → render-to-string contract (likely: IDecoration gains `display`/`void`, or the
     renderer imports the vocabulary metadata — note that crosses the current engine-internal
     boundary).
  2. Specify the author-name (non-platform) interaction. resolver.ts passes unknown names through
     as verbatim classes. Decide: can authors define void/param decorations? If an author writes
     [mybox=3:…], what attribute carries `3` (data-value? disallowed)? Void author macros
     ([mything] with no colon) — supported or platform-only? State it; otherwise it's an
     ADR-143-style silent gap.
  3. Once 1–2 are settled, add boundary tests for block-vs-inline rendering and
     author-vs-platform-name handling.

  Implementation Audit
    (no new code yet — ADR is PROPOSED/pre-implementation)
    text-blocks/types.ts:     INFORMS FAIL — IDecoration/TextContent are canonical here; ADR
                              correctly targets this file, but only plans a `value` add. Needs
                              display/void too (finding 1).
    engine/.../resolver.ts:   INFORMS FAIL — author names pass through verbatim as classes; ADR
                              doesn't reconcile param/void with this pass-through (finding 2).
    engine/.../platform-vocabulary.ts: MATCHES — ADR's entries-with-metadata is the right place;
                              note it's engine-internal, which collides with the renderer needing
                              it (finding 1/boundary).

  Bottom line: 9/12, NEEDS WORK — the design is sound and well-specified on syntax/whitespace/tests, but it
  under-specifies how block-vs-inline and the param value actually reach the renderer (the wire decoration is
  className+content today), and how the new mechanism interacts with author-defined class names (the
  resolver passes them through). Both are the silent-gap class this review exists to catch — worth closing
  before any code.

  Want me to revise ADR-183 to resolve findings 1 and 2 (put display/void on the wire decoration and define
  author-name param/void semantics), then re-review?

✻ Worked for 1m 55s

※ recap: We're designing ADR-183 (author text-layout macros like [br], [p], [center=%]). The /adr-review just
  flagged it NEEDS WORK (9/12): the wire decoration doesn't carry block-vs-inline info, and author-name
  param/void behavior is undefined. Next: revise the ADR to fix those two gaps, then re-review.

❯ I need examples for 1-2 to visualize the issues                                                            

⏺ Here are the two gaps made concrete. I'll show what the renderer (render-to-string) actually receives on
  the wire versus what it needs to emit.

  Finding 1 — the wire object can't distinguish block / inline / void

  Today an IDecoration is essentially { className, content }. The ADR adds value. So three very different
  author inputs produce near-identical wire objects:

  // author writes:        // render-to-string receives:                      // must emit:
  [em:Zork]            →   { className: 'sharpee-em',     content: ['Zork'] }     <span
  class="sharpee-em">Zork</span>
  [center=50:Notice]   →   { className: 'sharpee-center', value: '50',
                                                          content: ['Notice'] }   <div class="sharpee-center"
  data-sharpee-value="50">Notice</div>
  [br]                 →   { className: 'sharpee-br',     content: [] }           <br class="sharpee-br">

  The renderer has to choose <span> vs <div> vs <br> — but nothing in the object tells it which. value only
  appears on center, and a "block" flag appears nowhere. To decide, render-to-string would have to hardcode a
  name list or import platform-vocabulary — which is declared engine-internal ("No external package imports

  interface IDecoration {
    className: string;
    content: TextContent[];
    display?: 'inline' | 'block';   // ← new: span vs div
    void?: boolean;                 // ← new: self-closing element, ignore content
    value?: string;                 // (already proposed)
  }

  { className: 'sharpee-center', display: 'block',  value: '50', content:[…] }  // <div …
  data-sharpee-value="50">
  { className: 'sharpee-em',     display: 'inline', content:[…] }               // <span …>
  { className: 'sharpee-br',     void: true }                                   // <br …>

  Finding 2 — author-defined names collide with the new mechanism

  resolver.ts today: platform names get the sharpee- prefix; any other name passes through verbatim as a
  literal CSS class (that's how authors use their own styles):

  [em:x]          → className 'sharpee-em'     // platform
  [thief-taunt:x] → className 'thief-taunt'    // author — verbatim, no prefix

  Now layer the ADR's =value and void [br] onto that pass-through and three things are undefined:

  // (a) author param — what attribute carries the value?
  [mybox=3:x]   → { className: 'mybox', value: '3', content:[…] }
     //  <span class="mybox" data-sharpee-value="3">   ← a sharpee- data attr on a non-sharpee class?
     //  or data-value="3"?  or dropped?   ← ADR only defined data-sharpee-value for platform names

  // (b) author block — author names have NO vocabulary entry, so no `display`.
  //     Result: authors can ONLY ever get inline spans; [mybanner:…] can't be a block.

  // (c) author void — no colon, not a platform void macro:
  [mybreak]     → literal text "[mybreak]"      ← authors can't define void macros at all

  So the open decisions are small but real:
  - (a) author param value → data-value (generic), or data-sharpee-value is platform-only and author params
  are dropped/disallowed?
  - (b) are block decorations platform-only, or can authors declare one?
  - (c) are void macros platform-only (so [mybreak] stays literal — probably yes)?

  My lean: keep block + void platform-only (they're layout primitives the platform owns), and author params
  emit a generic data-value. That's the least surprising and keeps the sharpee- namespace clean — but it's
  your call.

  Want me to encode finding 1 (the display/void wire fields) and your answers to (a)/(b)/(c) into ADR-183,
  then re-review?

✻ Sautéed for 48s

❯ The author should be able to override the sharpee css, but that would be en-mass...this would solve one    
  problem on the render-side                                                                                 

⏺ Right — and that's a cleaner resolution to Finding 1 than the display/void wire fields I proposed. Let me
  make sure I'm tracking your insight, with examples.

  The idea: one neutral element, class-driven CSS, author-overridable en masse

  If the renderer emits the same element for every decoration (a <span> carrying the class + value), then it
  never has to decide block vs inline vs void — CSS does, via the .sharpee-* rules the platform ships and the
  author can override wholesale.

  // wire stays simple — className + (optional) value, NO display/void fields:
  [em:Zork]          → <span class="sharpee-em">Zork</span>
  [center=50:Notice] → <span class="sharpee-center" data-value="50">Notice</span>
  [br]               → <span class="sharpee-br"></span>
  [p]                → <span class="sharpee-p"></span>

  The platform stylesheet makes them behave — block-ness, breaks, gaps, width all live in CSS:

  .sharpee-em     { font-style: italic; }              /* stays inline (span default) */
  .sharpee-center { display: block; text-align: center; }
  .sharpee-center[data-value] { width: attr(data-value '%'); margin: 0 auto; }
  .sharpee-br     { display: block; }                  /* empty block = line break */
  .sharpee-p      { display: block; height: 1em; }     /* blank-line gap */

  So display: block on an empty span is a line break — the renderer stays dumb, and the block/inline/void
  distinction moves entirely into the (overridable) stylesheet. Finding 1 dissolves: no wire change needed 
  beyond value.

  The poem, end-to-end:
  
  [center=60:[em:Roses are red,[br]Violets are blue,[p]Sugar is sweet,[br]And so are you.]]
     →  <span class="sharpee-center" data-value="60">
          <span class="sharpee-em">Roses are red,<span class="sharpee-br"></span>Violets are blue,
          <span class="sharpee-p"></span>Sugar is sweet,<span class="sharpee-br"></span>And so are
  you.</span>
        </span>

  …and the author restyles all of it by shipping their own .sharpee-center { … } etc. — that's the "en masse"
  part: you override the platform stylesheet, not individual instances. Which is exactly ADR-174's model
  (classes + stylesheet, no inline styles).

  What it does to Finding 2
  
  It mostly dissolves that too:
  - (b) author blocks — no longer platform-only. An author writes [mybanner:…] → <span class="mybanner">,
  then ships .mybanner { display:block }. They get blocks for free via their own CSS.
  - (a) author params — data-value (generic, no sharpee- prefix) works for platform and author classes alike:
  <span class="mybox" data-value="3">.

  The one piece CSS can't solve is (c) void author macros — [mybreak] with no colon is a parser decision
  (stays literal), independent of rendering. Likely answer: void macros are platform-only (br/p); authors who
  want a break use those.

  Two caveats worth a sentence each

  - Non-web clients have no CSS. A terminal client maps class → behavior in its own table (sharpee-br → emit
  \n). That's already the channel model — each client decides rendering from the class. So "CSS" generalizes
  to "the client's class→presentation layer"; the wire still carries only the class.
  - Accessibility / semantic HTML. Everything-is-a-span means no real <p>/<br>/headings for screen readers.
  But ADR-174 already chose "no semantic HTML on the wire," so this is consistent with the existing decision,
  not a new regression.

  Net: your insight lets ADR-183 keep the wire as className + value (drop the display/void fields I floated),
  and pushes block/inline/void/width into the overridable stylesheet. Want me to revise ADR-183 to adopt
  this — uniform-span rendering, class-driven CSS, data-value for params, void macros platform-only — and
  note the en-masse override as the customization model? Then re-review.

✻ Churned for 1m 8s

❯ revise the ADR and re-review                                                                               

⏺ Adopting the uniform-span / class-driven-CSS model. This simplifies the wire (drops the display/void
  fields) and resolves both findings, so I'll rewrite the affected sections. Let me read the current ADR,
  then revise.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

       50  ```ts
       54 -interface VocabularyEntry {

       56 -  display: 'inline' | 'block';      // span vs block container

          +ere

       69 -| `center` | block   | no   | number?  | center; optional width as a % (e.g. 50)  |        
       70 -| `right`  | block   | no   | none     | right-align (rounding out the set)       |        
       63 +**`display`/inline-vs-block is not on the wire and not in the engine — it lives in the     
       64 +stylesheet.** This is the key simplification (see §3).                                     
       65  
       72 -Existing inline entries gain `display:'inline', param:'none'`.                             
       73 -                                                                                           
       66  ### 2. Syntax — two additive forms
       67  
       68  - **Optional parameter** in the name segment: `[name=value:content]`. The `=value` is parse
           d
       69    only before the first `:`, so content may freely contain `:` and `=`. Examples:
       70    `[center=50:Notice]`, `[indent=2:…]`, `[center:Notice]` (no param → default).
       79 -- **Void macro** (no content, no colon): `[br]`. A `[name]` with no colon resolves to a voi
          -d                                                                                          
       80 -  decoration when `name` is a registered void macro; otherwise it stays literal (ADR-174's 
       81 -  existing "bracket without `:` is literal" rule, now with a void-macro exception).        
       71 +- **Void macro** (no content, no colon): `[br]`, `[p]`. A `[name]` with no colon resolves t
          +o a                                                                                        
       72 +  void decoration when `name ∈ VOID_MACROS`; otherwise it stays literal (ADR-174's existing
       73 +  "bracket without `:` is literal" rule, now with the void exception).                     
       74  
       83 -Backward compatible: a plain `[name:content]` parses exactly as before; only `\n` semantics
          - change.                                                                                   
       75 +A plain `[name:content]` parses exactly as before; only `\n` semantics change.             
       76  
       85 -### 3. Wire shape — value as data, never as inline style                                   
       77 +### 3. Wire + render — one neutral element, presentation in CSS                            
       78  
       87 -`IDecoration` gains an optional `value?: string`. Rendering emits the value as a **data    
       88 -attribute**, not a style — consistent with ADR-174's "no inline styles" and the data-only w
          -ire:                                                                                       
       79 +`IDecoration` gains a single optional field, `value?: string`. The wire shape stays        
       80 +`{ className, content, value? }` — **no `display` or `void` field.** The renderer emits the
       81 +**same element for every decoration** — a `<span>` carrying the class and (if present)     
       82 +`data-value`, with void macros simply having empty content:                                
       83  
       84  ```
       91 -[center=50:Notice]  →  <div class="sharpee-center" data-sharpee-value="50">Notice</div>    
       92 -[br]                →  <br class="sharpee-br">                                             
       93 -[p]                 →  paragraph break (client maps `.sharpee-p` to a blank-line gap)      
       85 +[em:Zork]          →  <span class="sharpee-em">Zork</span>                                 
       86 +[center=50:Notice] →  <span class="sharpee-center" data-value="50">Notice</span>           
       87 +[br]               →  <span class="sharpee-br"></span>                                     
       88 +[p]                →  <span class="sharpee-p"></span>                                      
       89  ```
       90  
       96 -A poem — centered 60% column, italic, `[br]` per line, `[p]` between stanzas:              
       91 +Block-ness, line breaks, gaps, indent, and width are **entirely CSS**, in the platform     
       92 +stylesheet — which the author overrides *en masse* (ship their own `.sharpee-*` rules):    
       93  
       94 +```css                                                                                     
       95 +.sharpee-em     { font-style: italic; }                 /* stays inline (span default) */  
       96 +.sharpee-center { display: block; text-align: center; margin: 0 auto; }                    
       97 +.sharpee-center[data-value] { width: attr(data-value '%'); }  /* or equivalent */          
       98 +.sharpee-br     { display: block; }                     /* empty block = line break */     
       99 +.sharpee-p      { display: block; height: 1em; }        /* blank-line gap */               
      100  ```
      101 +                                                                                           
      102 +So the renderer never decides span-vs-div-vs-br — `display:block` on an empty span *is* a l
          +ine                                                                                        
      103 +break. `render-to-string` needs **no** access to the (engine-internal) vocabulary; it just 
          +emits                                                                                      
      104 +`<span class=… [data-value=…]>…</span>` and recurses. `data-value` is generic (no `sharpee-
          +`                                                                                          
      105 +prefix) so it works for platform and author classes alike.                                 
      106 +                                                                                           
      107 +The poem, end to end:                                                                      
      108 +                                                                                           
      109 +```                                                                                        
      110  [center=60:[em:Roses are red,[br]Violets are blue,[p]Sugar is sweet,[br]And so are you.]]
      111 +  →  <span class="sharpee-center" data-value="60"><span class="sharpee-em">Roses are red,<s
          +pan                                                                                        
      112 +     class="sharpee-br"></span>Violets are blue,<span class="sharpee-p"></span>Sugar is    
      113 +     sweet,<span class="sharpee-br"></span>And so are you.</span></span>                   
      114  ```
      115  
      102 -Block entries render as block containers (`div`); inline entries stay `span` (unchanged). T
          -he                                                                                         
      103 -client's platform CSS reads `.sharpee-center` + `data-sharpee-value` to apply width — the  
      104 -platform ships defaults; authors may override per ADR-174 / the author-customizable client.
      116 +**Non-web clients** (terminal) have no CSS; each client maps `class → behavior` in its own 
          +layer                                                                                      
      117 +(`sharpee-br` → emit `\n`, `sharpee-center` → center the run). That is already the channel 
          +model:                                                                                     
      118 +the wire carries the class, the client decides rendering. "CSS" generalizes to "the client'
          +s                                                                                          
      119 +class→presentation layer."                                                                 
      120  
      121 +### 4. Author-defined names                                                                
      122 +                                                                                           
      123 +`resolveClassName` already passes any non-platform name through verbatim as a class. That  
      124 +composes cleanly with §3:                                                                  
      125 +                                                                                           
      126 +- **Author param:** `[mybox=3:x]` → `<span class="mybox" data-value="3">x</span>`. Same gen
          +eric                                                                                       
      127 +  `data-value`; the author's own CSS reads it.                                             
      128 +- **Author block:** authors get block layout for free — `[mybanner:x]` → `<span class="myba
          +nner">`                                                                                    
      129 +  plus the author's `.mybanner { display:block }`. Block is not platform-only.             
      130 +- **Author void:** **not supported.** Void macros are platform-only (`VOID_MACROS`); `[mybr
          +eak]`                                                                                      
      131 +  (no colon, not a platform void name) stays literal, as today. Authors who want a break us
          +e                                                                                          
      132 +  `[br]`/`[p]`.                                                                            
      133 +                                                                                           
      134  ### Forgiving rules (extending ADR-174 AC-10..12)
      135  
      136  - Unknown name → literal (unchanged).
      109 -- A param on a `param:'none'` entry → param ignored, decoration still applies.             
      110 -- A required-but-missing param → entry's default (e.g. `center` = full width, `indent` = 1)
          -.                                                                                          
      111 -- A non-numeric value where `param:'number'` is expected → ignored (fall back to default). 
      137 +- `[name]` (no colon): void decoration iff `name ∈ VOID_MACROS`, else literal.             
      138 +- `[name=value:…]` where the name has no CSS use for a value → `data-value` still emitted, 
          +CSS                                                                                        
      139 +  ignores it. No engine-side validation or coercion; value is an opaque string.            
      140

          -.ts`

      118 -  `assemble.ts`.

      120 -  elements and the `data-sharpee-value` attribute.

          - the                                                                                       
      122 -  inline/block distinction if it surfaces in `ITextBlock`.

      127 -  (e.g. The Alderman's letter/program).                                                    
      143 +- **`@sharpee/engine`** (prose-pipeline): `decorations/parser.ts` (parse optional `=value`;

          +ged

          +logic.

      152 +  `.sharpee-center[data-value]` / `.sharpee-indent[data-value]` handling.

      156  ## Test requirements
      157  
      131 -- **End-to-end:** `parseDecorations('[center=50:Notice]')` → a decoration `{name:'center', 
      132 -  value:'50', display:'block', content:['Notice']}`; render → `<div class="sharpee-center" 
      133 -  data-sharpee-value="50">Notice</div>`.                                                   
      134 -- **Void:** `[br]` produces a line-break node; `[p]` a paragraph-break node. A literal `\n`
          - (and                                                                                      
      135 -  any whitespace run) collapses to a single space — it does **not** break. `[notamacro]` (n
          -o                                                                                          
      136 -  colon, unregistered) stays literal.                                                      
      137 -- **Whitespace:** `'a\n\n  b'` → `'a b'` (collapsed, single space); leading/trailing trimme
          -d.                                                                                         
      138 -- **Param boundary:** `[indent=2:x]` → level 2; `[indent:x]` → level 1 (default).          
      139 -- **Negative:** `[center=oops:x]` (non-numeric) → default width, decoration still applies; 
      140 -  `[em=5:x]` (param on a no-param entry) → param ignored, `em` still applies; `[center=50 x
          -]`                                                                                         
      141 -  (no colon) → literal text (existing forgiving rule).                                     
      142 -- **Backward compat:** every existing ADR-174 markup test still passes unchanged.          
      158 +- **End-to-end:** `parseDecorations('[center=50:Notice]')` → `{ className:'sharpee-center',
      159 +  value:'50', content:['Notice'] }`; render → `<span class="sharpee-center"                
      160 +  data-value="50">Notice</span>`.                                                          
      161 +- **Void:** `[br]`/`[p]` → `<span class="sharpee-br"></span>` / `sharpee-p` (empty content)
          +. A                                                                                        
      162 +  literal `\n` (and any whitespace run) collapses to one space — no break. `[notamacro]` (n
          +o                                                                                          
      163 +  colon, not void) stays literal.                                                          
      164 +- **Whitespace:** `'a\n\n  b'` → `'a b'`; leading/trailing trimmed.                        
      165 +- **Param passthrough:** `[indent=2:x]` → `data-value="2"`; `[indent:x]` → no `data-value`.
      166 +- **Author names (boundary):** `[mybox=3:x]` → `<span class="mybox" data-value="3">` (verba
          +tim                                                                                        
      167 +  class, generic data attr); `[mybreak]` → literal text (void is platform-only).           
      168 +- **Uniform element:** an inline (`em`) and a block (`center`) both render as `<span>` — th
          +e                                                                                          
      169 +  element does not differ; only the class (and CSS) does.                                  
      170 +- **Backward compat:** every existing ADR-174 markup test passes unchanged.                
      171  
      172  ## Acceptance criteria
      173  
      146 -- All four new names parse, validate, render to the documented wire output, and have platfo
          -rm                                                                                         
      147 -  CSS rules.                                                                               
      148 -- `center` honors an arbitrary integer % via `data-sharpee-value`; no inline `style=` appea
          -rs on                                                                                      
      149 -  the wire.                                                                                
      174 +- All five new names parse and render to the documented uniform-`<span>` output; `br`/`p`/`
          +indent`                                                                                    
      175 +  /`center`/`right` have platform CSS rules.                                               
      176 +- `center`/`indent` carry an arbitrary value via `data-value`; **no `style=` and no `displa
          +y`/void                                                                                    
      177 +  field appears on the wire** — block/inline is CSS only.                                  
      178 +- `render-to-string` contains no reference to the platform vocabulary and no span-vs-div br
          +anch.                                                                                      
      179 +- Author names pass through with `data-value`; void remains platform-only.                 
      180  - Existing inline-decoration behavior and tests are unaffected.
      181  - An author can write the macros in a description, a message, and a `ReadableTrait` text an
           d see
      152 -  them render (the Alderman letter/program are natural fixtures).

      184  ## Alternatives
      185  
      156 -- **Option A — class-only presets (stay within ADR-174).** Express width as discrete classe
          -s                                                                                          
      157 -  (`center`, `center-narrow`, `center-wide`) plus `indent-1/2/3`; no `=value` syntax, no wi
          -re                                                                                         
      158 -  change. Cheapest and fully consistent with "everything is a class," but no arbitrary %, a
          -nd                                                                                         
      159 -  every future parameterized need (column widths, table spans, rules) re-hits this wall. Vo
          -id                                                                                         
      160 -  `br` still needs the self-closing syntax regardless.                                     
      161 -- **Option C — inline styles on the wire.** Emit `style="width:50%"`. Rejected: violates   
      162 -  ADR-174's no-inline-styles rule and removes the client's control over presentation.      
      186 +- **Option A — class-only presets (stay within ADR-174).** Width as discrete classes       
      187 +  (`center`, `center-narrow`, `center-wide`) + `indent-1/2/3`; no `=value`. Cheapest and fu
          +lly                                                                                        
      188 +  "everything is a class," but no arbitrary %, and every future parameterized need (column 
      189 +  widths, table spans) re-hits the wall. Void `br`/`p` still need the colon-less syntax any
          +way.                                                                                       
      190 +- **Option C — inline styles on the wire** (`style="width:50%"`). Rejected: violates ADR-17
          +4's                                                                                        
      191 +  no-inline-styles rule and removes client control over presentation.                      
      192 +- **Superseded draft — `display`/`void` fields on the wire.** An earlier revision put block
          +/inline                                                                                    
      193 +  and void markers on `IDecoration` so the renderer could choose `<div>`/`<span>`/`<br>`. D
          +ropped                                                                                     
      194 +  in favor of §3's uniform `<span>` + class-driven CSS: simpler wire, dumber renderer, no  
      195 +  engine-internal vocabulary leak into channel-service, and authors gain block layout for f
          +ree.                                                                                       
      196  
      164 -**Why B over A:** a width value is *data*, which is exactly what the channel model says the
          - wire                                                                                      
      165 -should carry; A pushes that data into an ever-growing class enum. B reopens ADR-174 rule 1 
      166 -deliberately and narrowly — *optional* params, value-as-data — without weakening the       
      167 -no-inline-styles rule.                                                                     
      197 +**Why this over A:** a width value is *data* — what the channel model says the wire should 
      198 +carry — and class-driven CSS keeps the renderer trivial while still honoring no-inline-styl
          +es.                                                                                        
      199  
      200  ## Consequences
      201  
      171 -- **Reopens ADR-174's "no parameterized decorations."** Future parameterized layout (column
          -s,                                                                                         
      172 -  tables, rules) now has a sanctioned mechanism (`=value` + `data-sharpee-value`) instead o
          -f                                                                                          
      173 -  class explosion.                                                                         
      174 -- **First block-level decorations.** The pipeline/`ITextBlock` must distinguish inline vs b
          -lock;                                                                                      
      175 -  nesting a block inside an inline run is invalid and resolved by the forgiving rules.     
      176 -- **Platform CSS is now part of the decoration contract for params** — a param-bearing name
      177 -  without a matching CSS rule renders unstyled. Adding a name still means: vocabulary entry
          - +                                                                                         
      178 -  CSS rule (now also: data-attribute handling).                                            
      179 -- **One behavior change: whitespace collapses.** Natural newlines (and tab/space runs) beco
          -me a                                                                                       
      180 -  single space; `[br]` is the only break. Existing text that relied on literal `\n` for lin
          -e                                                                                          
      181 -  breaks must convert to `[br]` — notably The Alderman's letter and theatre program (the ro
          -oms                                                                                        
      182 -  cleanup already conforms, since its descriptions are single-paragraph prose). All other m
          -arkup                                                                                      
      183 -  is unaffected; no stored-format migration.                                               
      202 +- **Reopens ADR-174's "no parameterized decorations,"** narrowly: an *optional* `=value` ca
          +rried                                                                                      
      203 +  as `data-value`. Future parameterized layout (columns, table spans, rules) now has a sanc
          +tioned                                                                                     
      204 +  mechanism instead of class explosion. The no-inline-styles rule is untouched.            
      205 +- **Everything renders as `<span>`.** No semantic `<p>`/`<br>`/headings on the wire — but A
          +DR-174                                                                                     
      206 +  already chose "no semantic HTML," so this is consistent, not a new regression. Accessibil
          +ity                                                                                        
      207 +  remains a client concern (a client may map classes to semantic elements if it wishes).   
      208 +- **Presentation is fully in the (overridable) stylesheet.** Adding a name = vocabulary ent
          +ry +                                                                                       
      209 +  a platform CSS rule. Authors restyle *en masse* by shipping `.sharpee-*` overrides — ther
          +e is                                                                                       
      210 +  no per-instance styling (consistent with no-inline-styles); fine-grained control means an
      211 +  author class.                                                                            
      212 +- **One behavior change: whitespace collapses.** Natural newlines and tab/space runs become
          + a                                                                                         
      213 +  single space; `[br]`/`[p]` are the only breaks. Text that relied on literal `\n` must con
          +vert —                                                                                     
      214 +  notably The Alderman's letter and theatre program (the rooms cleanup already conforms). N
          +o                                                                                          
      215 +  stored-format migration.

          -ion;                                                                                       
      189 -awaiting accept/modify (A vs B).

          +ue not                                                                                     
      221 +represented on the wire and (2) undefined author-name interaction; both resolved by the    
      222 +uniform-span / class-driven-CSS model in §3–4. No implementation; awaiting accept.         

⏺ Revised. Re-running the review against the updated ADR.

  ADR Review: ADR-183 — Author layout macros (parameterized decorations)
  Status: PROPOSED
  ════════════════════════════════════

  Architecture Compliance
    Language layer separation:     PASS — markup parsed in engine; no prose outside lang/story.
    Logic location:                PASS — parse/resolve (engine), render (channel-service), types
                                   (text-blocks), CSS (platform-browser). Cleaner now: the
                                   renderer needs no vocabulary.
    Capability dispatch:           N/A — no verbs/behaviors.

  Contract Completeness
    All affected packages named:   PASS — engine files, text-blocks types, channel-service
                                   render-to-string, platform CSS, sharpee barrel, lang/stories.
    Update contracts specified:    PASS — "name = vocab entry + CSS rule"; VOID_MACROS export;
                                   whitespace-collapse step.
    Interface contracts specified: PASS — IDecoration = {className, content, value?}; render
                                   contract is one uniform <span> + optional data-value. The prior
                                   FAIL is gone because the renderer no longer *chooses* an
                                   element — CSS owns block/inline/void.
    Boundary contracts specified:  PASS — pipeline→channel-service carries className+value only;
                                   render-to-string explicitly does NOT import the (engine-internal)
                                   vocabulary; author-name boundary defined in §4.

  Test Requirements
    End-to-end test specified:     PASS — [center=50:Notice] → exact <span … data-value> output.
    Boundary tests specified:      PASS — author-name passthrough, void-is-platform-only,
                                   param-present-vs-absent, and an explicit uniform-element test
                                   (em and center both render as <span>).
    Negative tests specified:      PASS — [notamacro]/[mybreak] literal, whitespace collapse.

  Acceptance Criteria
    Explicit acceptance criteria:  PASS — concrete + verifiable, incl. "render-to-string has no
                                   vocabulary reference and no span-vs-div branch."
    Save/restore implications:     N/A — markup is authored text, not runtime state.
    Backward compatibility:        PASS — whitespace-collapse change + migration (letter/program).

  Open Questions
    No unresolved blockers:        PASS — both prior FAILs resolved; author void/param/block
  Score: 12/12
  Verdict: READY FOR IMPLEMENTATION

  Implementation Audit
    (still PROPOSED — no implementation yet)
    engine/.../resolver.ts:   ALIGNS — the verbatim author-name passthrough that was a *gap* last
                              round is now a *feature* the ADR builds on (§4). No change needed.
    text-blocks/types.ts:     CLEAN target — only adds IDecoration.value (no display/void).
    channel-service/render-to-string.ts: needs the uniform <span> + data-value emission; contract
                              is now fully specified and vocabulary-free.

  12/12, READY. Both findings from the last pass are resolved by your uniform-span / class-driven-CSS model:
  - Finding 1 — the renderer never picks an element, so block/inline/void don't need to be on the wire (only
  value does). CSS owns presentation; the wire stays {className, content, value?}.
  - Finding 2 — author params use the generic data-value, author blocks come free via author CSS, and void
  stays platform-only ([mybreak] literal).

  The revision is uncommitted (it edits the committed 6204ad63). Want me to commit the revision (and, since
  it's now READY, flip Status to ACCEPTED if you're ready to commit to reopening ADR-174's no-parameters
  rule)?

⏺ ADR-183 is ACCEPTED and committed (632128a7).

  Final shape of the decision:
  - Macros: [br]/[p] (void breaks), [center=%]/[indent=n]/[right] (parameterized), on top of ADR-174's inline
  vocabulary.
  - Render model: every decoration → one uniform <span class="sharpee-…" data-value="…">;
  block/inline/break/gap/width all live in the platform stylesheet, author-overridable en masse. The renderer
  stays vocabulary-free.
  - Whitespace: explicit-only breaks; all source whitespace (incl. newlines) collapses to a single space.
  - Author names: generic data-value, blocks via author CSS, void platform-only.
  - Reopens ADR-174's "no parameterized decorations" narrowly (optional =value as data; no-inline-styles rule
  intact).

⏺ Both converted (no .join left). But there's a real sequencing caveat I have to flag, because it affects
  what the player sees right now:

  Heads-up: this renders literally until ADR-183 ships

  ADR-183 is ACCEPTED but not implemented — br/p aren't in the platform vocabulary or VOID_MACROS yet. Under
  what the player sees right now:

  Heads-up: this renders literally until ADR-183 ships

  ADR-183 is ACCEPTED but not implemented — br/p aren't in the platform vocabulary or VOID_MACROS yet. Under
  the current parser, [br] (a bracket with no colon) hits the existing forgiving rule → emitted as literal
  text. So as of this commit, the letter and program will display:

  Dear Mr. Hewitt,[p]I write to inform you of my decision...

  — with literal [br]/[p] visible, and the old \n line breaks gone. That's a temporary regression in the
  rendered story until the engine implements ADR-183.

  So the conversion is correct against the target model, but it's ahead of the engine. Three ways to handle
  it:

  1. Implement ADR-183 next (engine prose-pipeline) so the markup actually renders — then the letter/program
  look right and the feature lands. But that's a platform change (packages/engine), which per your rules
  needs the green light, and it's the bigger piece of work.
  2. Hold the conversion — revert these two until ADR-183 is implemented, so the story keeps its current \n
  breaks in the meantime.
  3. Commit as-is — accept the temporary literal rendering; the story text is now future-correct and will
  "turn on" when ADR-183 ships.

  I haven't committed the conversion yet. Which do you want — implement ADR-183 now, hold the conversion, or
  commit it as future-correct?

⏺ This is a multi-package platform change, and one part carries real risk. Let me note the shape before
  editing:

  - render-to-string.ts is the terminal/ANSI renderer, not HTML — so the ADR's <span> output is the web
  renderer (platform-browser); the terminal path needs sharpee-br → \n, sharpee-p → \n\n.
  - Whitespace-collapse is globally behavior-changing. Existing content (notably Dungeo, per ADR-079
  alignment) could rely on multi-space/newline layout — collapsing it risks silently breaking transcripts.

  So I'll implement the safe, additive core now (the data model + parsing + void macros + terminal break
  mapping), which makes [br]/[p]/params work without disturbing existing text, and hold whitespace-collapse
  as a separate validated step (it needs a Dungeo transcript check first). Let me read the precise type block
  and the parser test location.

      74 + * listed here; any other colon-less bracket stays literal (ADR-174 AC-11).

      82 +/**

⏺ Now the parser — void macros + optional =value.

  Behavior Statement — parseBracketAt (ADR-183 changes)
  - DOES: returns a void IDecoration (content: []) for [name] when name ∈ VOID_MACROS; attaches a parsed
  value for [name=value:content]
  - WHEN: parsing bracket markup
  - BECAUSE: ADR-183 void breaks ([br]/[p]) and parameterized decorations ([center=50:…])
  - REJECTS WHEN: colon-less [name] with name ∉ VOID_MACROS → literal; empty name → children (both unchanged)

  │                  │ +br/p/indent/center/right + VOID_MACROS export               │ tests)            │
  ├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤

  engine and channel-service typecheck clean against the rebuilt text-blocks. Test grading: all new tests
  assert on real parsed output / rendered strings — GREEN, no RED/YELLOW.

  What this fixes right now

  The Alderman letter/program render correctly in the terminal/transcript path — [br]→newline, [p]→blank
  line. The "renders literally" regression is resolved for that path. (Seen after a ./sharpee build refreshes
  the bundle.)

  Deliberately not done (flagged, your call)

  1. Web/HTML renderer — [center=50:…] → <span class="sharpee-center" data-value="50"> lives in
  packages/platform-browser/src/channels/text-content.ts + platform CSS for
  .sharpee-br/p/indent/center/right. The terminal path works; the browser client + CSS is the remaining piece
  for visual layout in the web client.
  2. Whitespace-collapse (ADR §0) — deferred on purpose. It's a global behavior change, and ADR-079 says
  Dungeo has aligned text; collapsing multi-space/newlines could silently break Dungeo's room/ASCII layout.
  This needs a Dungeo transcript-regression check before flipping on. Until then, literal \n still passes
  through (backward compatible), and [br]/[p] are the explicit breaks.

  So I scoped to exactly the engine prose-pipeline you named (safe, additive, fully tested) and held the two
  riskier/cross-layer pieces.

  The changes are uncommitted, on ide/p2-ts-aware-editor (now also carrying platform/engine work — the branch
  is quite mixed). How do you want to proceed:
  - commit this engine slice (and where — here, or a fresh branch off main since it's platform work)?
  - and next: the web renderer + CSS, the whitespace-collapse + Dungeo validation, or stop here?

      117 +                                                                                           
      118 +/* ----- Layout macros (ADR-183) ----- */                                                  
      119 +/* All block-level; the void br/p render as empty spans. Presentation lives                
      120 +   here so authors override en masse by re-declaring these classes. */                     
      121 +                                                                                           
      122 +/* An empty block between inline runs collapses to a single line break. */                 
      123 +.sharpee-br {                                                                              
      124 +  display: block;                                                                          
      125 +}                                                                                          
      126 +                                                                                           
      127 +/* An empty block with height gives a blank-line (paragraph) gap. */                       
      128 +.sharpee-p {                                                                               
      129 +  display: block;                                                                          
      130 +  height: 1em;                                                                             
      131 +}                                                                                          
      132 +                                                                                           
      133 +.sharpee-center {                                                                          
      134 +  display: block;                                                                          
      135 +  text-align: center;                                                                      
      136 +}                                                                                          
      137 +                                                                                           
      138 +.sharpee-right {                                                                           
      139 +  display: block;                                                                          
      140 +  text-align: right;                                                                       
      141 +}                                                                                          
      142 +                                                                                           
      143 +.sharpee-indent {                                                                          
      144 +  display: block;                                                                          
      145 +  margin-left: 2em;                                                                        
      146 +}                                                                                          
      147 +                                                                                           
      148 +/* `[center=N:…]` and `[indent=N:…]` carry N as `data-value`. The platform                 
      149 +   default applies the unparameterized behavior (center / one indent level) so             
      150 +   markup degrades gracefully; honoring an arbitrary value (width %, indent                
      151 +   level) is left to author CSS or a client pass — the value is present on the             
      152 +   element either way. */

                                                   collapsing                                                                                                   would                                                                                                        break                                                                                                        the                                                                                                          cheap                                                                                                        validation                                                                                                   first.

     stories/dungeo/src/regions/bank-of-zork.ts:76:    'This is a small square room, which was used by a banofficer                                                                                                      whose                                                                                                        job                                                                                                          it                                                                                                           was                                                                                                          to                                                                                                           retrieve                                                                                                     safety                                                                                                       deposit                                                                                                      boxes                                                                                                        for                                                                                                          the                                                                                                          customer.                                                                                                    On                                                                                                           the                                                                                                          north                                                                                                        side                                                                                                         of                                                                                                           the                                                                                                          room                                                                                                         is                                                                                                           sign                                                                                                         which                                                                                                        reads                                                                                                        "Viewing                                                                                                     Room".
