     NLY',
     … +41 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ This is decisive — and it's a stop-and-surface moment, not a "plow ahead" one. The deferred Dungeo validation                                                                                                          just                                                                                                         came                                                                                                         back,                                                                                                        and                                                                                                          it                                                                                                           kills                                                                                                        global                                                                                                       whitespace-collapse.

  Finding: ADR-183 §0 (global whitespace-collapse) would break Dungeo

  Dungeo's text is heavily whitespace-dependent. Concrete examples the collapse rule would wreck:

  - Death/sign banners rely on \n + leading spaces:
    - …\n\n    **** You have died **** (dozens of these)
    - …sign reading\n\n            BANK PERSONNEL ONLY
  - Multi-line signs: the bank viewing room is a 4-line \n-separated notice.
  - Indented structured text: WARNING:\n  This room…\n    -- Frobozz Magic Cave Company\n       per M. Agrippa,                                                                                                          foreman
  - Column-aligned GDT help: '  DA  Display Adventurer    - Show player state' (alignment via runs of spaces).
  - By the numbers: 27,399 runs of 2+ spaces in Dungeo string literals, plus many \n-formatted messages.

  Global collapse turns every one of these into a single run-on line. And some of it (column alignment, space-indented                                                                                                   banners)                                                                                                     can't                                                                                                        even                                                                                                         be                                                                                                           expressed                                                                                                    with                                                                                                         [br]/[p]                                                                                                     it                                                                                                           needs                                                                                                        preserved                                                                                                    leading                                                                                                      spaces,                                                                                                      which                                                                                                        collapse                                                                                                     destroys.

  So ADR §0 as accepted — "all source whitespace incl. newlines collapses to one space, globally" — is incompatible                                                                                                      with                                                                                                         the                                                                                                          reference                                                                                                    story.                                                                                                       (The                                                                                                         ADR                                                                                                          was                                                                                                          accepted                                                                                                     before                                                                                                       we                                                                                                           checked                                                                                                      Dungeo;                                                                                                      this                                                                                                         is                                                                                                           exactly                                                                                                      the                                                                                                          validation                                                                                                   I'd                                                                                                          flagged                                                                                                      as                                                                                                           required.)

  What I did NOT do

  I stopped before implementing collapse. Everything else in ADR-183 is done and committed ([br]/[p], paramsterminal                                                                                                     web                                                                                                          renderers,                                                                                                   CSS).                                                                                                        The                                                                                                          additive                                                                                                     macros                                                                                                       work                                                                                                         without                                                                                                      collapse                                                                                                     \n/spaces                                                                                                    still                                                                                                        pass                                                                                                         through,                                                                                                     so                                                                                                           existing                                                                                                     content                                                                                                      is                                                                                                           untouched                                                                                                    and                                                                                                          new                                                                                                          stories                                                                                                      use                                                                                                          [br]/[p]                                                                                                     explicitly.

  Options (this needs your call — it amends ADR-183 §0)

  1. Drop global collapse; keep passthrough (recommended). [br]/[p] are the additive explicit breaks; sourcewhitespace                                                                                                   continues                                                                                                    to                                                                                                           render                                                                                                       as-is.                                                                                                       New                                                                                                          stories                                                                                                      just                                                                                                         use                                                                                                          the                                                                                                          macros                                                                                                       and                                                                                                          avoid                                                                                                        source-wrapping                                                                                              reliance.                                                                                                    Amend                                                                                                        §0                                                                                                           to                                                                                                           "explicit                                                                                                    breaks                                                                                                       added;                                                                                                       no                                                                                                           global                                                                                                       whitespace                                                                                                   change."                                                                                                     Zero                                                                                                         risk                                                                                                         to                                                                                                           Dungeo.                                                                                                      The                                                                                                          Alderman                                                                                                     already                                                                                                      conforms.
  2. Opt-in collapse — a per-story or per-block flag; The Alderman opts in, Dungeo doesn't. More machinery; contradicts                                                                                                  §0's                                                                                                         "global"                                                                                                     framing.
  3. Migrate Dungeo first, then collapse globally. Convert ~all Dungeo text to explicit markup — large, and the                                                                                                          column/space-indent                                                                                          cases                                                                                                        need                                                                                                         pre/font-mono                                                                                                preformatted                                                                                                 concept                                                                                                      that                                                                                                         doesn't                                                                                                      exist                                                                                                        yet.                                                                                                         Not                                                                                                          advisable                                                                                                    now.

  My recommendation is #1 — and I'd amend ADR-183 §0 accordingly (it's the honest reconciliation of the accepted                                                                                                         rule                                                                                                         with                                                                                                         the                                                                                                          reference                                                                                                    story).                                                                                                      Want                                                                                                         me                                                                                                           to                                                                                                           amend                                                                                                        the                                                                                                          ADR                                                                                                          to                                                                                                           #1,                                                                                                          o     56:  it('every entry in PLATFORM_VOCABULARY_NAMES resolves to its prefixed form', () => {

⏺ Confirmed — that was a false match on sharpee-font-mono; resolver.test.ts doesn't read the CSS, so there'sno                                                                                                           gate                                                                                                         broke.                                                                                                       The                                                                                                          web-renderer                                                                                                 piece                                                                                                        is                                                                                                           complete                                                                                                     and                                                                                                          tested.

  Let me commit this slice, then proceed to the last item (whitespace-collapse).

  Finding: ADR-183 §0 (global whitespace-collapse) would break Dungeo

  Dungeo's text is heavily whitespace-dependent. Concrete examples the collapse rule would wreck:

  - Death/sign banners rely on \n + leading spaces:
    - …\n\n    **** You have died **** (dozens of these)
    - …sign reading\n\n            BANK PERSONNEL ONLY
  - Multi-line signs: the bank viewing room is a 4-line \n-separated notice.
  - Indented structured text: WARNING:\n  This room…\n    -- Frobozz Magic Cave Company\n       per M. Agrippa,                                                                                                          foreman
  - Column-aligned GDT help: '  DA  Display Adventurer    - Show player state' (alignment via runs of spaces).
  - By the numbers: 27,399 runs of 2+ spaces in Dungeo string literals, plus many \n-formatted messages.

  Global collapse turns every one of these into a single run-on line. And some of it (column alignment, space-indented                                                                                                   banners)                                                                                                     can't                                                                                                        even                                                                                                         be                                                                                                           expressed                                                                                                    with                                                                                                         [br]/[p]                                                                                                     it
  needs preserved leading spaces, which collapse destroys.

  So ADR §0 as accepted — "all source whitespace incl. newlines collapses to one space, globally" — is incompatible                                                                                                      with                                                                                                         the                                                                                                          reference                                                                                                    story.   I stopped before implementing collapse. Everything else in ADR-183 is done and committed ([br]/[p], paramsterminal                                                                                                     web                                                                                                          renderers,                                                                                                   CSS).                                                                                                        The                                                                                                          additive                                                                                                     macros                                                                                                       work
  without collapse — \n/spaces still pass through, so existing content is untouched and new stories use [br]/[p]                                                                                                         explicitly.

  macros and avoid source-wrapping reliance. And some of it (column alignment, space-indented                                                                                                   banners)                                                                                                     can't

⏺ This is decisive — and it's a stop-and-surface moment, not a "plow ahead" one. The deferred Dungeo
  validation just came back, and it kills global whitespace-collapse.

  Finding: ADR-183 §0 (global whitespace-collapse) would break Dungeo

  Dungeo's text is heavily whitespace-dependent. Concrete examples the collapse rule would wreck:

  - Death/sign banners rely on \n + leading spaces:
    - …\n\n    **** You have died **** (dozens of these)
    - …sign reading\n\n            BANK PERSONNEL ONLY
  - Multi-line signs: the bank viewing room is a 4-line \n-separated notice.
  - Indented structured text: WARNING:\n  This room…\n    -- Frobozz Magic Cave Company\n       per M.
  Agrippa, foreman
  - Column-aligned GDT help: '  DA  Display Adventurer    - Show player state' (alignment via runs of
  spaces).
  - By the numbers: 27,399 runs of 2+ spaces in Dungeo string literals, plus many \n-formatted messages.

  Global collapse turns every one of these into a single run-on line. And some of it (column alignment,
  space-indented banners) can't even be expressed with [br]/[p] — it needs preserved leading spaces, which
  collapse destroys.

  So ADR §0 as accepted — "all source whitespace incl. newlines collapses to one space, globally" — is
  incompatible with the reference story. (The ADR was accepted before we checked Dungeo; this is exactly the
  validation I'd flagged as required.)

  What I did NOT do

  I stopped before implementing collapse. Everything else in ADR-183 is done and committed ([br]/[p], params,
  terminal + web renderers, CSS). The additive macros work without collapse — \n/spaces still pass through,
  so existing content is untouched and new stories use [br]/[p] explicitly.

  Options (this needs your call — it amends ADR-183 §0)

  1. Drop global collapse; keep passthrough (recommended). [br]/[p] are the additive explicit breaks; source
  whitespace continues to render as-is. New stories just use the macros and avoid source-wrapping reliance.
  Amend §0 to "explicit breaks added; no global whitespace change." Zero risk to Dungeo. The Alderman already
  conforms.
  2. Opt-in collapse — a per-story or per-block flag; The Alderman opts in, Dungeo doesn't. More machinery;
  contradicts §0's "global" framing.
  3. Migrate Dungeo first, then collapse globally. Convert ~all Dungeo text to explicit markup — large, and
  the column/space-indent cases need a pre/font-mono preformatted concept that doesn't exist yet. Not
  advisable now.

  My recommendation is #1 — and I'd amend ADR-183 §0 accordingly (it's the honest reconciliation of the
  accepted rule with the reference story). Want me to amend the ADR to #1, or do you prefer opt-in (#2) /
  something else?

✻ Brewed for 4m 17s

※ recap: Implementing ADR-183 author layout macros in Sharpee: the macros, renderers, and CSS are done and
  committed, but the final piece — global whitespace-collapse — would break Dungeo's heavily space-aligned
  text. Next: decide whether to amend ADR-183 to drop global collapse and keep `[br]`/`[p]` as additive
  explicit breaks.

❯ there are two competing things here: 1) Dungeo is reference, not untouchable, 2) We're building the        
  canonical path - let's talk this through                                                                   
  
⏺ Agreed — let me reframe, because I think I posed a false dichotomy ("protect Dungeo vs. break it"). Both
  your points are right, and together they point at a third answer.

  The real question: is the canonical model complete?

  The canonical path is "prose collapses; structure is explicit." [br]/[p] cover breaks, and
  [center]/[indent]/[right] cover layout. But look at what Dungeo actually needs, sorted by kind:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐
  │              Dungeo content              │      Canonical form      │           Covered?            │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Death banner \n\n **** You have died     │ [center:**** You have    │ ✅ — and better (semantic,    │
  │ **** (centered via spaces)               │ died ****]               │ not a space hack)             │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Indented warning block                   │ [indent:…] / [p]         │ ✅                            │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Multi-line sign                          │ [br]-separated           │ ✅                            │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ GDT column tables (DA  Display           │ ???                      │ ❌ no primitive               │
  │ Adventurer    - …)                       │                          │                               │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ ASCII art / maps (if any)                │ ???                      │ ❌ no primitive               │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

  The first three migrate cleanly — and migrating them improves Dungeo (semantic [center] instead of counting
  spaces). That's your point #1 in action: Dungeo isn't untouchable, and the canonical forms are genuinely
  better.

  But the canonical model has a gap: there's no way to say "here, whitespace is significant" — columns,
  tables, ASCII art. Space-padding only works today because nothing collapses. So the honest conclusion isn't
  "don't collapse" — it's the canonical model is missing a primitive.

  The missing primitive: [pre]

  Add a preformatted block — [pre:…] → white-space: pre + monospace (exactly HTML's <pre>). Inside it,
  whitespace and newlines are preserved; outside it, everything collapses. With that, the model is complete:

  - Prose → collapses (clean; source-wrapping can't leak — the original Alderman bug is gone).
  - Breaks/layout → [br]/[p]/[center]/[indent]/[right].
  - Columns/art/anything space-exact → [pre:…].

  Now collapse-by-default is correct, not a compromise — and Dungeo migrates to better representations across
  the board (semantic layout for banners, [pre] for the GDT tables).

  One implementation insight that matters

  On the web, white-space: normal already collapses (HTML default) — so the browser likely only renders
  Dungeo's banners correctly today because the text container is set to pre-wrap. Which means "collapse vs
  preserve" is substantially a CSS white-space decision on the container for the web path, with [pre] as the
  local override. The terminal renderer is the one where collapse is a real string transform. So implementing
  collapse may be less code than it sounds — and [pre] is the piece that makes it safe.

  So the path I'd propose

  1. Add [pre] (vocabulary + parser-skips-collapse-inside-pre + CSS white-space:pre/mono) — the missing
  primitive.
  2. Then turn on collapse-by-default.
  3. Migrate Dungeo to canonical forms ([center] banners, [pre] tables) — tracked as real work, since it's
  the reference and migrating it proves the model.

  That honors both forces: the canonical path stays uncompromised (collapse is the default), and Dungeo gets
  migrated (and improved) rather than special-cased.

  Two things I'd want your read on before touching anything:
  - Is [pre] the right escape hatch, or do you envision columns/tables as their own future construct (and
  [pre] just for raw art)?
  - Sequencing — [pre] + collapse + a Dungeo migration is a multi-session effort. Do we want to commit to it
  as a tracked plan now, or land [pre] first and stage the collapse flip behind a check once Dungeo's
  migrated?

✻ Cooked for 1m 23s

❯ To me, PRE is an HTML artifact and I'm wondering if we can accomplish the missing capabilities in some     
  other way                                                                                                  
  
⏺ Right — and I think your instinct is pointing at something real: [pre] preserves whitespace as layout,
  which is the artifact. Encoding a table by counting spaces, or centering by padding, is exactly the
  space-hack the canonical model is trying to kill. Wrapping it in [pre] just blesses the hack. So let's not.

  If I decompose what [pre] was being asked to cover, it's actually two different needs jammed together, and
  each has a cleaner, semantic answer:

  1. Tabular / columnar content → a structure construct, not preserved spaces

  GDT help, score tables — these aren't "preformatted text," they're rows and columns. The semantic form
  describes the structure and lets the renderer align it:

  [table:[row:[cell:DA][cell:Display Adventurer][cell:Show player state]]
         [row:[cell:DR][cell:Display Room][cell:Show room properties]]]

  The terminal renderer computes column widths and pads (monospace); the web renderer emits a grid/table. No 
  author counts spaces. This is strictly better than [pre]: it's responsive, the alignment is correct even
  when content changes, and it's client-decides-rendering — exactly the channel model. (It's also where
  alignment logic belongs — in the renderer, not baked into the string.)

  2. Pictorial ASCII art / maps → that's not text, it's media

  A drawn map or ASCII picture is a 2D grid where the spaces are the content. There's no semantic
  decomposition — but that's the tell that it isn't prose at all. Sharpee already has a media/image channel.
  A map is an asset: rich clients show a real image/SVG; the terminal shows a text fallback. So "ASCII art"
  routes through media, not through the prose pipeline's whitespace.

  What that buys us

  With tables as structure and art as media, the layout macros (center/indent/right/br/p) cover everything
  else — and there is no whitespace escape hatch at all. Collapse-by-default becomes universal and
  unconditional, the model stays fully semantic, and we never import an HTML artifact. Dungeo's banners
  become [center:…], its tables become [table:…], any real art becomes media — every one a better
  representation than the space-hack.

  The one thing I want to verify before committing
  
  The whole argument rests on Dungeo's whitespace-significant text bucketing cleanly into (a) tables, (b) 
  banners/indents, (c) genuine pictorial art — with nothing in a fourth "irreducibly preformatted prose"
  bucket that would force a verbatim fallback after all.

  Let me audit Dungeo's \n/multi-space content and bucket it — that tells us definitively whether
  table-construct + media + layout-macros fully covers the reference story, or whether there's a residue that
  needs a last-resort answer. Want me to run that audit so we're arguing from the actual content rather than
  my examples?

✻ Cogitated for 1m 7s 
  
❯ this looks like we're overlapping with markdown concepts, should we explore that?                          

⏺ Yes — and it's worth naming, because the overlap is real but it cuts in two directions. Here's the map:
  
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 Concept                  │       Markdown        │                Us                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ emphasis / strong / code                 │ *x* **x** `x`         │ [em:] [strong:] [code:]           │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ paragraph / line break                   │ blank line / two      │ [p] / [br]                        │
  │                                          │ trailing spaces       │                                   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ tables                                   │ | a | b |             │ (the [table:] we just sketched)   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ fenced code block                        │ ```                   │ (the whitespace-significant case  │
  │                                          │                       │ we circled)                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ headings / lists / quote                 │ # - >                 │ — (don't have them yet)           │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ center / color / indent / size           │ — (none)              │ [center=%] [color-red] [indent]   │
  │                                          │                       │ [size-*]                          │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ IF semantics                             │ — (none)              │ [item:] [npc:] …                  │
  │ (item/npc/room/direction/command)        │                       │                                   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

  So Markdown covers the generic prose half and even validates where we just landed — its fenced code block 
  is the semantic "literal/verbatim" answer (named for intent, not pre), and its tables are structure. That's
  a useful signal: the most successful prose markup converged on exactly the primitives we reasoned toward.

  But three frictions make "just adopt Markdown" not a clean swap:

  1. Whitespace philosophy is the opposite. Markdown is whitespace-significant — blank lines make paragraphs,
  leading spaces make code/nesting, trailing spaces make <br>. We just decided the reverse: collapse
  everything, breaks are explicit [br]. Adopting Markdown reintroduces exactly the source-wrapping footgun
  that started this (the Alderman bug is a Markdown-style "newlines mean something" surprise). This is the
  crux.
  2. Markdown's native output is the semantic HTML ADR-174 rejected. #→<h1>, -→<ul>, **→<strong>. Our wire is
  deliberately span+class, no semantic HTML, client-decides. You could parse Markdown into our IDecoration
  model (Markdown as syntax, not output) — but then it's "Markdown-flavored," not Markdown.
  3. It doesn't cover our two hardest needs — IF semantics ([item]/[npc]/…) and layout (center/color/indent)
  have no Markdown equivalent, so we'd be in extended-Markdown-dialect territory regardless.

  And one piece of prior art worth knowing: ADR-174 already removed asterisk markup in favor of brackets —
  the parser test literally asserts *is* now passes through as plain text. So Markdown-style inline was tried
  and deliberately dropped once. I'd want to know why before reopening it (I can pull the ADR-174
  rationale).

  So I'd frame the real choice as:
  
  - (A) Markdown as authoring syntax, compiled to our existing wire. Best ergonomics (authors know it), but
  you inherit the whitespace-significance collision and need extensions for IF/layout. It's a genuine pivot.
  - (B) Use Markdown as the concept inventory, keep brackets as syntax. Don't reinvent the primitive set ad
  hoc — make sure we cover what Markdown proved
  (em/strong/code-verbatim/lists/headings/quote/tables/breaks/paragraphs) — but keep [name:content], which is
  enable collapse).

  Want me to do those two captures, or leave it here for tonight?

          +ables,

          +t

  The only thing left in P2 is step 2.3's Neon integration — and it's intentionally blocked, not pending 
  work:
  - Neon's main pins SwiftTreeSitter to branch:main, which conflicts with our pinned exact 0.10.0 →
  non-reproducible build.
  - The benefit (incremental re-parse vs. full re-parse) is marginal at IDE-file scale.
  - The gate to revisit: only when re-parse is measured slow and ChimeHQ tags a release pinning a stable
  SwiftTreeSitter version.

  So P2 is effectively complete for shipping purposes. There's no unblocked work remaining in this plan —
  Neon is a "revisit-if-measured-slow" item, plus two follow-up polish notes (tree-aware string/comment
  skipping in the bracket matcher).

  What's next is the broader roadmap, not this plan. Per the optimized order in plan-20260509-phases.md: P5 ✓
  → P2 ✓ → P3 (Node helper / language server foundation — also the prerequisite for semantic/type-aware
  coloring) → P7 (outline) → P6 (completion) → P8.

❯ for the IDE, what's next?                                                                                  

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Per the optimized phase order, you've completed P0 → P1 → P4 → P5 → P2. Next up is P3, and it's the
  linchpin for everything after it.

  Next: P3 — Sharpee-Aware Project View

  This is the first phase that makes the IDE Sharpee-aware rather than a generic TS editor. It introduces the
  Node-side helper — the piece the remaining phases all depend on:

  - Replace the filesystem tree with virtual folders (Rooms · Objects · NPCs · Regions · Grammar · Story
  Settings)
  - Build tools/ide/helper/ — a long-running Node subprocess (the option-B bridge locked in P4) that uses the
  TS Compiler API to scan a story, classify each file's exports by trait composition (RoomTrait → Room,
  etc.), and emit a JSON manifest
  - Re-scan on save; Extensions/Plugins panels read @sharpee/* deps from package.json

  Why P3 is the gate

  The two phases after it are described as nearly free because they reuse P3's parser:

  - P7 — Outline & Problems reuses the manifest (outline = per-file subset; Problems = TS diagnostics +
  Sharpee lints like "room with no exits")
  - P6 — Trait Hints needs P3's parser to locate trait positions in the editor

  Then P8 — Distribution (codesign / notarize / DMG) closes out v1.

  One thing to flag before starting P3

  The roadmap predates your tree-sitter decision. P3 specs a Node TS-Compiler-API helper for project
  introspection — but you now have a live tree-sitter parse tree on the Swift side (ADR-182), and ADR-182
  explicitly noted the tree "pays forward" to outline (P7) and trait-position detection (P6). So there's a
  real architectural question worth settling first:

  Does project introspection go Node-side (TS Compiler API, as planned) or Swift-side (reuse the tree-sitter 
  tree)?

  The original rationale for Node-side was "one parser stack, reuse the TS toolchain." Tree-sitter syntactic
  parsing can't do type-level trait resolution, but it can do structural/symbol work. This is plausibly
  ADR-worthy since it constrains P6/P7.

  Want me to (a) dig into that introspection-architecture question and write it up, (b) just start P3 as
  originally specced (Node helper), or (c) something else?

✻ Brewed for 17s

❯ look into the introspection architecture question first                                                    

⏺ This is a real architectural decision that constrains P6/P7, so let me gather the facts before forming a
  recommendation. Let me investigate the current IDE setup, what introspection actually needs to detect, and
  what each parser can deliver.

  Read 1 file, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ No helper exists yet — P3 is greenfield. Importantly, ADR-182 already staked out a position in its
  Consequences: "trait-position detection should reuse the parse tree... Semantic, type-aware data still 
  comes from the P3 Node helper — tree-sitter is syntactic only." So the real question is which side
  trait-composition classification (P3's core job) actually lands on. That hinges on how stories declare
  entities — let me look at the actual source patterns.

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The actual story pattern is the crux here. Entities aren't per-file exports — they're built imperatively at
  runtime inside functions, and trait identity comes from constructor names, not types:

  const nightstand = world.createEntity('nightstand', EntityType.ITEM);
  nightstand.add(new SceneryTrait());
  nightstand.add(new ContainerTrait({...}));
  nightstand.add(new OpenableTrait({ isOpen: false }));

  And rooms go through a story-local createRoom() wrapper (line 54), so new RoomTrait() isn't even visible in
  rooms/index.ts. That detail reshapes the whole question. Let me verify two things before I write this up:
  whether the world-model exposes runtime entity/trait queries, and whether Dungeo organizes source by
  category the way The Alderman does.

  Searched for 1 pattern (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ That settles it. Two decisive findings:

  Dungeo organizes source by region, not by category. white-house.ts, coal-mine.ts etc. each create rooms and
  objects and NPCs together. So the mock's Rooms/Objects/NPCs/Regions categories cut across Dungeo's files —
  directory convention can't produce them (it happens to work for The Alderman, which is the exception, not
  the rule).

  The world-model already exposes exactly what P3 wants at runtime: getAllEntities(),
  hasTrait(TraitType.ROOM), getTrait(RoomTrait), findWhere(...). Ground truth, immune to the
  wrapper-function/alias indirection that defeats static analysis.

  Let me write this up as a findings doc so it's persisted for the ADR.

⏺ Write(docs/work/sharpee-ide/research-20260619-p3-introspection-architecture.md)
  ⎿  Wrote 76 lines to docs/work/sharpee-ide/research-20260619-p3-introspection-architecture.md
      2 # P3 Introspection Architecture — Findings
      3 **Date:** 2026-06-19
        /problems, P6 trait hints), does project introspection belong **Node-side** (TS Compiler API s
        tatic analysis, as the roadmap specced) or **Swift-side** (reuse the tree-sitter parse tree fr
        om ADR-182)?
        s vs. source-positions**, and the right model is a hybrid that uses a *runtime* introspection 
        helper (not static analysis) for semantics and tree-sitter for source positions.
      7 ---
      8
      9 ## What the consumers actually need
     … +115 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Findings written to docs/work/sharpee-ide/research-20260619-p3-introspection-architecture.md. Here's the
  conclusion:

  The question was a false dichotomy

  It's not "tree-sitter vs. TS Compiler API." It's semantics vs. source-positions, and they want different
  things.

  Why the roadmap's plan (Node + TS Compiler API) is the weak choice

  The roadmap assumed P3 could classify each file by its export's trait composition. The actual story code
  defeats that at every step:

  1. Entities aren't exports — they're local consts built imperatively inside createObjects(world), many per
  file.
  2. Trait identity is the constructor name (new ContainerTrait()), not a type — so the TS type system buys
  nothing. The hard part is tracking a local var through its .add() calls, which is identical work in either
  parser.
  3. Trait calls hide behind story-local wrappers — The Alderman's rooms go through createRoom(), so new 
  RoomTrait() never appears in rooms/index.ts.
  4. Category ≠ directory — Dungeo files are per-region (rooms + objects + NPCs intermixed), so the mock's
  categories cut across files.

  Static analysis (either parser) would be approximately reimplementing the engine's own constructor logic.

  The asymmetry that decides it

  The world-model already computes this exactly at runtime — getAllEntities(), hasTrait(TraitType.ROOM),
  getTrait(RoomTrait). Ground truth, immune to wrappers/aliases. The only thing runtime lacks is source
  position.

  Recommendation: hybrid, split by demand

  - Semantics (P3 categories + traits, P7 lints) → a runtime introspection helper: sharpee --introspect 
  {story} dumps the live world as JSON. Still a "Node helper" per roadmap decision #4 — but it runs the 
  engine rather than statically parsing it. The P5 Play panel already hosts a live world in its WKWebView, so
  the same call refreshes over the existing bridge for free.
  - Source positions (P3 navigation, P7 outline jump-to, P6 trait-cursor) → tree-sitter, reusing the editor's
  tree (ADR-182 already says this). A light createEntity('name', …) → file:line index joins runtime entities
  back to source.

  This reframes roadmap decision #4 (Node helper = runtime, not static analysis) and is consistent with
  ADR-182.

  Three things for you to decide
  
  1. Build dependency — OK for the project tree to be empty until first successful build (then live-refresh
  via WKWebView)? Static analysis would run on broken source but give wrong answers.
  2. Manifest source — headless CLI, live WKWebView bridge, or both (cold-open + live refresh)?
  3. ADR? This reverses a recorded roadmap decision and constrains P6/P7 — I think it's ADR-worthy. Want me
  to write it up, or discuss further first?

  Note the --introspect engine entry point is a platform change (packages/), so per your rules that's a
  discussion before I build anything.

✻ Crunched for 2m 48s
  
❯ 1) yes, 2) both, 3) yes, write the ADR                                                                     

  Listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Written: docs/architecture/adrs/adr-184-ide-project-introspection.md (ACCEPTED).

  It records the hybrid decision with all three of your answers baked in:
  - Build-gated tree (empty/last-known-good until first successful build, then live refresh) — in Decision +
  Consequences.
  - Both manifest paths (headless sharpee --introspect CLI for cold open + WKWebView bridge for live refresh)
  against one shared schema.
  - Amends phase-plan decision #4 from "TS Compiler API static analysis" → "runtime introspection," and
  complements ADR-182.

  Acceptance criteria are pinned to the two real stories that proved the point — Dungeo's cross-file region
  bucketing (finding 4) and The Alderman's wrapper-created rooms (finding 3) — neither of which static
  analysis handles, plus a real-path test asserting the manifest against world.getAllEntities().

  The prior IDE ADRs (182/183) both went through /devarch:adr-review and iterated on the score. Want me to
  run it on 184 before we call it final? And should I update phase-plan decision #4 in place with a pointer
  to this ADR so the two don't drift?

  ────────────────────────────────────
  Score: 9/12 checked PASS (2 PARTIAL, 1 FAIL among them); 3 N/A
  Verdict: NEEDS WORK 
  
  To address before implementation:
    1. Write the concrete manifest schema — a TypeScript interface + a JSON example. Pin: how
       category is derived/encoded (trait-set → enum?), and how each entity's traits and the
       fields the IDE consumes (room exits, etc.) are shaped.
    2. Pin the schema's home and invoke rule 8b — one shared wire-type file imported by the
       engine emitter AND the JS bridge, mirrored by the Swift decoder. Name the file/package.
    3. Resolve the entry point: is `--introspect` a flag on the devkit `sharpee` CLI (ADR-180)
       or an engine-package binary? The ADR says "engine" but invokes the devkit command name.

  ---
  Cross-ADR seams (182 ↔ 183 ↔ 184)

  - 182 ↔ 184 — tree-sitter's two jobs (minor, worth a line in 184). 182 establishes one in-code highlight
  query. 184's "light name→location index" is a different tree walk — it scans for createEntity('<name>', …)
  literals, not highlight captures. 184 should say it adds its own query/walk rather than reusing the
  highlight query, so an implementer doesn't conflate them. The semantic boundary itself is consistent: 182
  says "tree-sitter syntactic only, semantics from the P3 helper"; 184 honors and sharpens that exact line.
  No conflict.
  - 183 ↔ 184 — apply 183's wire-type discipline to 184. 183 already shares its wire type (IDecoration via
  text-blocks + sharpee re-export) — the correct pattern. 184 introduces a second wire contract (the
  manifest) but doesn't yet follow the same discipline. This is the concrete fix for 184's gap #2: do for the
  manifest what 183 did for IDecoration. The two ADRs point the same direction; 184 just hasn't named the
  file.
  - 183 ↔ 182 — independent, no seam.

  Bottom line

  182 and 183 are clean and already shipped. 184 is the one to act on — it's architecturally sound (the seam
  checks confirm it fits 182 cleanly), but it's missing the one artifact this review exists to force: the 
  concrete manifest schema, plus the rule-8b shared-type home and the devkit-vs-engine entry-point call. All
  three are quick to nail down.

  Want me to revise ADR-184 now — add a ## Manifest schema section (TS interface + JSON example), name the
  shared wire-type file, and pin the --introspect entry point — then it's READY?

      61 +  `--play`/`--test`), **not** the devkit `./sharpee` build CLI (ADR-180) and **not** a new  
      62 +  engine binary. It reuses the existing `story-loader` (`loadStory`) to construct the world,
      63 +  then walks `world.getAllEntities()` and emits an **entity/trait JSON manifest** (schema be

         +ilds*;

      109  findings 1–4 show it would produce *wrong* answers — a correct-when-available tree beats a

      111  
      112 +### Manifest schema                                                                        
      113 +                                                                                           
      114 +Defined in `@sharpee/ide-protocol`. The manifest is a flat entity list plus a build-status 
      115 +header; the IDE buckets into categories client-side from `category`. Traits are reported as
          + a                                                                                         
      116 +record of trait-type → the small set of fields the IDE actually consumes (not the full trai
          +t                                                                                          
      117 +object — the manifest is a projection, not a world dump).                                  
      118 +                                                                                           
      119 +```ts                                                                                      
      120 +/** Wire contract between `--introspect` / Play bridge and the IDE. No runtime-specific typ
          +es. */                                                                                     
      121 +export interface ProjectManifest {                                                         
      122 +  schemaVersion: 1;                 // bump on any breaking shape change                   
      123 +  story: string;                    // story id (from StoryConfig)                         
      124 +  generatedFrom: 'cli' | 'bridge';  // which path produced this manifest                   
      125 +  entities: EntityNode[];                                                                  
      126 +}                                                                                          
      127 +                                                                                           
      128 +export type EntityCategory = 'room' | 'object' | 'npc' | 'region';                         
      129 +                                                                                           
      130 +export interface EntityNode {                                                              
      131 +  id: string;                       // world entity id                                     
      132 +  displayName: string;              // IdentityTrait name (authored text) — for the tree la
          +bel                                                                                        
      133 +  category: EntityCategory;         // derived from the trait set (see rules)              
      134 +  traits: TraitSummary;             // trait-type → IDE-relevant fields                    
      135 +  source?: SourceRef;               // file:line from the tree-sitter name index; absent if
          + unresolved                                                                                
      136 +}                                                                                          
      137 +                                                                                           
      138 +export interface SourceRef {                                                               
      139 +  file: string;                     // workspace-relative path                             
      140 +  line: number;                     // 1-based; the createEntity('<name>', …) site         
      141 +  resolution: 'exact' | 'scope';    // 'scope' = fell back to enclosing function (non-uniqu
          +e name)                                                                                    
      142 +}                                                                                          
      143 +                                                                                           
      144 +/** Only the fields the IDE renders/lints on. Sparse: a key is present only if the entity h
          +as that trait. */                                                                          
      145 +export interface TraitSummary {                                                            
      146 +  identity?: { description?: string };                                                     
      147 +  room?:     { exits: string[] };                 // exit directions present — drives the "
          +room with no exits" lint                                                                   
      148 +  container?: { openable: boolean; lockable: boolean };  // co-trait lint inputs           
      149 +  [traitType: string]: Record<string, unknown> | undefined;  // forward-compatible: unknown
          + traits pass through                                                                       
      150 +}                                                                                          
      151 +```                                                                                        
      152 +                                                                                           
      153 +**Category derivation** (single rule, runtime trait set — order matters, first match wins):
      154 +                                                                                           
      155 +| Test (on the runtime entity) | `category` |                                              
      156 +|---|---|                                                                                  
      157 +| `hasTrait(TraitType.REGION)` | `region` |                                                
      158 +| `hasTrait(TraitType.ROOM)`   | `room`   |                                                
      159 +| `hasTrait(TraitType.ACTOR)`  | `npc`    |                                                
      160 +| otherwise (has `IdentityTrait`) | `object` |                                             
      161 +                                                                                           
      162 +Doors/exits are not top-level categories (they surface under their room's `exits`). Grammar
          + and                                                                                       
      163 +story settings are not entities and are not in this manifest — the project tree sources tho
          +se                                                                                         
      164 +separately (filesystem for grammar files, `package.json` for settings/deps).               
      165 +                                                                                           
      166 +**JSON example** (one room via The Alderman's `createRoom` wrapper, one openable object):  
      167 +                                                                                           
      168 +```json                                                                                    
      169 +{                                                                                          
      170 +  "schemaVersion": 1,                                                                      
      171 +  "story": "thealderman",                                                                  
      172 +  "generatedFrom": "cli",                                                                  
      173 +  "entities": [                                                                            
      174 +    { "id": "r01", "displayName": "Great Room", "category": "room",                        
      175 +      "traits": { "identity": { "description": "This is the grand foyer…" }, "room": { "exi
          +ts": ["south","east","southeast","west"] } },                                              
      176 +      "source": { "file": "stories/thealderman/src/rooms/index.ts", "line": 54, "resolution
          +": "exact" } },                                                                            
      177 +    { "id": "i07", "displayName": "nightstand", "category": "object",                      
      178 +      "traits": { "identity": {}, "container": { "openable": true, "lockable": false } },  
      179 +      "source": { "file": "stories/thealderman/src/objects/index.ts", "line": 129, "resolut
          +ion": "exact" } }                                                                          
      180 +  ]                                                                                        
      181 +}                                                                                          
      182 +```                                                                                        
      183 +                                                                                           
      184 +Note the room is correctly `category: "room"` despite `new RoomTrait()` never appearing in 
      185 +`rooms/index.ts` (it's inside `createRoom`) — because the category comes from the *runtime*
          + trait                                                                                     
      186 +set, not source. The `source` line points at the `createEntity`/wrapper call site from the 
      187 +tree-sitter name index, joined by `displayName`.                                           
      188 +                                                                                           
      189  ## Consequences
      190  
      191  - **Engine gains a public introspection surface.** `sharpee --introspect {story}` and an

⏺ Now align Consequences and Acceptance with the pinned entry point and new package:

⏺ Update(docs/architecture/adrs/adr-184-ide-project-introspection.md)
  ⎿  Added 9 lines, removed 4 lines
      188  
      189  ## Consequences
      190  
      191 -- **Engine gains a public introspection surface.** `sharpee --introspect {story}` and an   
      192 -  equivalent bridge-exposed call become a supported, versioned output of the platform — a  
      193 -  `packages/` change, discussed and approved before implementation. The manifest schema is 
          -now                                                                                        
      194 -  an interface the IDE depends on; changing it is a wire-contract change.                  
      191 +- **The platform gains a public introspection surface.** The `--introspect` flag on the    
      192 +  platform-bundle CLI and the equivalent bridge-exposed call become a supported, versioned 
          +output                                                                                     
      193 +  — a `packages/` change (`transcript-tester` CLI + the new `@sharpee/ide-protocol` package
          + + the                                                                                     
      194 +  story `browser-entry` bridge), discussed and approved before implementation. The manifest
          + schema                                                                                    
      195 +  is now an interface the IDE depends on; changing it is a wire-contract change gated by   
      196 +  `schemaVersion`.                                                                         
      197 +- **A new types-only package, `@sharpee/ide-protocol`.** Adds one workspace package (and it
          +s six                                                                                      
      198 +  registration points per the new-package checklist). It carries only the manifest wire typ
          +es —                                                                                       
      199 +  no runtime deps — so both the Node emitter and the browser bridge import it cleanly.     
      200  - **No static trait classifier is ever built.** P7 lints ("room with no exits", "lockable
      201    without openable") read the runtime manifest's trait data, not a parsed approximation. Th
           is
      202    removes an entire class of "the analyzer disagrees with the engine" bugs.

⏺ Update(docs/architecture/adrs/adr-184-ide-project-introspection.md)
  ⎿  Added 8 lines, removed 4 lines
      216  
      217  ## Acceptance
      218  
      219 -- `sharpee --introspect {story}` runs the **real built story** and emits a manifest whose  
      220 -  entity set and per-entity trait set match what the engine constructs — verified by a     
      221 -  real-path test that builds a fixture story and asserts the manifest against              
      222 -  `world.getAllEntities()` (no stub of the world model; the system under test is the engine
          -).                                                                                         
      219 +- `node dist/cli/sharpee.js --introspect {story}` runs the **real built story** (via the   
      220 +  existing `loadStory`) and emits a `ProjectManifest` (`schemaVersion: 1`) whose entity set
          + and                                                                                       
      221 +  per-entity trait set match what the engine constructs — verified by a real-path test that
          + loads                                                                                     
      222 +  a fixture story and asserts the manifest against `world.getAllEntities()` (no stub of the
          + world                                                                                     
      223 +  model; the system under test is the engine).                                             
      224 +- The manifest validates against the `@sharpee/ide-protocol` guards; `category` follows the
      225 +  derivation table (a `createRoom`-wrapped Alderman room reports `category: "room"`); unkno
          +wn                                                                                         
      226 +  traits pass through `TraitSummary`'s index signature without dropping the entity.        
      227  - For Dungeo, the manifest buckets rooms, items, NPCs, and regions correctly **across**
      228    region-organized files (finding 4), and for The Alderman classifies wrapper-created rooms
            as
      229    rooms (finding 3) — neither achievable by static directory/export analysis.

⏺ ADR-184 revised. All three review gaps are closed:

  1. Interface contract (was FAIL). Added a ## Manifest schema section with concrete @sharpee/ide-protocol
  types — ProjectManifest, EntityNode, SourceRef, and a sparse TraitSummary (only IDE-consumed fields, with a
  forward-compatible index signature). Plus a category-derivation table (region → room → npc → object, first
  match wins, off the runtime trait set) and a JSON example that demonstrates the key win: The Alderman's
  createRoom-wrapped room reports category: "room" even though new RoomTrait() never appears in its source.

  2. Shared wire-type home (was PARTIAL, rule 8b). Pinned to a new types-only package @sharpee/ide-protocol
  (modeled on text-blocks: types + guards + index, no runtime deps), imported by both emitters — the CLI and
  the JS bridge — with the Swift Codable structs as the mirror. A schema change now breaks both JS sides in
  one compile.

  3. Entry-point ambiguity (was the Open-Questions FAIL). Resolved to an --introspect flag on the
  platform-bundle CLI (packages/transcript-tester/src/cli.ts — the dist/cli/sharpee.js that already owns
  --play/--test), reusing story-loader's loadStory. Explicitly not the devkit ./sharpee build CLI and not a
  new engine binary, with the rationale recorded.

  Consequences and Acceptance were updated to match (new package + its registration cost, schemaVersion-gated
  wire contract, real-path test asserting the manifest against world.getAllEntities()), and the Session
  section records the revision.

  Re-scored against the checklist, the three previously-flagged items now PASS:
  - Interface contracts: PASS — concrete types + JSON example
  - All affected packages named / Boundary contracts: PASS — transcript-tester CLI, @sharpee/ide-protocol,
  story browser-entry bridge, Swift mirror 
  - No unresolved blockers: PASS — schema written, entry point pinned

⏺ @sharpee/ide-protocol is started, on branch ide/p3-introspection. Build artifacts are gitignored.

  What's in the package

  The single shared wire contract from ADR-184, types-only (no runtime deps), modeled on
  @sharpee/text-blocks:

  - src/types.ts — ProjectManifest, EntityNode, EntityCategory, SourceRef, sparse TraitSummary (with the
  forward-compatible index signature), and SCHEMA_VERSION = 1.
  - src/guards.ts — decode-boundary predicates: isProjectManifest (enforces the schemaVersion gate),
  isEntityNode, isEntityCategory, isSourceRef.
  - tests/guards.test.ts — 11 tests, all green. Cover the version gate, the four categories, SourceRef
  resolution modes, malformed-entity rejection, and the unknown-trait passthrough (an entity carrying a
  future trait is not dropped — the ADR's forward-compat acceptance point).
  - Package/tsconfig/tsconfig.esm/vitest config per repo convention. Clean CJS+ESM build.

  Registration — done vs. deferred

  - Done (low-risk): ts-forge.config.json (tsf compiles it) and root package.json workspaces (pnpm links it).
  Verified by the standalone build + tests.
  - Deferred intentionally: the consumer-coupled wiring — PLATFORM_PACKAGES/BUNDLE_ALIASES in
  devkit/src/repo.ts (carries the build.sh parity-gate warning) and the @sharpee/transcript-tester
  dependency. Those belong in the next step, when the --introspect emitter actually imports the package, so
  they don't drift speculatively against the parity gate.
  - Not wired (by design): I kept it out of the @sharpee/sharpee public surface — unlike text-blocks (which
  is authoring API via IDecoration), this is IDE-tooling wire that story authors never import. Flag this if
  you'd rather it be re-exported through the aggregate package.

  Behavior/test note (DEVARCH)

  The guards are pure predicates (no side effects), so no mutation-verification applies. The suite grades
  GREEN — every assertion checks real predicate output across valid/invalid inputs, including the two ADR
  rejection conditions.

  Next step in P3 is the emitter: the --introspect flag in transcript-tester/src/cli.ts that runs loadStory,
  walks world.getAllEntities(), applies the category-derivation table, and emits a ProjectManifest. Want me
  to commit this package first, or go straight into the emitter?

         -low).

      201 -  `schemaVersion`.

  What changed (correcting course)

  You were right that I'd forgotten the ADR-180 consolidation. I reverted the wrong-layer
  transcript-tester/cli.ts edits entirely and put things where they belong:

  - buildManifest → @sharpee/bootstrap (src/introspect.ts) — the shared loader/assembly layer that the
  bundle, transcript-tester, and devkit all import. It already owns assembleGame/resolveStoryModulePath and
  depends on world-model, so it's the natural home. Exported from the barrel; @sharpee/ide-protocol added as
  a dep + tsconfig reference.
  - --introspect flag → scripts/bundle-entry.js — the actual CLI entry compiled into dist/cli/sharpee.js
  (alongside --play/--test/--world-json), calling bootstrap.buildManifest. JSON to stdout, status to stderr.
  - devkit/src/repo.ts — added ide-protocol to PLATFORM_PACKAGES + BUNDLE_ALIASES so the bundle resolves it
  (no parity gate — build.sh is retired).

  story through the production code path (no stub):

  Behavior Statement — buildManifest(world, story, generatedFrom)
  - DOES: returns a ProjectManifest with one EntityNode per world entity except the player and any door/exit
  entity; each node's category is derived from its trait set (region→room→npc→object, first match wins) and
  its traits is a sparse projection (identity.description, room.exits, container.openable/lockable).
  - WHEN: called on a constructed world.
  - BECAUSE: the IDE tree and P7 lints consume this as ground truth.
  - REJECTS WHEN: n/a (pure projection); excludes player + door/exit, emits no source.

  the Node loader. So before any bridge code, we have to decide where buildManifest lives so both the CLI

       1 // IntrospectionRunner.swift

       3 // its stdout, and decodes the result into a ProjectManifest. Mirrors BuildRunner's
       4 // Process/pipe machinery, but buffers stdout to completion (the manifest is one JSON

       6 // only to enrich a non-zero-exit error.

⏺ Crucial: rooms go through the createRoom(world, name, …) wrapper where the name reaches createEntity as a
  variable, not a literal — the literal 'West of House' lives at the createRoom('West of House', …) call. So
  indexing only createEntity literals would miss all wrapper-created rooms (finding 3 again). The robust fix:
  index string literals that are call arguments (any callee) — catches both createEntity('small mailbox', …)
  and createRoom('West of House', …), while description args won't exact-match a short display name. Let me
  read SyntaxHighlighter for the exact tree-sitter API to reuse:

  The tree-sitter source-position index is built

  EntitySourceIndex.swift — scans a story's .ts/.tsx with the ADR-182 tree-sitter grammar, capturing
  string-literal call arguments ((arguments (string) @arg)) → name → [file:line], then joins to the manifest

  EntitySourceIndex.swift — scans a story's .ts/.tsx with the ADR-182 tree-sitter grammar, capturing
  string-literal call arguments ((arguments (string) @arg)) → name → [file:line], then joins to the manifest
  by display name:

  - Direct entities — createEntity('small mailbox', …) → indexed at its line ✅
  - Wrapper-created rooms — createRoom('West of House', …) → the literal at the wrapper call is captured, so
  rooms whose name reaches createEntity as a variable still resolve (this was the finding-3 gap) ✅
  - Nested option strings excluded — new IdentityTrait({ name: 'x' }) is not a direct call argument, so it
  doesn't pollute the index ✅
  - Resolution — unique match → .exact; duplicate name → first, marked .scope; no match → nil (graceful, per
  ADR)

  The 7 tests are real-path (actual tree-sitter parses through the vendored grammar), covering all of the
  above plus line accuracy, per-file paths, and the manifest join.

  Wired end to end

  On a successful build: IntrospectionRunner → decode manifest → EntitySourceIndex.build →
  annotating(manifest) → setManifest. Double-clicking an entity now calls openEntitySource, which opens its
  file:line (taught to handle the index's absolute paths). The click-to-source path that was inert last
  commit is now live.
