✻ Sautéed for 1m 43s

❯ one thing about build - the IDE should track what needs to be rebuilt and use --skip-until                 

⏺ Let me ground this in how --skip actually works today before proposing the IDE change, since "track what
  needs rebuilding" depends on the skip semantics. Let me check the devkit build CLI and the IDE's current
  BuildSettings.skipFrom:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== devkit build: how --skip is parsed + what it does ===" && grep -rn
      "skip\|Skip\|skipFrom\|skipUntil\|skip-until\|skipPla…)
  ⎿  === devkit build: how --skip is parsed + what it does ===
     packages/devkit/src/commands/browser.ts:82:  // Skip dotfiles to match build.sh's `cp "$ASSETS_DIR"
     /*` (bash glob excludes dotfiles,
     … +70 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Now I understand the mechanics: --skip <pkg> means skip every platform package before <pkg>, then build 
  from there — so the flag name is --skip but the semantics are exactly your "skip-until."
  BuildSettings.skipFrom already plumbs it, but it's a manual setting today. You want the IDE to compute it
  automatically from what changed.

  This is a non-trivial feature with a couple of real design forks, so let me check two things before
  proposing — whether the platform can be skipped entirely (the common author case: only story files
⏺ Confirmed the mechanics, and there's a useful detail: in buildPlatform, if skipTo matches no package the
  skipping flag never flips off, so the entire platform gets skipped — meaning "story-only" rebuilds are
  already expressible (though only via an unmatched sentinel today).

  This is a non-trivial, cross-cutting feature, so here's the plan before I touch anything:

  Goal

  The IDE computes --skip <pkg> automatically so a build resumes from the earliest changed platform package —
  or skips the platform entirely when only the story changed (the common author case: nothing under
  packages/ changed).

  How --skip works (confirmed)

  --skip X skips every platform package before X in dependency order (PLATFORM_PACKAGES), then builds X → end
  → story → bundle. So it's exactly "skip-until X." The story always rebuilds.

  Proposed design

  1. Record build state — on each successful build, persist (per project) the git HEAD + timestamp. Extend
  BuildSettingsStore/SessionState.
  2. Detect changes — before a build, git diff --name-only <last-built-commit> HEAD + working-tree status,
  filtered to packages/**, to get the changed package dirs. Git is precise and repo-aware (beats mtime
  scanning).
  3. Compute the skip target — map changed dirs onto the dependency order; the earliest changed = --skip.
  None changed → skip whole platform.
  4. Feed it in — set BuildSettings.skipFrom automatically (it already flows to --skip), shown in the build
  UI so the author sees what's being skipped.

  Two decisions — both touch packages/ (devkit), so I want your call first

  A. Where the dependency order lives. The IDE needs PLATFORM_PACKAGES order to pick the earliest changed
  package. Options:
  - devkit emits it — add sharpee build --print-order (JSON); IDE reads it → never drifts. (Recommend.)
  - static Swift copy — duplicate the list in the IDE; simple but can drift from repo.ts.

  B. Skip-whole-platform mechanism. Today an unmatched --skip value silently skips everything — which is also
  a typo footgun. Options:
  - Add an explicit --skip-platform (or --story-only) flag to devkit — clear and safe. (Recommend.)
  - Reuse the unmatched-sentinel behavior — no devkit change, but fragile/implicit.

  My recommendation: devkit emits the order (A) + add an explicit skip-platform flag (B) — two small,
  well-contained devkit additions — then the IDE change is pure logic (git diff → order → flag) and fully
  unit-testable.

  Want me to go with that, or keep it IDE-only (static order + sentinel, no devkit changes)? And should I
  scope v1 to coarse (only-story-changed → skip platform; any packages change → full build) or go straight to
  the fine-grained earliest-package skip you described?

✻ Cogitated for 2m 3s

❯ would it be better if the IDE leveraged the npm packages and never had to rebuild the platform?            

⏺ That's the better question — and architecturally, yes, for the audience the IDE is actually for. Let me
  ground one thing first: how ready the standalone/npm path is, since that decides whether this is "do now"
  or "blocked."

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== standalone build path (out-of-repo story) ===" && grep -n "inRepo\|standalone\|Phase
      U2\|node_modules\|published\|regist…)
  ⎿  === standalone build path (out-of-repo story) ===

     === are @sharpee/* actually published to npm? (version + private) ===
     … +9 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== standalone author build: what it does ===" && sed -n '1,30p'
      packages/devkit/src/standalone/build.ts…)
  ⎿  === standalone author build: what it does ===
     /**
      * CLI: sharpee build
     … +31 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Yes — verified the key facts, and the answer is a clear yes for the IDE's actual audience, and it's not
  even blocked:

  - npm view @sharpee/sharpee version → 1.0.0 is live on the public registry. The platform is already
  published.
  - devkit/src/standalone/build.ts already exists: it builds a .sharpee bundle + browser client from a story 
  project, consuming installed packages — no platform build.

  Why npm-leverage is the right model

  The platform-rebuild problem is an artifact of how the IDE builds today: it treats a story as living inside
  the sharpee monorepo, built against source via ./sharpee build, which recompiles every @sharpee/* from
  packages/*/src. That's the contributor workflow.

  For the author workflow — what an IDE for IF authors should be — the story is its own project: package.json
  depends on @sharpee/sharpee@1.0.0, and:
  - the platform sits prebuilt in node_modules → never rebuilt;
  - "build" = compile the story's src/ + emit the bundle (the standalone build already does exactly this);
  - platform upgrades are deliberate (npm update), matching your "author owns lifecycle / no implicit
  backcompat" stance.

  So the entire --skip/change-tracking machinery I proposed evaporates for authors — there's nothing to skip
  because there's no platform build.

  The real shape: two audiences
  
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┬────────────┬───────────────────┐
  │                                               │      Project      │   Build    │ --skip relevance  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────┤
  │ Authors (IDE's audience)                      │ standalone, deps  │ story src/ │ none — no         │
  │                                               │ from npm          │  only      │ platform build    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────┤
  │ Contributors (you, editing platform +         │ the monorepo      │ source     │ the only place    │
  │ stories/dungeo,thealderman)                   │                   │ rebuild    │ --skip helps      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┴────────────┴───────────────────┘
  
  The IDE currently only understands the monorepo (PackageDetector scans packages/). It's not project-mode 
  aware.

  Recommendation — reframe the work
  
  Instead of building --skip change-tracking (which only helps the contributor case), the higher-leverage
  move is project-mode detection in the IDE:
  - Standalone story project → npm/installed-platform build, platform never rebuilt. This is the default for
  authors.
  - Monorepo → keep the source build; --skip becomes an optional contributor nicety (or skip it entirely).

  One gap to close for author mode

  Introspection (--introspect) currently lives only on the monorepo bundle (scripts/bundle-entry.js). For
  standalone projects it must run via the installed/global sharpee CLI. The good news: buildManifest lives in
  @sharpee/bootstrap (published), so the capability ships — but the --introspect flag isn't on the
  standalone/global CLI yet (CLAUDE.md notes the global sharpee command is ADR-180 Phase U2). That's the
  wiring to add so the project tree works in author mode.

  This is a strategic call (which workflow the IDE optimizes, mode detection, build-per-mode) — I'd treat it
  as ADR-worthy. Want me to write that ADR (IDE project-mode + npm-platform build, demoting --skip to the
  monorepo path), or dig into the standalone introspection wiring first?

✻ Cooked for 1m 30s

❯ explain                                                                                                    

⏺ Here's the mechanism concretely — what "leverage the npm packages" actually means and why it removes the
  platform build.

  What the platform is in each mode

  Today — the IDE builds stories inside the monorepo (source mode):

  A story like stories/dungeo does import { WorldModel } from '@sharpee/world-model'. Inside the monorepo,
  the pnpm workspace resolves that import to packages/world-model/ — the source. So ./sharpee build dungeo
  does:

  recompile @sharpee/core → text-blocks → world-model → … → engine → bundle   (the platform, every build)
  then compile the story
  then assemble the bundle

  It recompiles ~25 platform packages from TypeScript every time, in dependency order, because in a workspace
  their dist/ can be stale relative to their src/. That's the slow part, and it's what --skip was trying to
  avoid.

  npm mode — the story is its own project that depends on the platform:

  A standalone game is an ordinary npm package, e.g. ~/my-game/:

  npm mode — the story is its own project that depends on the platform:

  A standalone game is an ordinary npm package, e.g. ~/my-game/:

  { "dependencies": { "@sharpee/sharpee": "^1.0.0" } }

  npm install pulls the already-compiled @sharpee/* packages (their published dist/ JavaScript — verified
  live at 1.0.0) into node_modules/. Now import { WorldModel } from '@sharpee/world-model' resolves to
  prebuilt JS on disk, not source.

  "Build" becomes just:

  compile ~/my-game/src/*.ts  →  JS   (imports resolve to node_modules prebuilt dist)
  emit the .sharpee bundle + browser client

  The platform is never recompiled — for the same reason a web app doesn't recompile React when you build it.
  It's a dependency: immutable, prebuilt, installed once. The author didn't write it and can't change it, so
  there's nothing to rebuild.

  Why this dissolves the --skip problem

  --skip/change-tracking exists to avoid recompiling unchanged platform packages. In npm mode there are no
  platform packages to compile in the first place — so the optimization is moot. Build time collapses from
  "the whole platform" to "one small story package" (seconds).

  Introspection / Play still work

  - Introspect needs a CLI that loads the built story and dumps the world. buildManifest lives in
  @sharpee/bootstrap — which is published — so the capability ships in node_modules. The only gap: the
  --introspect flag is currently only on the monorepo bundle (scripts/bundle-entry.js); it needs exposing on
  the installed/standalone CLI so author projects can call it.
  - Play is the same browser bundle, built from the story + the node_modules platform.

  The catch — why the source build doesn't go away

  You personally edit the platform itself (engine, stdlib) and the reference stories (stories/dungeo,
  thealderman) that live inside the monorepo. When you change the engine, you want it rebuilt — so the source
  build stays for that workflow. npm mode is for authors who only write stories against a fixed platform
  version.

  How the IDE picks

  It already has the detectors (WorkspaceRoot, PackageDetector, StoryDetector). On opening a folder:

  - has packages/ + pnpm-workspace.yaml → monorepo / contributor mode → source build (where --skip would
  help)
  - is a story project (package.json deps on @sharpee/*, a node_modules) → author mode → npm build, platform
  never rebuilt

  So the net: for the IDE's real audience the answer is yes — adopt the npm/installed-platform build and the
  platform-rebuild question disappears entirely; the source build + --skip survives only as the contributor
  path.

✻ Worked for 46s

❯ the ide is not meant for monorepo work - it's meant for authors outside the monorepo                       

⏺ Got it — that's a real scope correction, and it simplifies everything. Let me check how a standalone
  project is actually built/invoked so my answer is concrete rather than hand-wavy:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== does @sharpee/devkit expose a 'sharpee' bin? ===" && grep -n "\"bin\"" -A3
      packages/devkit/package.json…)
  ⎿  === does @sharpee/devkit expose a 'sharpee' bin? ===
     7:  "bin": {
     8-    "sharpee": "./dist/cli.js"
     … +21 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ That clarifies the whole thing — single audience, no monorepo mode. And the infra already supports it: I
  checked, and the standalone build (devkit/src/standalone/build.ts) compiles only the story (npx tsc +
  esbuild … --external:@sharpee/*), treating every @sharpee/* as external and resolved from node_modules. The
  platform is never compiled — exactly the model. @sharpee/devkit even ships the sharpee bin, so an author
  project just needs it as a dep.

  So here's the corrected picture and what it means for the work.

  What's now dead

  --skip / change-tracking — drop it entirely. It's a monorepo-source-build concept. The IDE never rebuilds
  the platform, so there's nothing to skip. That whole thread is moot.

  What the IDE actually builds (author mode)

  An author opens ~/my-game/ (outside the monorepo). It has @sharpee/sharpee + @sharpee/devkit as deps. The
  IDE runs the project's installed sharpee build (via npx/node_modules/.bin/global), which compiles src/
  against the prebuilt platform in node_modules. Build = seconds, one package.

  What I built that's now mis-aimed

  Honestly, a few pieces I just landed assume the monorepo — they were a pragmatic stepping stone, but they
  need realigning to author mode:

  ┌───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┐
  │             Piece             │              Currently              │           Should be            │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ BuildRunner                   │ ./sharpee build <story> at the      │ sharpee build in the author    │
  │                               │ monorepo repoRoot                   │ project dir (installed bin)    │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ IntrospectionRunner           │ node dist/cli/sharpee.js            │ sharpee introspect from the    │
  │                               │ --introspect (monorepo bundle)      │ project's installed CLI        │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ buildSucceeded                │ resolves story name →               │ the open project root is the   │
  │                               │ stories/|tutorials/ subdir          │ story                          │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ StoryDetector/PackageDetector │ scan packages//stories/             │ one project = one story        │
  └───────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┘

  What survives unchanged

  The consumer/render stack is mode-agnostic and still good: ProjectManifest decode, ProjectStructure, the
  tree views, the Files | Structure toggle, and EntitySourceIndex (it scans the project's own src/ either
  way).

  The one real gap
  
  I put --introspect on the monorepo bundle (scripts/bundle-entry.js). For author projects it has to live on
  the devkit sharpee CLI (dist/cli.js) — a sharpee introspect command that loads the project's compiled story
  (dist/index.js) + the node_modules platform and dumps the manifest. buildManifest is in
  @sharpee/bootstrap, which is published, so devkit can import it — small addition.

  Recommendation

  This is the IDE's foundational stance, so it's ADR-worthy: the IDE is a standalone IF authoring tool; it 
  operates on npm-based story projects and never builds the platform. The ADR would record the single-mode
  decision, kill --skip, and define the build/introspect invocation against the installed CLI.

  Want me to write that ADR, or go straight to adding sharpee introspect to the devkit CLI (the gap that
  unblocks author-mode introspection)?

  It records the single decision — the IDE operates only on standalone npm-based story projects and never 
  builds the platform — and follows the implications through:

  - Project model: one project = one story (the open folder); no packages//stories/ scanning, no monorepo
  mode.

  node_modules. --skip/change-tracking is formally abandoned (it only ever helped the monorepo build).

  IDE decoder is unchanged.

  The one substantive gap (both FAILs are the same root issue)

  The author project's dependency surface is unspecified — and it's load-bearing.

  Stories import platform sub-packages directly: Dungeo/The Alderman do import { RoomTrait } from 
  Verdict: NEEDS WORK

  The one substantive gap (both FAILs are the same root issue)

  The author project's dependency surface is unspecified — and it's load-bearing.

  Stories import platform sub-packages directly: Dungeo/The Alderman do import { RoomTrait } from
  '@sharpee/world-model', import { GameEngine } from '@sharpee/engine', etc. For author mode to work:
  - sharpee build externalizes @sharpee/* → those modules must resolve from the project's node_modules.
  - sharpee introspect requires the compiled story at runtime → same.

  So: does @sharpee/sharpee (the umbrella the ADR names as the author dependency) make @sharpee/world-model,
  @sharpee/engine, … resolvable as their own module specifiers? If it only re-exports their symbols under
  @sharpee/sharpee, then a story importing @sharpee/world-model won't resolve unless that package is a direct
  dependency. The ADR doesn't pin this, and it determines whether sharpee init scaffolds one umbrella dep or
  the full @sharpee/* set.

  Fix: add a short "Author project dependency surface" subsection stating either (a) stories import only from
  @sharpee/sharpee (umbrella re-exports everything, single dep), or (b) the project depends on each
  @sharpee/* it imports, and sharpee init scaffolds that set. Whichever is true today should be verified
  against an actual sharpee init output. This also resolves the "no unresolved blockers" FAIL.

  Two minor tightenings (not blockers)

  - Name the world-assembly reuse: sharpee introspect should reuse bootstrap.assembleGame (as ADR-184's
  monorepo path does) rather than re-implementing world construction — make that explicit so an implementer
  doesn't fork it.
  - Publish pinning: note that the republished @sharpee/devkit's @sharpee/bootstrap dependency range must
  target the new bootstrap version (so a fresh npm install pulls buildManifest), and likewise
  bootstrap→ide-protocol.

  Everything else is solid — the publish plan, the test strategy (real installed-CLI path), and the decisive
  single-mode scope are strong.

  Want me to (a) verify what @sharpee/sharpee actually re-exports / what sharpee init scaffolds, then add the
  dependency-surface subsection and the two tightenings to ADR-185 → re-review to READY, or (b) just apply
  the edits as written?

  dep) that would have silently blocked author-mode build and introspect — now pinned as required work.

❯ we removed npx in 1.0 - the template should actually be built into the npm packages somehow and be current 
  for 1.0 and the IDE                                                                                        

⏺ That's a meaningful correction — and it means the template fix in ADR-185 is wrong as written (it shouldn't
  just bump a literal version or keep npx). Let me verify the real mechanics before I rewrite that section:
  where the template ships from, how init injects values, and where npx appears.

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== does devkit ship the templates? (its package.json files + where templates live) ===" && grep
      -n "\"files\"\|templates\|\…)
  ⎿  === does devkit ship the templates? (its package.json files + where templates live) ===
     3:  "version": "1.0.0",
     26:  "files": [
     … +26 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Verified — and it confirms your point. Three real findings:

  1. The template isn't reliably shipped in devkit. Templates live under packages/sharpee/templates/story/,
  but init.ts (in @sharpee/devkit) looks for them relative to its own dist. So a published @sharpee/devkit
  may not carry them at all — that's the "built into the npm package somehow" gap.
  2. npx is all over the standalone path — the template script (npx sharpee build-browser), plus
  build.ts/build-browser.ts/init.ts/test-npm.ts (npx tsc, npx esbuild, npx serve, npx transcript-test). Stale
  against the 1.0 "no npx" decision.
  3. No version injection — init only replaces STORY_ID/TITLE/AUTHOR/DESCRIPTION; the @sharpee/sharpee
  version is a hardcoded ^0.9.61-beta literal, so it can't track 1.0.

  Let me confirm two more things before rewriting the ADR section — whether devkit's files ships templates,
  and where a current version could be injected from:

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== devkit package.json files + any prepublish/copy-template step ===" && sed -n '/"files"/,/]/p'
      packages/devkit/package.js…)
  ⎿  === devkit package.json files + any prepublish/copy-template step ===
       "files": [
         "dist",
     … +23 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ All confirmed — and it's worse than a stale literal:

  - devkit's files = ["dist", "fixtures"] — templates are not shipped, and there's no
  packages/devkit/templates. So a published @sharpee/devkit@1.0.0 carries no template — sharpee init from npm
  would fail to find it. That's the "built into the npm package" gap, literally.
  - No version injection (only STORY_ID/TITLE/AUTHOR/DESCRIPTION are substituted).
  - The IDE has no scaffold path — it only opens projects (the init grep hits were incidental).

  Let me rewrite the ADR's scaffold section to the correct approach — devkit owns and ships the template,
  injects the current version, drops npx, and the IDE uses the same scaffold:

⏺ ADR-185 updated to your corrected model. The scaffold approach is now:

  - devkit owns and ships the template — move it into @sharpee/devkit and add to its files, so a published
  devkit (which owns init) actually carries it. (Today it ships only dist+fixtures → sharpee init from npm
  finds nothing.)
  - Version injected at init, not hardcoded — a placeholder filled from devkit's own release version (devkit
  + platform publish in lockstep), so a scaffolded project always pins the matching @sharpee/sharpee and
  @sharpee/devkit. No ^0.9.61-beta drift.
  - No npx (1.0 decision) — template scripts call sharpee/tsc directly via local devDeps; the straggler npx 
  tsc/esbuild/serve/transcript-test in build.ts/build-browser.ts/init.ts/test-npm.ts are removed.
  - The IDE uses the same scaffold — "New Story" runs the installed sharpee init; build/introspect invoke the
  project's local sharpee bin. One scaffold definition, shipped in devkit, serves both the CLI and the IDE.

  This reflows through Affected packages, Publishing, Acceptance, and the Session note. The two earlier
  review FAILs stay resolved (dependency surface specified; no hidden blockers), so the verdict holds at
  READY — the change here makes the scaffold work correct and concrete rather than the naive "bump a
  literal."

  One honest note: this widens the implementation scope of ADR-185's follow-up — it now includes a devkit
  scaffold rework (own/ship template + version injection) and an npx purge across the standalone toolchain,
  on top of the introspect command + 3-package publish + tools/ide realignment. All captured in the ADR.

  ADR-185 is still uncommitted (along with the earlier ADR-184 revisions and the memory edit from this
  session). Want me to commit the ADR now?

⏺ Fixed — all three .scope references in ADR-184 now match what EntitySourceIndex actually does:

  - schema comment → 'exact' = unique match; 'scope' = name occurs at multiple sites, first reported

  This drops the misleading "fell back to the enclosing function" language (which the code never did). The
  implementation behavior — locations.count == 1 ? .exact : .scope, returning the first location, nil if none

⏺ Confirmed: @sharpee/sharpee depends on neither devkit nor bootstrap. So:

  No — you don't need to publish @sharpee/sharpee

  The introspect feature ships entirely through the CLI dependency chain, which the umbrella isn't part of:

  author project
  ├── @sharpee/sharpee      (runtime — UNCHANGED, no republish)
  └── @sharpee/devkit@1.0.1  (devDep, the CLI)
          └── @sharpee/bootstrap@1.0.1   (pulled transitively)
                  └── @sharpee/ide-protocol@1.0.0  (pulled transitively)

  bootstrap loads automatically — the author never names it; @sharpee/devkit@1.0.1 pins it (workspace:*
  publishes as exact 1.0.1), so installing devkit brings bootstrap@1.0.1 + ide-protocol with it.

  devkit "latest" depends on the project's declared range — with a caret (^1.0.0), a fresh npm install or npm
  update @sharpee/devkit resolves to 1.0.1. An existing lockfile pins until npm update (consistent with
  "authors update on their own schedule").

  Two things to get right

  1. Publish in dependency order: ide-protocol → bootstrap@1.0.1 → devkit@1.0.1. Each dependency must already
  be on npm before the dependent publishes (else the pinned range resolves to nothing).
  2. New scaffolded projects still need @sharpee/devkit added to the template (the scaffold rework in
  ADR-185) — today the template declares no devkit, so sharpee init projects wouldn't have the CLI at all.
  Existing author projects just add devkit + npm update.

⏺ Committed and pushed to main (5a40ca26).

  To recap your question: @sharpee/sharpee does not need republishing. The introspect feature reaches author
  projects purely through @sharpee/devkit@1.0.1 → (transitively) @sharpee/bootstrap@1.0.1 →
  @sharpee/ide-protocol@1.0.0. devkit pins bootstrap exactly, so bootstrap rides along automatically; the
  author only ever declares @sharpee/devkit, and a caret range picks up 1.0.1 on npm update.

  Done so far on ADR-185: the sharpee introspect command (verified real-path against Dungeo), with devkit +
  bootstrap bumped to 1.0.1.

  Remaining before authors can actually use it:
  1. Scaffold rework — add @sharpee/devkit to the sharpee init template (it's absent today), ship the
  template inside devkit, inject the version, drop npx.
  2. Publish in order: ide-protocol → bootstrap@1.0.1 → devkit@1.0.1.

      node_modules/@davidcornelson/tsf/dist…)

     ts-forge publish — Publish workspace packages to npm from staging directory.
     … +26 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
 
⏺ That answers it cleanly — tsf publishes per-package and in dependency order. Here's the workflow for your
  three packages.

  The two-step flow

  # 1. Stage all packages to ~/.tsf-publish/ (publish reads tarballs from there)
  tsf build --npm

  # 2. Publish — pick ONE of these targeting options:
  tsf publish --changed --dry-run     # dry-run first: shows exactly what would publish
  tsf publish --changed               # real publish

⏺ That answers it cleanly — tsf publishes per-package and in dependency order. Here's the workflow for your
  three packages.

  The two-step flow

  # 1. Stage all packages to ~/.tsf-publish/ (publish reads tarballs from there)
  tsf build --npm

  # 2. Publish — pick ONE of these targeting options:
  tsf publish --changed --dry-run     # dry-run first: shows exactly what would publish
  tsf publish --changed               # real publish

  Use --changed — it does exactly what you want

  tsf publish --changed = "only packages whose version is greater than what's on the npm registry." Since you
  bumped devkit → 1.0.1 and bootstrap → 1.0.1 (both > the published 1.0.0), and ide-protocol is unpublished,
  --changed selects exactly those three — and tsf publishes them in dependency order automatically
  (ide-protocol → bootstrap → devkit), which is the ordering constraint we discussed. No manual list, no
  ordering to get right.

  If you'd rather be explicit:
  tsf publish --packageList ide-protocol,bootstrap,devkit
  (still resolved in dependency order regardless of the list order).

  Notes

  - Tag: default is latest — which is what you want, since the platform's 1.0.0 is on latest and authors'
  ^1.0.0 ranges pick up 1.0.1. (verify uses --tag beta --dry-run; that's staging verification, not a
  release.)
  - Prereq: tsf build --npm must run first (publish packs from its ~/.tsf-publish/ staging), and you need to
  be npm login'd with publish rights to the @sharpee scope (npm whoami to check).
  - This is tsf, not raw npm publish — it handles the workspace:* → concrete-version rewriting so
  devkit@1.0.1 pins bootstrap@1.0.1 correctly.

  I can run the dry-run for you (it's safe — tsf build --npm then tsf publish --changed --dry-run) to confirm
  it selects exactly those three before you do the real publish. Want me to? I won't run the actual publish
  — that's irreversible and needs your npm auth, so it's yours to execute.

⏺ Update(packages/devkit/src/standalone/init.ts)
  ⎿  Added 23 lines
      19    storyTitle: string;
      20    author: string;
      21    description: string;
      22 +  /** Injected `@sharpee/sharpee` dependency range. */                                      
      23 +  sharpeeRange: string;                                                                     
      24 +  /** Injected `@sharpee/devkit` dependency range. */                                       
      25 +  devkitRange: string;                                                                      
      26  }
      27  
      28  /**
      29 + * Dependency ranges to inject into a scaffold, derived from this devkit's own              
      30 + * version (so a project pins the platform line this CLI shipped with — never a             
      31 + * stale literal). The platform (`@sharpee/sharpee`) is pinned to the major line            
      32 + * (its patch may lag devkit's); `@sharpee/devkit` is pinned to its own current             
      33 + * version so the scaffold gets an introspect-capable CLI.                                  
      34 + */                                                                                         
      35 +function platformRanges(): { sharpeeRange: string; devkitRange: string } {                  
      36 +  let version = '1.0.0';                                                                    
      37 +  try {                                                                                     
      38 +    const pkgPath = path.join(__dirname, '..', '..', 'package.json');                       
      39 +    version = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(pkgPath, 'utf-8')).version || version;             
      40 +  } catch {                                                                                 
      41 +    /* fall back to the major-1 line */                                                     
      42 +  }                                                                                         
      43 +  const major = version.split('.')[0];                                                      
      44 +  return { sharpeeRange: `^${major}.0.0`, devkitRange: `^${version}` };                     
      45 +}                                                                                           
      46 +                                                                                            
      47 +/**                                                                                         
      48   * Prompt user for input
      49   */
      50  async function prompt(question: string, defaultValue?: string): Promise<string> {

  Root cause

  devkit was missing a per-package ts-forge.json with { "assets": ["templates/**"] } — that's tsf's mechanism
  for staging non-dist content (tsf deletes the files array, so files alone ships nothing). sharpee had this
  file; devkit didn't. So devkit@1.0.2 shipped code-only, and sharpee init from npm was still broken. Now
  fixed with packages/devkit/ts-forge.json.

  devkit@1.0.2 shipped broken — no story template — because tsf deletes the package.json files array during

  templates. Verified: tsf build --npm --package devkit now stages templates/story into
  ~/.tsf-publish/sharpee/devkit/.

  files"), and saved a memory so this doesn't bite a third time (it already cost us on ide-protocol and
  devkit templates).

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Verification done — and it caught two real issues that local testing couldn't. Here's the full picture.

  ✅ The author flow works end-to-end (published packages)

  npm i @sharpee/devkit → sharpee init (template shipped) → npm i (platform pulled) → tsc build → sharpee 
  introspect → valid ProjectManifest. The scaffold-shipping fix is confirmed in production.

  ⚠️  Finding A — version injection falls back to ^1.0.0 in the published layout

  The scaffold injected @sharpee/devkit": "^1.0.0", not ^1.0.3. Cause: platformRanges() reads
  __dirname/../../package.json, which is right for the monorepo (dist/standalone/ nesting) but wrong for the
  published package (tsf flattens dist, so standalone/ is at the root → ../../ overshoots the package) → the
  try/catch falls back to the literal '1.0.0'.

  Functionally fine (^1.0.0 resolves to the latest 1.0.x = 1.0.3), but it's the stale-literal problem
  relocated into a fallback, and it'd break on a future major bump. Fix: make the package.json lookup robust
  (try both paths, like TEMPLATES_DIR already does). Needs a devkit 1.0.4.

  ⚠️  Finding B — the starter story categorizes as object (more significant)

  The emitted manifest:
  player    → category: object, traits: {}
  start-room → category: object, traits: {}
  Cause: the starter template's index.ts creates entities with createEntity('start-room', 'room') + raw
  attributes, and never adds RoomTrait/IdentityTrait. buildManifest categorizes by trait presence
  (hasTrait(ROOM)), so a typed-but-traitless "room" lands in object with empty traits. (Also the story's
  createPlayer entity leaks past the player-exclusion.)

  This is really two things:
  1. The starter template isn't idiomatic — a "room" with no RoomTrait isn't a functioning Sharpee room (no
  exits/behavior). So the official scaffold currently produces a non-working story and a mis-categorized
  tree. That's the bigger problem.
  2. buildManifest could be more robust — fall back to EntityType (the 'room'/'actor' type arg) and
  attributes when traits are absent, so even attribute-style stories categorize sensibly. (Would need a
  bootstrap republish.)

  Recommendation

  - Finding B is the priority — fix the starter template to be idiomatic (add IdentityTrait + RoomTrait,
  connect via exits), which makes a fresh project actually playable and correctly categorized. Optionally add
  the EntityType fallback to buildManifest for robustness.
  - Finding A — fold the version-path fix into the same devkit republish.

  Both want a devkit 1.0.4 (template + path fix); B's robustness option also wants a bootstrap bump. Want me
  to do the starter-template fix + the version-path fix (A + B-template) together, and separately decide on
  the buildManifest fallback?

✻ Churned for 3m 38s

⏺ Critical discovery for B: the @sharpee/sharpee umbrella doesn't export the trait classes — only TraitType,
  StoryInfoTrait, WorldModel/IFEntity (types). No RoomTrait, IdentityTrait, or EntityType. That's why the 
  starter template uses createEntity(name, 'room') + attributes — the trait classes simply aren't reachable
  from the umbrella an author depends on.

  So B-template can't be made idiomatic in isolation. Let me do Finding A now (it's unblocked), then surface
  B's fork.

  Finding A — robust version-path in platformRanges() (handles both monorepo dist/standalone and the
  published flattened standalone layouts):

❯ explain - a story can import world-model                                                                   

⏺ You're right — I over-constrained it. A standalone story can import @sharpee/world-model directly;
  "umbrella-only" was my assumption, not a rule. That dissolves the fork entirely:

  - The umbrella (@sharpee/sharpee) gives the story-shell types (Story, StoryConfig, Parser, …).
  - The traits and EntityType come from @sharpee/world-model — which is already in the author's node_modules
  (the umbrella depends on it), and the template just declares it directly so it's not a phantom dep.

  So the idiomatic template is straightforward — no umbrella change, no buildManifest change, no sharpee
  republish. A + B-template stay devkit-only → one 1.0.4. Let me confirm world-model is published and exports

  silent ^1.0.0 fallback.

  One follow-up I noticed (separate)

  The manifest still leaks a player → object entry. That's not the template — assembleGame (in
  @sharpee/bootstrap) pre-creates a placeholder 'player', and the story's createPlayer makes the actual
  player, so buildManifest excludes only world.getPlayer() and the orphan placeholder surfaces. Fixing it is

  actors). Want me to take that next, or hold for after you republish 1.0.4 and re-verify?

⏺ The mechanism is clear: the engine's start() (game-engine.ts:342) calls story.createPlayer(world) and
  setPlayer() to the real player — but assembleGame already pre-created a placeholder 'player' (to satisfy
  the GameEngine constructor), which is then orphaned. Since createPlayer is mandatory on every Story, the
  placeholder is always redundant. The fix belongs in assembleGame — but I must confirm removing it is safe
  for play (the engine constructor took that player). Let me check whether the engine uses a stored
  this.player or world.getPlayer():

⏺ grep "this.player" returns nothing — the engine doesn't store the constructor player; it uses
  world.getPlayer() everywhere. So removing the orphaned placeholder is safe. And since introspect already
  saw the built world right after assembleGame, the engine's start() runs createPlayer/initializeWorld
  synchronously before returning. Let me read the exact spot to place the cleanup:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ The placeholder is player (line 93); the engine's start() replaces it. Adding the cleanup right after
  start():

  Behavior Statement — assembleGame (placeholder cleanup):
  - DOES: removes the bootstrap-created placeholder player entity after engine.start() when the story's
  createPlayer has replaced it as world.getPlayer().
  - WHEN: assembling any game (createPlayer is mandatory on every Story).
  - BECAUSE: the orphaned placeholder otherwise leaks into world.getAllEntities() (introspection shows a
  stray player).
  - REJECTS WHEN: n/a — only removes when the active player differs from the placeholder (defensive: never
  removes the live player).

⏺ Pushed (e32be813). Player leak fixed.

  What it was

  assembleGame created a placeholder 'player' (only to satisfy the GameEngine constructor), then
  engine.start() created the real player via story.createPlayer() and re-pointed world.setPlayer() —
  orphaning the placeholder, which leaked into world.getAllEntities() as a stray player → object. Now
  assembleGame removes it after start(). Safe because the engine reads world.getPlayer(), never the
  constructor player.

  Verified real-path: a scaffolded story now introspects to exactly Starting Room → room — no orphan.
  Bootstrap suite green (26 tests).

  To ship: republish bootstrap → 1.0.2
  
  tsf build --npm && tsf publish --changed
  devkit@1.0.4 depends on bootstrap@^1.0.1, so a fresh author install picks up 1.0.2 transitively — no devkit
  republish needed.

  ADR-185 status

  The introspection + author-scaffold path is now correct and complete (modulo the bootstrap republish):
  - ide-protocol@1.0.0, bootstrap@1.0.2 (pending publish), devkit@1.0.4 — all verified
  - sharpee init → build → introspect produces a clean, correctly-categorized manifest

  Remaining ADR-185 work:
  1. Internal npx purge (devkit build internals — build.ts/build-browser.ts/test-npm.ts).
  2. tools/ide realignment to the installed sharpee bin.

  Want me to take the internal npx purge or the IDE realignment next?

⏺ Mapping the current flow makes the scope clear: the IDE is deeply monorepo-shaped — WorkspaceRoot finds the
  monorepo by pnpm-workspace.yaml+packages/core, BuildController runs ./sharpee build <story> at that root,
  BuildSettings carries a story name, and buildSucceeded resolves stories/<name>. Realigning to author mode
  inverts all of that and is coupled across the project-open model, both runners, build settings, and the
  Play panel — with a few genuine design decisions.

  That's a real multi-file refactor with decisions I shouldn't guess on, so let me write a tight plan first
  (per the project's planning convention), then execute on your go. Let me check one thing the plan needs —
  where the standalone build emits its browser output (for the Play panel):

⏺ Bash(cd /Users/david/repos/sharpee
      echo "=== standalone build-browser: output location ===" && grep -n
      "outDir\|outfile\|dist/web\|web/\|browser\|path.join\|mkdir" p…)
  ⎿  === standalone build-browser: output location ===
     2: * CLI: sharpee build-browser
     4: * Bundles a Sharpee story for web browser deployment.
     … +27 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Here's the "smaller issue" explained concretely.

  The IDE will invoke the project's installed sharpee bin (<projectRoot>/node_modules/.bin/sharpee) for build
  and introspect. That bin — and the @sharpee/* platform packages the story compiles against — only exist
  after npm install. And I just confirmed standalone sharpee build only produces the browser client
  (dist/web/, which the Play panel loads) when the project has src/browser-entry.ts, which is added by
  sharpee init-browser (a fresh sharpee init project doesn't have it).

  So an author can be in two "not-ready" states the IDE will hit:

  1. No npm install yet → node_modules/.bin/sharpee is absent → BuildRunner/IntrospectionRunner fail with
  "executable not found" / missing-module errors.
  2. No browser client → sharpee build silently skips the browser step ("Skipped (no src/browser-entry.ts)"),
  so dist/web/ never appears and Play has nothing to load.

  The decision is how the IDE handles these — fail raw, or detect and guide:

  - Option A — minimal: just run the command. If the bin is missing, the raw error lands in the build panel;
  Play shows its existing empty-state placeholder when dist/web/ is absent. The author has to know to run npm
  install / sharpee init-browser themselves.
  - Option B — guided (recommended): the IDE detects the missing prereqs and surfaces actionable affordances:
    - bin missing → a "Run npm install" banner/button (optionally runs it for them),
    - no src/browser-entry.ts → Play shows "Add a browser client" with a button that runs sharpee 
  init-browser.

  Much better author UX (no cryptic errors on a fresh project), but more work — detection logic + the
  affordance UI + running npm install/init-browser from the IDE.

  My recommendation: B, since hand-holding a freshly-scaffolded project into a runnable state is core to what
  an authoring IDE should do — but it's a meaningful chunk on top of the realignment. A reasonable middle
  path: do the realignment + Option A first (get author-mode working), then layer B as a follow-up increment.

  Which do you want — A now (B later), or B as part of this realignment?

✻ Crunched for 1m 10s

        1  # Sharpee Build System (devkit)
        2  
        3 -This guide explains how to build Sharpee platform packages, stories, and client            
        4 -bundles with **`@sharpee/devkit`** (ADR-180). devkit orchestrates the build; `tsf`         
        5 -compiles. In-repo, invoke it with `./sharpee`; once                                        
        6 -`@sharpee/sharpee` is installed, the `devkit` bin is on PATH (`devkit build …`).           
        3 +Sharpee stories are built with **`@sharpee/devkit`** (ADR-180) — the `sharpee` command. dev
          +kit                                                                                        
        4 +orchestrates the build; `tsf` compiles. There are two ways to use it:                      
        5  
        8 -## Quick Start                                                                             
        6 +- **Authors** write a standalone story project. The `sharpee` command comes from `@sharpee/
          +devkit`,                                                                                   
        7 +  and the platform (`@sharpee/sharpee`) is a normal npm dependency — your story compiles ag
          +ainst it                                                                                   
        8 +  and the platform is **never rebuilt**.                                                   
        9 +- **Contributors** work inside the Sharpee monorepo and build the platform packages themsel
          +ves with                                                                                   
       10 +  the repo wrapper `./sharpee`.                                                            
       11  
       12 +---                                                                                        
       13 +                                                                                           
       14 +## Authoring a story (standalone project)                                                  
       15 +                                                                                           
       16  ```bash
       11 -# Build platform + story, then bundle (most common)                                        
       12 -./sharpee build dungeo                                                                     
       17 +# One-time: get the `sharpee` command                                                      
       18 +npm install -g @sharpee/devkit                                                             
       19  
       14 -# + self-contained browser client (dist/web/dungeo/)                                       
       15 -./sharpee build dungeo --browser                                                           
       20 +# Scaffold a new story project                                                             
       21 +sharpee init my-game                                                                       
       22 +cd my-game                                                                                 
       23  
       17 -# + zifmia multi-user server (tools/zifmia/dist/)                                          
       18 -./sharpee build --zifmia                                                                   
       24 +# Install the platform (pulls @sharpee/sharpee + @sharpee/devkit from npm)                 
       25 +npm install                                                                                
       26  
       20 -# Show all options                                                                         
       21 -./sharpee                                                                                  
       27 +# Compile the story (+ the .sharpee bundle, + the browser client if present)               
       28 +sharpee build                                                                              
       29 +                                                                                           
       30 +# Emit the project manifest (rooms / objects / NPCs / regions) as JSON                     
       31 +sharpee introspect                                                                         
       32  ```
       33  
       24 -## Build Options (`devkit build`)                                                          
       34 +`sharpee init` scaffolds `src/index.ts` (a starter story), `package.json` (pinned to the pl
          +atform                                                                                     
       35 +version `devkit` shipped with), and `tsconfig.json`. The platform is prebuilt in `node_modu
          +les` —                                                                                     
       36 +there is no platform build step.                                                           
       37  
       38 +### Adding a web client                                                                    
       39 +                                                                                           
       40 +```bash                                                                                    
       41 +sharpee init-browser          # adds src/browser-entry.ts                                  
       42 +sharpee build                 # now also emits the browser client → dist/web/              
       43 +# serve dist/web/ with any static file server, e.g.:                                       
       44 +python3 -m http.server -d dist/web                                                         
       45 +```                                                                                        
       46 +                                                                                           
       47 +### Author commands                                                                        
       48 +                                                                                           
       49 +| Command | What it does |                                                                 
       50 +|---|---|                                                                                  
       51 +| `sharpee init [dir]` | Scaffold a new story project |                                    
       52 +| `sharpee init-browser` | Add a web client (`src/browser-entry.ts`) to the project |      
       53 +| `sharpee build` | Compile `src/` + emit `.sharpee` (and the browser client if `src/browse
          +r-entry.ts` exists) |                                                                      
       54 +| `sharpee build-browser` | (Re)build only the browser client → `dist/web/` |              
       55 +| `sharpee introspect` | Emit the project manifest (ADR-184/185) as JSON on stdout |       
       56 +| `sharpee ifid` | IFID utilities (generate, validate) |                                   
       57 +                                                                                           
       58 +### Author outputs                                                                         
       59 +                                                                                           
       60 +| Target | Output | Contents |                                                             
       61 +|---|---|---|                                                                              
       62 +| Story | `dist/` | Compiled story (`dist/index.js`) |                                     
       63 +| Story bundle | `dist/<id>.sharpee` | Zipped story for distribution |                     
       64 +| Browser client | `dist/web/` | Self-contained web client (open `index.html`) |           
       65 +                                                                                           
       66 +---                                                                                        
       67 +                                                                                           
       68 +## Contributing to the platform (monorepo)                                                 
       69 +                                                                                           
       70 +Inside the Sharpee monorepo, `./sharpee` builds the platform packages **and** an in-repo re
          +ference                                                                                    
       71 +story (e.g. `dungeo`) **and** the CLI bundle. This path is for working *on* Sharpee, not fo
          +r                                                                                          
       72 +authoring stories.                                                                         
       73 +                                                                                           
       74 +```bash                                                                                    
       75 +./sharpee build dungeo                 # platform + story + bundle                         
       76 +./sharpee build dungeo --browser       # + browser client (dist/web/dungeo/)               
       77 +./sharpee build dungeo --skip stdlib   # resume the platform build from a package          
       78 +./sharpee bundle                       # reassemble dist/cli/sharpee.js only               
       79 +./sharpee clean                        # remove dist/ dist-esm/ tsbuildinfo                
       80 +./sharpee verify                       # tsf build --npm + publish dry-run                 
       81 +./sharpee                              # show all options                                  
       82 +```                                                                                        
       83 +                                                                                           
       84 +### Monorepo build options (`./sharpee build`)                                             
       85 +                                                                                           
       86  | Flag | Description |
       87  |------|-------------|
       28 -| `[story]` | Story name to build (resolved `stories/<name>` then `tutorials/<name>`) |    
       29 -| `--browser` | Also build the self-contained browser client (`dist/web/<story>/`); require
          -s a story |                                                                                
       30 -| `--zifmia` | Also build the zifmia multi-user server (`tools/zifmia/dist/`) |            
       88 +| `[story]` | In-repo story name (resolved `stories/<name>` then `tutorials/<name>`) |     
       89 +| `--browser` | Also build the self-contained browser client (`dist/web/<story>/`) |       
       90  | `--skip <package>` | Resume the platform build from this package short-name |
       91  | `--version <v>` | Version to stamp (default: `packages/sharpee/package.json`) |
       92  | `--build-date <iso>` | Frozen build date (determinism / parity) |
       93  | `--no-version` | Skip version stamping |
       94  | `--no-genai` | Skip genai-api generation |
       95  | `--no-bundle` | Build packages/story but skip the CLI bundle |
       37 -| `--esm` | Also run the ESM build pass (implied by `--browser`/`--zifmia`) |              
       96 +| `--esm` | Also run the ESM build pass (implied by `--browser`) |

       39 -Other commands: `devkit bundle` (assemble `dist/cli/sharpee.js` only),

       41 -(`tsf build --npm` + publish dry-run), `devkit test:npm <location>` (npm consumer test).   
       98 +### Build order (monorepo)

       43 -## Build Order                                                                             
       44 -                                                                                           
      100  1. **Stamp versions** — writes `version.ts` + `package.json` versions (unless `--no-version

       46 -2. **Build platform** — compiles all platform packages in dependency order (via `pnpm --fil
          -ter`)                                                                                      
      101 +2. **Build platform** — compiles all platform packages in dependency order (`pnpm --filter`
          +)

      104  5. **Bundle** — assembles `dist/cli/sharpee.js`
       50 -6. **Client targets** — `--browser` and/or `--zifmia` (if requested)                       
      105 +6. **Browser client** — `--browser` (if requested)

      106 -## Performance Tips                                                                        
      107 -

      133  ### Stale / silent no-op build
      134  
      116 -`devkit build` asserts each package emits `dist/index.js` (the `.tsbuildinfo` silent-no-op 
      117 -class is precluded). If artifacts look stale, run `devkit clean` then rebuild.             
      135 +`sharpee build` asserts each package emits `dist/index.js` (the `.tsbuildinfo` silent-no-op
          + class is                                                                                  
      136 +precluded). If artifacts look stale, run `sharpee clean` (monorepo) or delete `dist/` (auth
          +or                                                                                         
      137 +project) and rebuild.                                                                      
      138  
      139  ### Circular dependency
      140  
      141  ```bash
      122 -npx madge --circular stories/dungeo/dist/index.js                                          
      142 +npx madge --circular dist/index.js                                                         
      143  ```
      144  
      145  Fix by changing barrel imports to direct file imports.
      146  
      127 -### TypeScript errors                                                                      
      147 +### TypeScript errors (monorepo)                                                           
      148  
      149  Build the failing package's dependencies first, or use `--skip <pkg>` to build from it.

⏺ Update(tools/ide/SharpeeIDE/Build/BuildRunner.swift)
  ⎿  Added 1 line, removed 1 line
      2  // Owns a single child `./sharpee build` process: spawns it, streams stdout/stderr to

⏺ Pushed (b5a1e316). New Story is done.

  File → New Story… (⌘N)

  1. Prompts for a title.
  2. NSSavePanel to pick a location / create the folder (default name = kebab of the title,
  canCreateDirectories).
  3. StoryScaffold copies devkit's story template into the folder, substituting
  {{STORY_TITLE}}/{{STORY_ID}}/{{AUTHOR}}/{{DESCRIPTION}} and the ^1.0.0 version ranges.
  4. Opens the new project (which kicks off introspect-on-open once it's built).

  Self-contained — the template is bundled into the app (devkit's templates/story added as an app resource
  via project.yml, verified present in SharpeeIDE.app/Contents/Resources/), so authors create a project
  entirely in the IDE with no global CLI. 4 StoryScaffold unit tests + 177 IDE tests pass.

  The natural follow-on

  A freshly-created project has no node_modules yet, so build/introspect/play won't work until npm install
  runs. That's exactly increment 6 (auto-housekeeping) — after New Story creates/opens a project (or on
  opening any project with package.json but no node_modules/.bin/sharpee), the IDE silently runs npm install.
  That would make New Story → ready-to-build a one-click flow.

  Remaining ADR-185 IDE work

  1. Auto-housekeeping (6) — silent npm install (and sharpee init-browser when Play needs a browser entry).
  Most valuable — completes the New Story → buildable loop.
  2. Cleanup (3/5) — remove the now-dead monorepo code (WorkspaceRoot, StoryDetector, PackageDetector,
  BrowserEntry, BuildSettings.story, settings story picker, dead chains) + their tests. Involves deleting 
  files, so I'll need your go-ahead.

  The author loop is now one-click

  New Story → create + open → auto npm install (streamed in the Build panel) → Build → introspect + Play. No
  manual setup steps — opening any author project with missing deps installs them silently; a built project
  introspects on open.

  (install only re-introspects if the project's already built; build introspects + reloads Play).

✻ Brewed for 49s
  
❯ 1. we need a browser template, 2. what does that include: a) channel-io rendering, b) nextjs or ???        

⏺ Good questions — let me think through both rather than just pick.
  
  a) Channel-IO rendering — yes, that's the architecture

  The whole story runs client-side in the browser: each turn the in-browser engine emits ADR-163 channel
  packets, and @sharpee/platform-browser's BrowserClient renders that stream to the DOM (main text, status
  line, media, etc.). That's exactly what dungeo/thealderman's browser-entry.ts do. So the template's job is
  just: assemble the engine + BrowserClient over the channel stream, plus the HTML surface it binds to. The
  template = three files — browser-entry.ts.template (the wiring), index.html (the DOM surface + element ids
  BrowserClient expects), styles.css.

  b) Next.js — I'd push back on this one

  ADR-170 (+ the existence of platform-browser) deliberately settled the web UI as framework-free — no
  React/Next/Lit/Web-Components. An IF story page doesn't need what Next.js brings: no SSR (the engine +
  world live in the browser, nothing to render server-side), no routing (it's one page), no React. The
  current sharpee build-browser already produces a self-contained static bundle (esbuild → dist/web/) that
  you can drop on any static host. Next.js would reintroduce the exact framework dependency that ADR was
  about avoiding, plus a build server, for no gain.

  So my recommendation: vanilla TS + platform-browser (channel-IO) + esbuild → static dist/web/. Same stack
  the reference stories and the standalone build already use.

  The real open question (for you)

  Given that's the stack, how much should the starter template include?
  - Bare: mount BrowserClient with a default theme and the standard surface (text pane + status line +
  command input) — a working but plain page the author restyles.
  - A bit more: the above plus commented hooks for the things authors commonly touch (themes, a status-line
  layout, a media region).

  I'd lean bare-but-correct (a starter, not a showcase). Does framework-free channel-IO match your intent —
  and bare vs. a-bit-more? Once we agree, I'll base it on thealderman's working setup so it's genuinely
  current, not the stale published copy.

     dist/web/.DS_Store

⏺ A built browser client (e.g. dungeo/, thealderman/) is six files:

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐
  │      File       │                        Source                        │      Author-editable?       │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ game.js (+.map) │ esbuild bundle of browser-entry.ts + the in-browser  │ no — build output           │
  │                 │ engine + platform-browser (~6.6M)                    │                             │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ index.html      │ the DOM surface BrowserClient binds to               │ yes                         │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ styles.css      │ story/theme styling                                  │ yes                         │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ base.css        │ platform-browser's base UI styles                    │ no — copied from the        │
  │                 │                                                      │ platform at build           │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ decorations.css │ the ADR-174 decoration classes (.sharpee-*)          │ no — copied from the        │
  │                 │                                                      │ platform                    │
  └─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘

  So this tells us exactly what the template needs to be vs. what the build supplies:

  - Template (devkit templates/browser/) → browser-entry.ts.template + index.html + styles.css (the three
  author-facing files).
  - Build supplies → game.js (esbuild) and base.css + decorations.css (from platform-browser).

  And usefully: the index.html and styles.css already sitting in dist/web/thealderman/ are current, working 
  examples — the template versions are basically the genericized form of those (the same DOM/element-ids
  BrowserClient expects, minus story-specific bits).

  That confirms the scope is small and concrete. Want me to look at dist/web/thealderman/index.html next so
  we can see the actual surface the template should mirror?

⏺ This is clarifying. Two findings:

  1. The Alderman's browser-entry.ts IS the current IDE-generated scaffold — line 2 literally says "generated
  by Sharpee IDE." It's the minimal channel-io wiring: BrowserClient + ThemeManager, reads story.config for
  title/author, binds the DOM in initialize(), connectEngine + start. Genericizing it is tiny — only
  'thealderman-' (storagePrefix) and the theme-storage key are story-specific → {{STORY_ID}}. Everything else
  is already generic.

  2. The HTML/CSS template is also missing — and the browser build relies on it. build-browser.ts looks for
  the project's own browser/index.html/styles.css, else falls back to templates/browser/index.html/styles.css
  — which don't exist, so it warns "HTML template not found." The dist/web/thealderman/* files are stale
  build output from when the template existed.

  So the full browser template is three files, all currently missing: browser-entry.ts.template, index.html,
  styles.css.

  And there's a DOM contract to honor: initialize() binds ~14 element ids — text-content, main-window,
  command-input, location-name, score-turns, the save/restore/startup <dialog>s, menu-bar, etc. The
  index.html must provide exactly those, or the client silently fails to wire up.

  Before I write anything: the cleanest source for the index.html/styles.css is The Alderman's working setup,
  but its source html/css don't exist (only the built dist/web/ copies, generated from the old template). So
  I'd want to confirm those dist/web/thealderman/index.html + styles.css are actually current (they match
  the live browser-entry.ts ids) before templatizing them.

  Two things I'd like your read on:
  - Is taking dist/web/thealderman/{index.html,styles.css} as the basis OK, or is there a canonical source
  for them I should use instead?
  - Want me to show you the genericized browser-entry.ts.template (it's basically The Alderman's file with 2
  swaps) before I commit to the other two?
